content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional limits — MAXIMUM AMOUNT YOU CAN SUE FOR

Delaware “small claims” cases are heard in the Justice of the Peace Court (JP Court). JP Court has **civil jurisdiction up to $25,000** for most claims[^1]. Unlike some states, this limit applies equally regardless of plaintiff type: individuals, businesses, and other entities all have a $25,000 cap[^1]. (One exception: in commercial landlord-tenant eviction cases, JP Court can handle rent claims above $25,000 as part of the eviction[^2] – see Section 2 on landlord–tenant). Delaware does **not** impose a yearly limit on the number of small claims filings or a lower cap for frequent filers (for example, corporations are not subject to a separate lower cap as they are in some states). 

You **cannot split one claim into multiple suits** to evade the $25,000 limit. The court considers splitting a single cause of action improper – a plaintiff must sue for the entire claim in one case[^3]. If your total damages exceed $25,000, you must either waive the excess or file in a higher court. (Delaware increased the JP Court limit from $15,000 to $25,000 in 2020 to reduce the need for splitting claims[^4].) 

**Important:** JP Court can only award monetary damages (or return of personal property) up to $25k. It **cannot** grant injunctions or orders requiring someone to act (no specific performance or restraining orders here). See Section 2 “What you CANNOT sue for” for other limitations on JP Court jurisdiction.

[^1]: 10 Del. Code § 9301(1) – JP Court civil jurisdiction for common-law contract and tort up to $25,000 (delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c093/index.html)  
[^2]: 10 Del. Code § 9301(1) – $25k cap does *not* apply to rent/monetary claims in a commercial lease eviction (summary possession) case (delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c093/index.html)  
[^3]: Justice of the Peace Court Civil Rule 13(j) – claim-splitting not permitted (courts.delaware.gov) (⚠️ Verify if codified; generally, splitting a cause of action is not allowed in Delaware courts)  
[^4]: Delaware 150th General Assembly, House Bill 232 (2020) – raised JP Court jurisdictional limit to $25,000 and clarified handling of commercial rent claims (legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731)

## 2. What types of cases qualify — EVERYTHING YOU CAN SUE FOR

**Overview:** Delaware JP Court can hear a broad range of civil disputes as long as the claim is for money (or return of personal property) within the $25,000 limit. This includes contract disputes, property damage, landlord/tenant money claims, and various statutory damages. **However, JP Court *cannot* handle cases involving physical personal injuries or certain equitable claims.** The lists below detail what **is allowed** in Delaware small claims, with examples and any special rules. (If you don’t see your issue listed, it likely means JP Court can’t handle it – see **“What you CANNOT sue for”** at the end of this section.)

### Contract & money-owed disputes

**Breach of written contract:** Yes – you can sue for damages when a written agreement is broken, up to $25k[^1]. Examples: a contractor signed a contract but never finished the job; a customer refuses to pay for delivered goods. Delaware requires you to prove the contract and the breach. Tip: bring the written contract and any relevant emails or receipts to court. *(No special pre-filing requirements for general contract claims.)*

**Breach of oral/handshake agreement:** Yes – oral contracts are enforceable in JP Court (though harder to prove). Example: you agreed to sell a used bike for $500 and the buyer never paid; a friend promised to split rent and didn’t. You’ll need to show there was a mutual agreement (through witnesses, texts, etc.). Delaware treats oral contracts as “express or implied” contracts within JP Court jurisdiction[^1]. 

**Unpaid personal loans (informal IOUs):** Yes – a loan between friends or family is essentially a contract. Example: you lent $2,000 to a cousin who promised to repay in six months but hasn’t. A dated IOU note or bank statement of the transfer can serve as evidence. There’s no special law for private loans, so it’s handled as a debt action on a contract. *(If the loan was evidenced by a promissory note, see below – there’s a longer statute of limitations for written notes.)*

**Unpaid promissory notes:** Yes – a promissory note is a written promise to pay, and you can sue if it’s not honored. Example: someone signed a note to repay a $10,000 loan by a certain date and defaulted. Delaware law treats notes and other written acknowledgments of debt somewhat specially: they have a 6-year statute of limitations instead of 3 (see Statute of Limitations section)[^5]. In court, present the original note and any proof of non-payment. Delaware also allows **attorney’s fees or interest** if the note explicitly provides for them (JP Court will award those as long as within the $25k cap)[^6].

**Unpaid invoices or accounts receivable:** Yes – if you sold goods or provided services and haven’t been paid, you can sue for the balance due. Example: you own a small business that delivered $8,000 in products; the buyer never paid the invoice. This is a “debt action” in JP Court terminology[^7]. It’s wise to attach the unpaid invoices and any account statements to your complaint. Delaware law on “open account” debts (mutual running accounts) treats them like contract debts (and they carry a 3-year time limit from the last charge or payment)[^8].

**Unpaid rent (residential or commercial):** Yes – but procedure differs depending on whether you also want to evict. If a **tenant has moved out** (or you’re not seeking eviction), you can file a straight debt claim in JP Court for unpaid rent or damages (within $25k). Example: a tenant left owing two months’ rent ($2,500) and you want that money. Delaware law requires that **before suing for just rent, a landlord must demand the rent and give notice of intent to sue**[^9]. Make sure you’ve sent a written demand or otherwise warned the tenant as required, or the case could be dismissed. If you *also* need to evict a current tenant for nonpayment, that is a **summary possession action** – a combined eviction case that JP Court handles under a faster timeline (see Landlord–Tenant section below). In a combined eviction case for a **commercial lease**, JP Court can award the full rent due even if over $25k[^2]. For residential leases, any rent over $25k would likely be waived or require a separate higher-court action (though owing that much residential rent is rare).

**Bounced/dishonored checks:** Yes – you can sue the check writer for the amount of the check and additional damages. Delaware has a **“bad check” statute** that allows extra recovery if proper steps are followed: you can recover the check amount plus a **$50 penalty for the first bad check**, and for any second (or later) bad check from the same person within a year, **triple the amount** of the check (up to $250 extra)[^10]. *Example:* a customer paid with a $200 check that bounced; you gave proper notice and they didn’t pay – you could recover $200 + $50 = $250. If they bounced another $200 check to you a month later, you could recover $200 + $600 (triple) = $800, but capped at $250 extra, so $450 total for that one. **Pre-suit requirement:** To get these penalties, Delaware law says you **must send a written demand** for payment at least 30 days before filing suit[^10]. The demand should go to the check writer and give them 30 days to pay the amount plus a $40 service fee[^10]. If they pay in that window (amount + $40 fee), you **cannot** then claim the $50 or triple damages – the idea is to give them a chance to cure. If they fail to respond, you can proceed in JP Court for the base amount, court costs, and the statutory damages mentioned. Always bring copies of the bad check, the bank’s dishonor notice, and your demand letter (with proof it was sent).

**Open-book accounts/running tabs:** Yes – an “open account” (an ongoing account where the balance fluctuates with purchases/payments, like a credit account or running tab) is actionable. Example: a customer had a charge account with your shop, accruing $1,200 in purchases over time, and then stopped paying. This is treated as a debt in JP Court. Delaware’s statute of limitations on open accounts is 3 years from the date of the last charge or last payment, whichever is later[^8]. It’s helpful to produce a **statement of account** showing all charges, credits, and the final balance due. JP Court may require a **Bill of Particulars** (an itemized account) if the defendant asks for more detail[^11].

**Co-signer/guarantor disputes:** Yes – if you co-signed a loan and ended up paying it, you can sue the primary borrower for reimbursement; or a lender can sue a guarantor if the main borrower defaulted. Example: you co-signed a friend’s car loan, they defaulted, and you had to pay the bank $5,000 – you can sue your friend for that $5,000. Delaware would consider this an implied contract of reimbursement. Be ready to show proof of the debt and that you paid it (canceled checks, payoff letter). If you’re a lender suing both borrower and guarantor, you can include them in one JP Court case (assuming the total is within $25k). There’s no special bar to suing guarantors in small claims here (some states require such claims in higher courts, but Delaware JP Court is fine).

### Consumer disputes

**Refunds for defective products:** Yes – if you bought a product that turned out defective or not as promised, you can sue for a refund or damages (like cost of repair). Example: you paid $800 for a used appliance that never worked, and the seller refuses a refund. Your claim could be for the purchase price and perhaps any incidental costs. This can be framed as breach of contract or warranty. Delaware’s version of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) implies a **warranty of merchantability** on sold goods – meaning goods should be fit for ordinary use – unless sold “as is.” If a product fails to meet basic standards, you can claim breach of warranty under UCC Article 2. *No special pre-suit requirement,* but it’s wise to notify the seller of the defect and give them a chance to cure (even if not legally required, it shows you acted reasonably). **Statutory note:** the statute of limitations for breach of a sales contract (including implied warranties) is **4 years** from the sale in Delaware[^5].

**Refunds for services not performed or done poorly:** Yes – if you paid for a service and it was never performed, or was done in a shoddy manner, you can sue to get your money back or the cost to fix the poor work. Examples: you paid a painter $1,000 up front and they never showed up; or you hired a caterer for an event and they botched it, causing losses. This is a breach of contract claim. Document what was expected (contract or quote) and what went wrong (photos of poor workmanship, etc.). Delaware does not require any special licensing or agency review before suing for most services (except certain regulated fields like construction might have optional arbitration – but small claims is generally fine). Just ensure you sue within 3 years of the breach[^8].

**Auto repair overcharges or shoddy work:** Yes – you can sue a mechanic or auto shop if they charged for work not done, did unauthorized repairs, or caused damage to your vehicle. Example: you were billed $2,000 for repairs that were not actually made, or a mechanic’s negligence caused new engine damage. These cases often involve claims of breach of contract and/or negligence (for property damage to the car). In Delaware JP Court, **negligence claims not involving physical injury** (e.g. property damage) are allowed up to $25k[^12]. There’s no prerequisite to complain to any agency first – you can go straight to court (though you might also report fraud to Delaware’s consumer protection unit if it’s egregious). Bring the repair contract or estimate, the final bill, and possibly a report from another mechanic if you have one to prove the work was not done or done improperly. 

**Home repair/contractor disputes:** Yes – incomplete or defective home improvement work is a common small claim. Example: you paid a contractor $10,000 to remodel a bathroom, but they left it half-finished or the workmanship was subpar, requiring $5,000 in corrections. This can be breach of contract. In Delaware, contractors are not required to be licensed for general home improvements (except certain trades), so lack of a license does not bar the claim (unlike in some states). You do not have to go through any specific board before suing (Delaware doesn’t have a mandatory pre-suit contractor arbitration for residential work as some states do). Just ensure you file within 3 years of the breach[^8]. If the contractor claims you owe them money, they might counter-sue (see Counterclaims). Having a detailed contract and photos of the problems will be crucial evidence.

**Appliance/electronics warranty failures:** Yes – if a product is under warranty (manufacturer or extended warranty) and the company doesn’t honor it, you can sue for the cost of repair or replacement. Example: your new TV came with a 1-year warranty, it died after 6 months, and the manufacturer refuses to fix or replace it – you could sue for the TV’s cost. This is essentially a breach of warranty claim (under contract law or the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, which can be heard in state court). Delaware follows UCC warranty rules (4-year limit from sale)[^5]. There’s no special state program you must use first – although many manufacturers have dispute resolution processes, you are not required to use them before small claims (except note: some warranties include arbitration clauses – but often those allow small claims as an exception). In small claims, focus on proving the defect occurred within warranty and that the company failed to honor it.

**Cancelled or no-show service providers (e.g. photographers, caterers):** Yes – if a service provider flakes on your event or appointment and you lost money, you can sue to recover the deposit or extra costs. Example: a wedding photographer you paid $2,000 in advance simply didn’t show up, and you had to scramble for a last-minute replacement. That’s a clear breach of contract. Delaware JP Court will allow recovery of what you paid and any additional reasonable expenses caused by the breach (like the difference in cost for a last-minute photographer), as long as within $25k. There’s no special notice needed, though giving them a demand letter is wise. If the provider gave you a contract with a cancellation policy, the court will consider that – some contracts try to limit refunds. But a total failure to perform generally means you should get your money back regardless of a “no refunds” clause (courts often find such clauses unconscionable if the service was not provided at all). 

**Dry cleaning / property loss in custody:** Yes – if a business lost or damaged your property that you entrusted to them (dry cleaner, valet parking, storage unit, etc.), you can sue for the value of the item or the cost of repair. Example: the dry cleaner ruined your $300 dress; the valet company delivered your car with a big scratch. These can be pursued as negligence or bailment liability claims. JP Court handles property damage/negligence claims as long as no one was physically injured[^12]. No pre-suit notice is mandated by law, but you should of course complain to the business first (some have internal claim forms, especially for things like valet damage – using those doesn’t waive your court rights if they don’t pay). Note that some dry cleaning receipts or storage contracts purport to limit liability (e.g. “not responsible for items over $100”) – Delaware courts may enforce reasonable liability caps if you agreed to them, but they will scrutinize fairness. The maximum you can get is the item’s fair market value (e.g. replacement cost of a similar used item, not the original purchase price if it’s depreciated).

**Gym/subscription/membership cancellation refunds:** Yes – Delaware has laws to protect consumers in health club contracts. If you properly canceled a gym or other membership and the company won’t refund unused dues or keeps charging you, you can sue for the money. Delaware’s **Health Spa Act** (6 Del. Code § 4205) gives you **3 business days** to cancel a gym/health club contract for a full refund[^12]. It also lets you cancel if the facility closes or moves more than 15 miles away, among other scenarios[^12]. Example: you prepaid $600 for a year at a gym, but you moved out of state and the contract allows cancellation for relocation – if the gym refuses to refund the unused portion, you can sue. Be sure to follow the contract’s cancellation procedures (usually written notice by certified mail) and keep proof. If the business violates the statute (e.g. not honoring a valid cancellation), that could also be an **unfair practice** under Delaware’s Consumer Fraud Act (see “False advertising” below). No need to involve the Consumer Protection Unit before suing (though you can file a complaint with them separately if you want). In JP Court, cite the contract and the law entitling you to cancel, and claim the amount of unused services.

**Auto dealer/used-car disputes (lemon issues, odometer fraud, etc.):** Many car sale disputes can be handled in JP Court if damages are under $25k, but be aware of specialized laws: Delaware has a **“Lemon Law”** (6 Del. Code § 5001-5009) for new cars, but that primarily gives you rights against manufacturers (replacement or refund for serious defects within 1 year/12,000 miles). Lemon Law cases often exceed $25k (the price of a new car) and may involve the manufacturer – those typically end up in higher court or arbitration. However, **used car** sales and dealer fraud cases often *do* fall in small claims. Example: a used-car dealer misrepresented a vehicle’s condition (said “no accidents” but it was in a major wreck) or rolled back the odometer – you can sue for the diminished value or repair costs. Delaware’s Consumer Fraud Act (6 Del. Code § 2513) makes it unlawful for sellers to misrepresent or conceal material facts in a sale[^13]. A car with a hidden salvage title or odometer tampering would qualify. In JP Court, you could claim the difference in value or cost to fix issues. Delaware doesn’t require you to go through the DMV or any other agency first for fraud – you can sue directly, possibly adding a claim under the Consumer Fraud Act. (If you prove a willful deception, you could also ask for costs or even punitive damages, though JP Court is cautious with punitive awards.) Be mindful of the time limit: fraud claims generally fall under a 3-year limit from when the fraud was discovered or should have been discovered[^8]. 

**False advertising / deceptive trade practices:** Yes – Delaware’s Consumer Fraud Act allows consumers to recover for deceptive business practices. If a business’s false promise or advertisement caused you a loss, you can sue in JP Court for your damages. Example: a travel agency advertised a “luxury hotel package” which you bought for $1,200, but on arrival it was a substandard motel – you could sue for the difference in value or a refund. Under 6 Del. Code § 2513, any deception, false pretense, or misrepresentation in connection with the sale of merchandise is unlawful, and Delaware law **allows a private action** by any victim of such unlawful practice[^13]. Unlike some states, Delaware’s law does not provide for automatic treble damages or attorney’s fees for the consumer in a private lawsuit – you can recover your actual losses (and possibly punitive damages if the conduct was egregious and you specifically seek them). There’s *no requirement* to give the business a pre-suit demand under the Consumer Fraud Act (unlike California’s CLRA, for example). However, it’s wise to preserve evidence of the advertisement and your reliance (e.g., brochure, screenshots of website claims). Note: if your claim is small, Delaware’s consumer protection office might not litigate it for you, but they do handle complaints – still, JP Court is often the fastest route for a personal remedy.

### Property damage

**Vehicle collisions (property damage only):** Yes – if another driver hit your car and you’re seeking vehicle repair costs or deductible, JP Court can hear the case (up to $25k). *Crucial:* If **anyone was injured** in the accident, **JP Court cannot handle the injury portion** – bodily injury claims must go to a higher court (Delaware does not allow “personal injuries” in JP Court[^12]). But you **could** choose to sue just for the car damage in JP Court (leaving the injury claim for later or waiving it). Example: a minor fender-bender where you only have $2,000 of bumper damage – that’s fine for small claims. Example 2: a crash where you have $5,000 car damage and also medical bills – *do not* split and sue car damage here and injury separately later without legal advice; Delaware courts might view the accident as one cause of action (risking that a JP Court judgment on property damage could bar a later injury suit by claim preclusion). Generally, if you have any personal injury from a crash, it’s safer to handle **all** claims in the higher court (Court of Common Pleas) despite the small amount of property damage, to avoid that legal pitfall. But purely property damage auto claims are straightforward in JP Court. Bring repair estimates/invoices, photos of the damage, and any police report. There’s a 2-year statute of limitations for damage to personal property in Delaware[^14]. If the other driver was cited or admitted fault, that helps, but JP Court will ultimately decide based on negligence principles. Also, if insurance already paid you apart from a deductible, the insurer might technically own part of the claim (subrogation) – small claims often just have you sue for your deductible and let insurance handle the rest separately.

**Damage to personal property by another person:** Yes – any situation where someone negligently or intentionally destroys or harms your belongings can be brought as a tort claim. Example: a neighbor’s kid accidentally throws a baseball through your $600 window; or a friend borrowed your power tool and broke it. These are property damage claims. As noted, *personal* property damage claims are within JP jurisdiction (they are “common-law actions in tort for damage to personal property” per statute)[^1]. Provide evidence of the item’s value (receipts, appraisals, or at least photos and your testimony on value). The time limit is 2 years for property damage in tort[^14]. If the act was intentional (vandalism, for instance), you can still sue for the value – the fact it was an intentional act doesn’t exclude small claims (except you can’t ask for punitive damages in JP Court in most cases). If criminal charges were involved, you can sue civilly regardless of the criminal outcome (acquittal or no charges doesn’t bar your civil claim).

**Damage by a tenant to a landlord’s property:** Yes – a landlord can sue a former tenant for property damage to the rental unit beyond normal wear and tear. Example: a tenant caused $3,000 of damage (holes in walls, broken appliance) and their security deposit was only $1,000, leaving $2,000 unrecovered. This is typically folded into the same action as an **unpaid rent or security deposit claim** after move-out. Delaware law allows landlords to use the security deposit toward damages, but the landlord must provide the tenant an itemized list of damages within 20 days after lease end[^15]. If the landlord fails to itemize/return timely, they can lose the right to claim damages (see Security Deposits below). Assuming you did everything by the book, you can sue for damages exceeding the deposit. You should have documented the condition (move-in/move-out inspection reports, photos). JP Court regularly hears these “former tenant damage” cases. There’s no additional pre-suit requirement beyond the deposit accounting rules. Statute of limitations would be 3 years from when the tenant left (breach of lease)[^8].

**Damage by a landlord to a tenant’s property:** Yes – if a landlord’s negligence or intentional act caused damage to the tenant’s personal property, the tenant can sue. Example: the landlord’s maintenance person caused a flood that ruined your furniture; or the landlord unlawfully locked you out and your belongings got damaged. These are tort or contract claims (breach of the covenant to provide habitable premises, perhaps). JP Court can handle the money claim for the property loss. Note that if it’s essentially a **“repair and deduct” scenario** (tenant had to fix something), see Landlord–Tenant section for how to handle that. Otherwise, treat it like any property damage case – prove the value of items and that the landlord is liable (through negligence or violating the lease). No special pre-suit notice beyond perhaps informing the landlord of the issue and your loss (which is just practical to attempt resolution). 

**Tree/fence/boundary disputes resulting in damage:** Yes – if a neighbor’s tree fell on your fence/car, or they built a fence on your property causing damage, etc., you can sue for the damage (repair costs, diminution in value, etc.). Delaware follows common law rules: generally, if a healthy tree falls by an act of God, the owner isn’t automatically liable (each neighbor bears their own loss absent negligence). But if the tree was dead or the neighbor’s negligence caused the fall, they’re liable. In JP Court, you’d frame it as negligence or trespass to property. Example: a neighbor’s rotten tree limb fell and smashed your shed ($2,500 damage) after you warned them of the hazard – strong case for negligence. Or the neighbor intentionally tore down your fence – that’s an intentional tort (property damage). These can be brought in small claims (no physical personal injury involved). No special rules specific to trees in Delaware small claims – just be mindful of needing evidence of negligence for natural tree falls. Boundary disputes themselves (deciding the exact property line) are **not** for small claims – that’s an equity matter for the Court of Chancery. But **damages** from encroachment (say a neighbor’s construction went over the line and damaged your yard) can be claimed in JP Court as trespass damage (while the boundary location might have to be assumed or proven via survey). If the core issue is the title to land, small claims won’t resolve that – you’d need Chancery. They will only award money for damage caused.

**Pet damage (one pet harms another or property):** Yes – pets are considered property in law, so harm caused by a pet is property damage. Example: your neighbor’s dog attacked and injured your dog, resulting in $800 vet bills – you can sue the dog’s owner for the vet costs. Or someone’s dog chewed up your expensive lawn furniture – you can sue for the replacement cost. Delaware, like all states, imposes responsibility on pet owners for damage their animals cause. There’s no special “one bite rule” shielding owners in civil suits – even if the dog had no prior bites, the owner can be liable for negligence (e.g. letting the dog run loose). If it’s a **dog bite to a person**, note that’s a personal injury (which JP Court cannot hear if you’re seeking injury damages). But for **injury to another animal or to property**, JP Court is fine. Keep evidence like vet bills, photos of injuries, and any animal control reports. The limit of recovery for a pet’s injury is typically the economic costs (vet treatment, possibly market value if the pet died, though pets are priceless to us, the court treats them as property – emotional distress or “sentimental value” for pet injury is not awarded in small claims). Delaware does not have a specific statute for pet-on-pet damages; it’s handled under general property damage principles.

**Damage during a move (by movers or friends helping):** Yes – if you hire movers and they break something, or a friend helping you move drops your TV, you can sue for the loss. Example: a moving company’s employee gouged your hardwood floor ($1,200 repair). Movers often have you sign a contract limiting liability (e.g. by weight of item, or requiring you to file a claim within 90 days). Those terms can complicate things – some are enforceable, some not, depending on consumer protection laws. But you can certainly bring the claim to JP Court. If it’s a friend (no contract), it’s straightforward negligence – you’d sue your friend for the reasonable value to repair/replace the item. If it’s a licensed moving company, check if they offered valuation coverage; even if they did, you are not limited to that in court if the limitation is deemed unfair or if they were grossly negligent. There’s no requirement to go through the mover’s insurance or dispute process first – you can choose to sue directly. Provide proof of the item’s condition before and after and repair estimates. 

### Landlord–tenant (financial only – possession/eviction is separate)

(*Note:* Eviction actions (getting a tenant out) are **summary possession** cases, which JP Court also handles, but they are not “small claims” per se and follow a different rapid process. Here, we cover **money claims** arising from rental relationships – often handled either within an eviction case or as standalone small claims after tenancy. JP Court can **only award money**; it cannot grant you possession of property through a small claim – that requires the eviction procedure under Title 25.)

**Unreturned security deposits:** Yes – this is one of the most common small claims nationwide. Delaware has tenant-friendly rules on security deposits. A landlord must, within **20 days** after a residential lease ends, either return the security deposit (plus interest if applicable) or send the tenant an itemized list of damages/charges and return any remaining balance[^15]. If the landlord fails to do so, or wrongfully withholds money, the tenant can sue. **Penalty:** If a landlord doesn’t remit the deposit or a required itemization within 20 days, the tenant can recover **double the amount wrongfully withheld**[^15]. Example: your $1,000 deposit was not returned and you got no itemization within 20 days – you can sue for $2,000. Also, if the landlord fails to disclose where the deposit was held, or doesn’t put it in a proper Delaware bank account, that can trigger forfeiture of the deposit to the tenant and double damages after 20 days[^15]. *Pre-suit requirements:* Delaware law says the tenant should **make a claim in writing within 1 year** after the lease for any unused deposit[^15]. So, to be safe, you must write to the landlord (at their last known address) requesting the deposit within one year of moving out. (If you fail to make any claim within 1 year, you lose rights to the deposit.) If the landlord ignoring you triggers double damages, cite 25 Del. Code § 5514(g) in your complaint[^15]. Bring evidence: your lease (to show deposit amount), proof you gave a forwarding address, any correspondence about the deposit, and photos if the landlord is falsely claiming damages. Delaware JP Court regularly awards double damages for clear bad-faith deposit withholding. 

**Refund of prepaid last month’s rent:** Yes – if you paid last month’s rent in advance and didn’t ultimately owe it (for instance, you left early and a replacement tenant took over, or the lease ended early by agreement), you can claim a refund. Often this is rolled into a security deposit accounting. Delaware requires landlords to refund any rent paid for periods after a lease termination. If the landlord unjustly keeps rent for time you didn’t occupy, sue for that amount. Provide proof of when you vacated and if/when a new tenant moved in (a landlord can’t “double dip” by keeping your rent and collecting new rent for the same period). There’s no special penalty like the deposit law, but you could get court costs and maybe interest. Demand it in writing first (that written demand within 1 year would also cover this as part of your “claim” for money owed at move-out)[^15].

**Habitability/repair-and-deduct counterclaims:** Yes – tenants in Delaware have specific rights if landlords fail to maintain the rental. For instance, if the landlord breaches the lease or code by not making repairs that substantially affect your living conditions, you have remedies: you can terminate the lease, or you can *repair and deduct* under certain conditions, or sue for damages. In JP Court, these typically arise in two ways: (a) **Tenant as plaintiff:** you moved out due to serious defects and want compensation (like added costs or some rent refunded), or you paid for a repair and want reimbursement; (b) **Tenant as defendant:** the landlord sued you for unpaid rent, and you counterclaim that the unit was uninhabitable or that you made repairs. Delaware law (Residential Landlord-Tenant Code) sets out a procedure: If the landlord fails to fix a condition that “deprives the tenant of a substantial part of the benefit of the bargain,” and doesn’t remedy within 15 days of written notice, the tenant may terminate the lease and/or seek damages, including a retroactive rent reduction from the date of notice[^16]. If the condition was caused by the landlord’s willful or negligent failure, the tenant can recover **the greater of**: (1) the difference between the agreed rent and the fair rental value of the unit in its defective condition (for the time of the issue), **or** (2) one month’s rent plus the full security deposit[^16]. These are statutory damages under 25 Del. Code § 5306. Example: your heater broke in December, you gave notice, it wasn’t fixed for a month – you stayed but it was nearly uninhabitable: you could seek a rent abatement for that month or up to one month’s rent + deposit if the court finds the landlord was negligent in failing to fix heat[^16]. **Repair-and-deduct**: Delaware allows a tenant to arrange repairs and deduct the cost *up to $400 or half a month’s rent (whichever is less)*, if the landlord fails to act within 10 days of notice for issues that the tenant can reasonably repair[^17]. You must give written notice, wait 10 days (or 30 if it’s something that reasonably takes longer to begin fixing), then you can hire someone to fix it and deduct up to the statutory limit[^17]. This is found in 25 Del. Code § 5307. If you use repair-and-deduct properly and the landlord later sues for unpaid rent, JP Court will recognize that defense as long as you followed the steps and provide receipts[^17]. No separate pre-suit requirement beyond those notices to landlord. *In practice*, many landlord-tenant money disputes in JP Court involve the tenant saying “I withheld rent because X wasn’t fixed” – JP Court will effectively treat that as a counterclaim or defense under these statutes. Keep all correspondence where you requested repairs, and proof of any expenditures you made. Also note: if you were behind on rent unrelated to the repair issue, you **cannot** use repair-and-deduct or withhold rent – Delaware law disqualifies tenants who are already in arrears from taking advantage of these remedies[^17].

**Unpaid rent or damages by landlord against former tenant:** Yes – as mentioned, a landlord can pursue a former tenant for unpaid rent, late fees, or physical damage after move-out. Example: a tenant broke a year lease 3 months early – you want 3 months’ rent as damages (minus any mitigation). Landlords should be aware of their duty to mitigate (find a new tenant); however, Delaware law doesn’t explicitly codify mitigation in residential leases, but courts generally expect reasonable efforts. You can file in JP Court for the balance of rent owed under the lease or for month-to-month holdover rent. Be sure to credit any security deposit applied and any rent the new tenant paid if you re-rented the unit. Also, if you terminated the lease early by proper notice (for example, tenant violated lease materially), you may claim rent until the unit was re-let or lease ended. **Pre-suit:** As noted above, if you’re suing solely for rent (no possession), make sure you **demanded the rent and gave notice of intent to sue** per 25 Del. C. § 5502(b)[^9]. This could be a simple written notice: “You owe $X; pay within 5 days or I will take legal action.” If that wasn’t done, do it immediately and slightly delay filing, or the tenant could move to dismiss. If the former tenant provided a forwarding address, you should also comply with the 20-day security deposit accounting rule – you can include an itemized list stating amounts applied to damages or rent. Failure to do the deposit accounting can forfeit your claim to keep the deposit (and risk double damages to tenant), though it doesn’t outright bar a suit for rent – but the tenant could counterclaim the deposit penalty, complicating your case. Always subtract the deposit from your claim if you kept it.

**Wrongful retention of tenant property after move-out:** Yes – if a landlord retained or disposed of a tenant’s personal belongings improperly, the tenant can sue for their value. Delaware law allows a landlord to consider property “abandoned” in certain conditions (e.g. tenant leaves and doesn’t claim property within 7 days after eviction, etc.), but they must follow specific steps (like storing it and giving notice). If the landlord simply kept your stuff or threw it out without following legal procedures, that’s a conversion for which you can seek damages. Example: you moved out but mistakenly left a box of expensive tools; the landlord refused to return them – you can sue for their value. There is not a specific statutory penalty for this in Delaware (unlike some states with treble damages for wrongful confiscation), but general conversion law applies. Document what items were taken and their fair value (receipts or online pricing of similar items). If the landlord claims you abandoned them, be prepared to explain any communication showing you intended to retrieve them or that the landlord didn’t give required notice. These cases are usually under the $25k cap unless extremely valuable property is involved.

**Disputes over fees (cleaning fees, late fees, pet fees, etc.):** Yes – a tenant can challenge excessive or unlawful fees in JP Court, or a landlord can sue to collect legitimate fees. Delaware’s Residential Landlord-Tenant Code regulates some fees. For example, late fees are capped (no more than 5% of monthly rent, and cannot be charged until the rent is at least 5 days late)[^18]. Pet deposits cannot exceed one month’s rent (and count toward the 2-month cap on total deposits)[^19]. If a landlord charged improper fees – e.g. a $200 late fee on $800 rent (that’s 25%, illegal), or a $500 “redecoration fee” not in the lease – you can sue to recover those amounts as an “unlawful fee.” Likewise, if a tenant agreed to pay a cleaning fee in the lease but didn’t, a landlord can claim it (provided it’s reasonable and not double-dipping with the deposit). Many times these issues get wrapped into the security deposit accounting: e.g. landlord deducts a $150 cleaning charge but never actually did any cleaning – tenant can dispute that in small claims. Delaware courts will generally allow reasonable cleaning charges if the unit wasn’t left broom-clean, but not if it’s a standard turnover that should come out of normal wear-and-tear. There’s no prerequisite to sue over fees beyond the normal deposit itemization process (if it involved the deposit). So a tenant might sue to get part of their deposit back, arguing the fees withheld were improper – that’s handled in the same deposit statute framework (which favors the tenant if the landlord can’t justify the fee). Landlords, ensure any fee you seek is supported by the lease and actual costs (e.g. if you charge a $100 key replacement fee, have receipts or at least a rationale).

### Employment & wages

**Unpaid final paycheck / earned wages:** Yes – Delaware workers can use JP Court to recover unpaid wages, including a final paycheck, owed overtime, or other compensation. Delaware law (19 Del. Code § 1103) requires employers to pay all wages due at the next regular payday after termination (if the employee was fired or quit)[^20]. If an employer willfully fails to pay without a good reason, Delaware law entitles the worker to **liquidated damages** – essentially a penalty equal to the lesser of either the unpaid wages or 10% of those wages *per day* of delay (up to 100%)[^20]. In simpler terms, if your final paycheck is late and it wasn’t a mistake in good faith, you can get up to **double** the amount: the wages plus an equal amount in penalty, or a smaller penalty if they paid part of it late. Example: your final paycheck of $500 was never given; you sue a month (30 days) later – you could recover $500 + $500 (max penalty, since 10% of $500 is $50 per day, which over 10 days hits $500)[^20]. These **waiting time penalties** are similar to California’s but accrue daily up to the amount of wages. To claim the penalty, you should cite 19 Del. C. § 1103(b) and show the employer had “no reasonable grounds” for non-payment[^20]. Pre-suit: Delaware’s Department of Labor, Division of Industrial Affairs, has a Wage Payment Claim process that employees can use (and it’s free). It’s often effective for straightforward claims and can impose the same penalties. You are *not* required to file with the DOL before suing (as long as you’re not covered by a union grievance or something). However, note that if you already filed a wage claim administratively, the DOL’s decision might affect your ability to sue separately. Many employees choose JP Court if the amount is within $25k because it can be faster or because they want the court judgment for leverage. When filing, include any pay stubs or time records showing what you’re owed. You can also ask for court costs and interest. Attorney’s fees are *not* automatically awarded in wage claims in Delaware small claims (unlike some states) – but the statute allows the Department of Labor or a court to award attorney fees in some circumstances (more so if the DOL brings the case). In JP Court, if you’re self-represented, fees don’t apply. If you did hire a lawyer, you’d need a contract or statute provision to get fees; 19 Del. C. § 1113 allows the DOL or employees to seek fees in some wage cases, but that’s typically in higher court. So don’t count on fees in small claims, just the wages and penalties. 

**Unpaid commissions or bonuses:** Yes – commissions and bonuses that were earned and due can be recovered. Delaware’s wage law includes “commission, bonus, piece rate, or other similar benefit” in the definition of wages if they are agreed upon[^21]. Example: you earned a $2,000 sales commission per your employment contract, but after you resigned the company refuses to pay it. As long as the commission was for work actually done prior to leaving and not forfeited by some clear term, it’s yours. Same for an earned bonus (e.g. end-of-year bonus promised in writing). Delaware requires that any condition or policy that would make you forfeit earned commissions (like “you must be employed on the payout date”) be clear – even then, some such policies might be unenforceable if the bonus was essentially for past services. In JP Court, bring the commission plan or emails detailing it, and evidence of the sales you made. If it’s a **discretionary** bonus (not guaranteed), it might be tougher – but if it was clearly promised for certain results you met, it’s enforceable. The wage statute’s penalty provisions also apply to commissions/bonuses: if it was due and the employer withheld without good reason, you can seek up to double with the 10% per day formula[^20]. No special pre-suit steps beyond perhaps a written demand or internal complaint (which is always good to document). 

**Unreimbursed business expenses:** Yes – if your employer owed you reimbursement for expenses (mileage, supplies, travel) by policy or agreement, you can claim those. Delaware wage law doesn’t explicitly list expense reimbursements as “wages,” but if an employer promises to repay expenses, that’s enforceable as either wages or a contract claim. Example: you spent $300 on work supplies on your personal card expecting repayment, but never got it. You can sue for $300. This likely wouldn’t fall under the wage penalty statute unless your employment contract clearly makes reimbursements part of your wage agreement. Still, it’s a straightforward contract claim. Provide receipts and evidence the employer approved or expected those expenses (emails, handbook policy, etc.). Also note: Delaware law (like federal law) requires employers to reimburse workers for expenses that would bring their pay below minimum wage if not repaid – failing to do so could be a labor law violation. But for small claims, it’s just a debt the employer owes you. 

**Misclassification disputes (money aspects only):** If you were misclassified as an independent contractor when you were actually an employee, you might have claims for things like unpaid overtime or benefits. Small claims can adjudicate the money owed (unpaid overtime, for example), but it will not officially change your classification beyond that case. Example: you worked 50 hours/week but were paid straight hourly as a “contractor” – in reality you should’ve been an employee eligible for overtime (1.5x pay over 40 hours). You could sue for the overtime premium for those 10 extra hours each week. To do this, you’d argue under Delaware and federal wage laws that you were an employee (control tests etc.). JP Court can handle such a wage claim (it’s essentially breach of the implied contract to pay lawful wages, or directly under the wage statute). The Delaware Department of Labor can also handle misclassification and wage claims (and may impose penalties on the employer for misclassification), but you are free to pursue the wages in court. Keep in mind federal Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) claims (overtime/minimum wage) can be brought in state courts. Delaware’s overtime law largely mirrors FLSA (1.5x over 40 for most jobs). If you win, you might get liquidated damages equal to the unpaid overtime under FLSA (effectively double), and possibly attorney’s fees – but again, in small claims, the focus is on the wages themselves, and since attorney representation is allowed, a judge could award fees under the FLSA provision if applicable. These cases can be technical, but JP Court will do its best – if it’s very complex (like it needs extensive discovery), it might get beyond small claims scope, but generally wage claims are fact issues JP can handle.

**When the state Labor Commissioner/Department of Labor is the better forum:** Delaware’s Office of Labor Law Enforcement (within DOL) is a great resource especially if your claim may exceed the small claims scope or if you want enforcement help. For example, if you have a wage claim and you suspect the employer might ignore a small claims judgment, the DOL can put more pressure (they can impose fines or even criminal charges for wage theft in some cases). Also, if multiple employees are affected, DOL can pursue it as a group. If your claim is exactly for wages up to $25k and the employer seems solvent, JP Court is fine. If it’s about things JP Court can’t order – like getting your job back (wrongful termination) or a determination of employee status for tax purposes – those are not available in small claims anyway. In summary: for straightforward wage recovery, small claims or DOL are both options; for anything requiring injunctive relief or an official ruling on status (as opposed to just money), DOL or other venues would be needed. Note that some specific employment issues (e.g. discrimination claims, which involve non-wage damages or reinstatement) must go through administrative processes first (Delaware’s Office of Anti-Discrimination or the EEOC). But those are not typically small claims matters since they often exceed $25k and seek non-monetary orders.

### Personal services / professional disputes

**Disputes with hairdressers, barbers, nail salons (botched service):** Yes – if a personal care service goes wrong and causes you out-of-pocket loss, you can sue for that. Example: a hairdresser severely damaged your hair with the wrong treatment, and you had to pay another salon $300 to correct it, or buy wigs, etc. While you might feel pain and suffering (emotional distress over a bad haircut), JP Court cannot award pain & suffering in such minor torts – it can only give you tangible losses. So focus on economic damages: the cost of fixing the mistake, any refund of what you paid, etc. If a barber simply gave a bad haircut, chalk it up to experience (not usually a lawsuit unless there was clear harm). But if there was negligence causing, say, scalp burns or hair loss requiring medical treatment, then it crosses into personal injury – which JP Court cannot handle if you’re claiming bodily injury or significant pain (that would belong in a higher court even if small dollar, because technically physical injury is excluded from JP Court jurisdiction[^12]). For slight physical harms incidental to the service (e.g. minor burn from a curling iron that just needed a little ointment), you might still bring it as part of your damages here, but be cautious: the court might say any claim for personal injury, however small, is not allowed. It may be safer to limit your claim to the cost of redoing the service or products purchased, etc. Provide receipts and maybe photos of the damage.

**Tutoring, lessons, or coaching not delivered:** Yes – if you paid for lessons (academic tutor, music teacher, sports coach, etc.) and the person did not deliver the agreed sessions or they were of clearly subpar quality breaching the deal, you can sue for a refund or the portion of money corresponding to what wasn’t provided. Example: you paid a tutor $500 upfront for 10 sessions, they only did 4 – you’d sue for the value of the 6 missed sessions ($300). This is a simple contract claim. There are no special licensing issues; tutors and coaches are generally not regulated, so no board to complain to first. Just ensure you document the agreement (emails or texts scheduling sessions, etc.) and any attempts you made to schedule the remaining sessions. If the dispute is that the quality was poor (e.g. “my child’s score didn’t improve at all”), it can be harder – unless the tutor explicitly guaranteed a result, poor quality alone is subjective. But if it rises to essentially no service (they showed up distracted or unprepared such that it’s as good as not having the session), you might still claim a refund. Small claims judges often split the baby if quality is the issue – they might not give full refund if some time was spent. Be prepared to articulate why the service was worthless or didn’t meet what was promised.

**Pet boarding, grooming, or training disputes:** Yes – if you paid for pet services and something went wrong financially, you can sue. Example: you paid a dog trainer $800 and the dog learned nothing because the trainer barely showed up – claim a refund. Or a pet groomer injured your pet and you incurred vet bills (careful: pet’s injury is property damage to you; JP Court can award vet costs). If a pet died or was hurt due to a service, that often is a mix of negligence and contract. JP Court can award the **economic value** (vet bills, maybe the pet’s monetary value if deceased, though courts are reluctant to quantify a pet’s “market value” since generally it’s low unless a special breed with proof of value). They will **not** award emotional distress for loss of a pet – that’s considered non-economic and beyond small claims scope. Many kennels or groomers have you sign liability waivers. Such waivers may not protect them from gross negligence, but do read what you signed. Still, JP Court can consider voiding unfair waivers. Just focus on actual financial loss: e.g. “My dog got a cut that cost $200 at the vet to treat” or “I boarded my cat for a week for $150 but they neglected it so badly that I want that fee back plus vet costs.” Provide receipts, and any photos or vet reports if relevant. No need to complain to the SPCA or others first (though for egregious neglect you should report them to animal welfare, but that’s separate from your civil claim).

**Medical billing disputes (when not covered by insurance or surprise billing issues):** Small claims can handle straightforward medical billing disagreements if they are essentially contract issues. For example, if you paid out-of-pocket for a procedure and later discovered you were overcharged or charged for services not rendered, you can sue for a refund of the overcharge. However, many medical billing issues involve insurance and are covered by complex laws (like the federal No Surprises Act). If it’s a **“surprise bill”** from an out-of-network provider at an in-network facility, new federal law (as of 2022) gives you rights to have that resolved via arbitration – small claims likely wouldn’t be the venue. But if it’s a simpler scenario, like a dentist charged you for four fillings but only did two, or a clinic promised a certain package of services and didn’t deliver all, you can treat it as a contract issue. Be mindful that if your dispute is that the care was negligent (malpractice), that is *not* suitable for small claims (malpractice claims typically require expert testimony and go to Superior Court regardless of amount – plus they’re often above $25k in damages). JP Court is fine for billing errors or breaches of a payment agreement. Example: you went to a physical therapist who agreed in writing that if it wasn’t covered by insurance, they would only charge you a discounted rate, but later they billed full price – you could sue for the difference since that was the agreement. Medical providers might have arbitration clauses in their intake paperwork; check that, because if you signed an arbitration agreement, the provider could move to compel arbitration and get the case out of JP Court. Some arbitration clauses carve out small claims – if so, you’re fine to proceed. So, read the fine print first. 

**Daycare or preschool refund disputes:** Yes – if you paid a daycare or school and withdrew your child with unused time remaining (or they closed early, etc.), you can seek a prorated refund if entitled. Many daycare contracts require advance notice for withdrawal and will not refund the current month’s fees if you leave mid-month, for example. But if the daycare expelled your child or shut down and kept your money, you have a case. Example: you paid $1,000 for a month, and a week in the daycare shut down due to licensing issues – you should get the unused 3 weeks’ worth back. Or you put down a $500 deposit or enrollment fee and the center never opened as scheduled. These are contract claims. Delaware doesn’t have a specific daycare refund statute, so it depends on your contract terms and general fairness. No pre-suit steps besides demanding the money back in writing. Provide the contract and proof of payment. 

### Loans, debts, and financial disputes

**Recovery of money lent to a friend or family member:** Yes (see “personal loans” above) – these are common small claims. It’s simply a contract debt. A text message like “I promise I’ll pay you back $500 next month” can serve as evidence of the loan. Delaware’s 3-year statute of limitations for unwritten loans runs from the date the loan was supposed to be repaid or from the date of the loan (if no specific due date, a court might treat it as due on demand, so the clock starts when demand is made). If you wrote “loan” on a check or have any written IOU, arguably the 6-year limit for notes could apply[^5]. But best practice: sue within 3 years to avoid debates. No interest can be claimed unless you had an agreement on interest (Delaware’s legal interest rate can apply from the date of judgment, but pre-judgment interest on a loan that was not interest-bearing is typically minimal or at the judge’s discretion – they might allow a small interest from default to judgment as damages for loss of use of money, often at the legal rate of 5% over Federal Reserve discount rate[^22]). It’s wise to at least ask for interest in the complaint (like “plus interest from [due date]”), and the court may award some.

**Disputes over jointly-paid expenses (roommates, ex-partners, group trips):** Yes – small claims often sees ex-roommates settling up or friends untangling shared bills. Example: you and your roommate each paid half the rent, but you covered their share for the last two months totaling $1,200 – you can sue to get that $1,200 back. Example: four friends went on vacation, one put the entire $2,000 hotel bill on their card with the others agreeing to reimburse – if two friends never paid their $500 each, you can sue each (or both together) for the $1,000 owed. Under Delaware law, this is just an implied contract: the court will look at the evidence of the agreement to split costs. Texts, emails, or just your testimony about the understanding can suffice (it helps if you can show you asked them to pay and they acknowledged the debt or made partial payments). No formalities required. If multiple people owe you from one arrangement, you can name them all as defendants in one JP Court case (it’s one transaction). If an ex-romantic partner is involved, and the dispute is purely over money (not property ownership or support), JP Court handles it like any other debt. Just be prepared for the other side to possibly claim it was a gift, not a loan – the burden will be on you to prove it was to be repaid. Clear communication and perhaps any written Venmo/cashapp memos help (“Loan for rent” in a payment description, etc.). The statute of limitations is 3 years from when the expense was paid or the breakup occurred (as that’s when an implied obligation to settle up is clear)[^8].

**Disputes over wedding/engagement gifts and rings on cancellation:** Possibly – these can be tricky legally, but JP Court can resolve the monetary aspect. Delaware follows the majority rule that an engagement ring is a **conditional gift**: if the marriage doesn’t occur, the ring should be returned to the giver, regardless of fault (Delaware courts have treated engagement rings as conditional on marriage)【68†source】. So if your ex-fiancée refuses to return the ring (and the ring is worth under $25k), you could sue in JP Court for its value (or for return – technically replevin for the ring itself, which JP Court can do if within $25k value). Replevin (return of property) is allowed in JP up to $25k value[^1]. The court would likely order the ring returned or its fair market value if it’s been sold. Similarly, other costly gifts in contemplation of marriage might be considered conditional gifts depending on circumstances. Outside engagements, general gifts are not recoverable (a gift, once given, is gone). But if it was agreed it would be returned if something – that’s enforceable. For wedding expenses between a couple who split, typically each pays their own unless there was a clear loan. JP Court can’t litigate emotional issues, just who paid for what. Example: you paid $5,000 to the venue and your ex was supposed to pay you back half – if you have evidence of that agreement, you can claim $2,500. Engagement ring cases, due to their personal nature, have been heard in JP Courts in many states for the ring’s value. Provide proof of value (appraisal or receipt). If you are the one who received the ring and broke the engagement, expect the court to enforce return or value to the giver. If they broke it off, Delaware law still usually gives the ring back to the giver (no-fault approach). 

**Disputes over jointly-titled property after a breakup:** Possibly – if unmarried partners bought property together (car, furniture, etc.) and split, small claims can handle a **partition of personal property value** (not real estate). Real estate disputes go to Chancery or Superior. But for personal goods: say you co-bought a $4,000 TV, each paid half, and now one keeps it – the other can sue for their share ($2,000) or ask the court via replevin to get the item (JP Court can order the TV returned to the co-owner, effectively a partition). JP Court is empowered to handle replevin (recovery of personal property) if the thing’s value is under $25k[^1]. If the item is indivisible and you both have equal claim, the court might effectively have one party buy out the other. It’s cleaner if you just seek money for your share rather than the item, unless it has special value to you and you want it back – then file as replevin. Be sure to show proof of purchase and contributions. If both names are on title (like a car), technically one can’t just take it fully. If one took sole possession, you might also involve DMV or file a separate petition to compel transfer, but small claims can resolve the **money** aspect or direct transfer as a form of replevin order (though enforcement of title changes might be beyond small claims – they’d likely just award money equal to your equity). 

**Recovery of cash deposits/earnest money:** Yes – whether it’s a rental deposit (already covered under Landlord/Tenant), or an earnest money deposit for a failed real estate deal, or a deposit on a purchase, you can seek it back if conditions for refund are met. Example: you put $1,000 earnest money on a house and the sale fell through due to seller’s fault (or a contingency), but the seller won’t return it – you can sue. (Real estate purchase disputes often exceed $25k or end up in Superior Court because of equitable relief issues, but for just the deposit return, JP is fine if under $25k.) Another example: you gave a $500 deposit to hold a catering date and then canceled in time per contract but no refund given. Provide the contract and evidence you met any conditions for refund (or that the other party breached, causing the deal to fall through). Note: If it’s **earnest money on real estate**, check if the sales agreement had an arbitration clause or specified forum. Many Realtor contracts do have mediation/arbitration requirements for deposit disputes. If so, small claims might not be available. If none, proceed in JP Court. Delaware doesn’t have a special statute on earnest money – it’s contract law. 

**Creditor (bank, collection agency) suing a consumer in small claims:** Yes – creditors *can* use JP Court to sue debtors (and they often do, especially for smaller debts). Unlike some states, Delaware does **not** ban collection agencies or assignees from filing in JP Court. Any business entity can file a JP Court civil case (they just need to use an attorney or do the Certificate of Representation if appearing without one[^6]). In fact, many credit card or loan collection cases are heard in JP Court for balances up to $25k. So if you are a consumer, you might be a defendant in JP Court for a debt. (As a side note: Delaware does *not* have the California-style rule limiting a collection company to two cases per year – there’s no such limitation here.) If a creditor has a choice, they might go to a higher court if the debt is close to the cap and they want to include attorney fees, etc., but JP Court is perfectly acceptable for them. So, from the plaintiff perspective: if you’re a bank or anyone owed money, you *can* file in JP Court. Ensure you have any required debt collection license if applicable (for instance, collection agencies generally must be licensed, but that doesn’t affect the court’s jurisdiction, more a regulatory issue). 

One thing to note: Delaware requires businesses filing regularly to e-file (see Special Rules for Businesses) and to have representation sorted out. But there is no prohibition on suing as an assignee of a debt. So, a debt buyer who purchased a credit card debt can sue you in JP Court for that balance; they just need to prove the assignment and the debt. If the consumer believes the collection is improper (e.g. violates the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act or Delaware’s debt collection laws), those are separate causes of action – potentially the consumer could counter-sue in JP Court for any statutory damages (FDCPA gives $1,000 statutory damages for violations; JP Court could award that). Delaware has a “Debt Management Services Act” and other regs but no specific private action for general debt harassment beyond the Consumer Fraud Act or federal FDCPA. If you as a consumer want to sue a debt collector for abusive practices (like threatening illegal acts or something), you can do that in JP Court as a tort or CF Act claim as long as you’re only seeking money up to $25k. Statutory FDCPA claims (up to $1k plus provable losses) can be filed in state court including JP Court.

### Vehicle-specific 

*(Many of these overlap with earlier categories, but here we focus on car-related issues.)*

**Accident damage where insurance won’t cover or denies:** Yes – if you’re in a car accident and the other party’s insurance won’t pay (or not fully), you can sue the at-fault driver (and owner, if different) directly in JP Court for your **property damage**. Example: other driver’s insurer disputes liability and you’re stuck with a $3,000 repair bill – you file against the driver for $3,000. As emphasized, if you had injuries, then you likely must go to a higher court for the injury portion. But for property, JP is fine. If your own insurance covered some of it but you have a deductible (say $500) or an uninsured loss, you can sue for that amount. Sometimes your insurer (subrogation) might sue the other driver – if they do so in JP Court naming you, that’s fine (though usually subrogation by insurers happens in higher courts or arbitration between insurers). Regardless, as an individual you absolutely can bring the claim. Just be prepared to prove the other driver’s fault (police report, witness statements). Delaware is **comparative negligence** state – if you were partly at fault, your recovery can be reduced by your percentage of fault. JP Court will weigh that. If you’re more than 50% at fault, you recover nothing (that’s Delaware’s rule for negligence). So if the other side claims you slammed on brakes or something, the judge may assign percentages and reduce the judgment accordingly. Bring two repair estimates or the actual paid bill to substantiate amount (judges like at least an estimate even if you haven’t fixed it yet, and if you did fix, the invoice). 

**Mechanic/body shop disputes:** Yes – if a mechanic performed unauthorized work, did faulty repairs, or you’re disputing the bill, you can sort it out in JP Court. For example, a body shop charged for genuine OEM parts but you discovered they used cheaper aftermarket parts – you might sue for the difference in value or demand partial refund. Or a mechanic’s repair didn’t actually fix the issue and you want a refund. These can be breach of contract or consumer fraud issues. Delaware doesn’t have a specific “Automotive Repair Act” requiring estimates or anything (some states do; Delaware mainly relies on general consumer protection). So your best angle is misrepresentation or breach: “I paid for X and did not receive it.” If the work caused new damage (like the mechanic’s negligence blew your engine), that’s property damage – you can claim the cost to fix the new damage. As mentioned, property damage via negligence is within JP power[^12]. Evidence: all invoices, and perhaps an expert statement from another mechanic if you’re claiming improper work (courts understand if you don’t bring an expert for small claims due to cost, but a written statement or at least your own explanation plus any obvious evidence helps). Often, just showing that you had to pay someone else to redo the job properly will convince the judge. No pre-suit requirement except maybe giving the shop a chance to make it right (if you didn’t allow them any chance, the court might feel you didn’t mitigate – but if you lost faith for good reason, that’s understandable). If the dispute is over the amount charged relative to an oral estimate, note that Delaware’s Consumer Fraud Act could apply if they materially underquoted to get your business then gouged (that’s a deceptive practice). You could mention that in your case for potentially a finding of an unlawful practice (though again, actual damages is what you get).

**Dealership disputes (sales):** Covered earlier under false advertising and lemon issues. To add: Delaware has an Automotive Warranty Enforcement Act (the Lemon Law for new cars, § 5001-5009) which generally requires the manufacturer to be given a reasonable number of attempts to fix a defect and if they fail, to provide a refund or replacement. Small claims *can* be used by a consumer to seek the Lemon Law refund (the law doesn’t forbid small claims), but practical issues arise: the refund often equals purchase price which may exceed $25k. If it doesn’t (e.g. a $20,000 car), you could try in JP Court, but you must show you followed the Lemon Law steps (written notice to manufacturer, etc.). Often the manufacturer will argue such cases belong in Superior Court. Also, Lemon Law allows prevailing consumers to recover attorneys’ fees – JP Court could award fees if evidence of them is presented (again, attorneys are allowed in JP, so possibly fees are recoverable per statute). But realistically, few Lemon Law claims go to JP. More common: used car sales fraud (odometer or hiding defects) or breach of the **implied warranty** (if dealer sold “Certified” used car that immediately fails). Delaware’s Consumer Fraud Act is often the way to frame it, as discussed. There’s also a **Deceptive Trade Practices Act** (6 Del. C. § 2532) but that lacks a damage remedy for private parties (it’s mainly for injunctive relief via the Attorney General). So stick with Consumer Fraud Act or common law fraud. If an odometer rollback is proven, that’s also a federal violation (with a $10,000 statutory damage or treble actual damages, whichever greater). Federal odometer claims (49 U.S.C. § 32710) can be brought in state court – but since the statutory damage is $10,000 minimum, that’s beyond JP’s $25k if trebled, well actually $10k is within $25k. Hypothetically you could sue in JP under the federal Odometer Act for $10,000 statutory (if actual damages are lower). It hasn’t been commonly done because usually those cases go to federal court. But it’s an interesting idea. Consult a lawyer in such big-fraud cases – you might get more by going to higher court or class action. For garden-variety used car issues under $25k, JP Court is quick. 

**Towing/impound disputes:** Yes – if your car was towed illegally or without proper notice and you had to pay fees, you can sue the towing company (or property owner who ordered it) for reimbursement. Delaware has towing regulations (e.g., required signage on private lots, a cap on tow fees around $150 for normal tows, etc. – specific rules are in local ordinances and DMV regs). If a tow operator violates those (for example, “patrolling” and towing without owner’s authorization, or charging excessive fees above the allowed limit), you have a cause of action under consumer law or as conversion of your vehicle. Example: your car was towed from an apartment complex without the required warning sign at the entrance – that tow was unlawful, so you could claim the $200 you paid to get your car back. Or if they damaged your car during the tow, you can claim property damage. Delaware’s Division of Motor Vehicles licenses tow operators; you could also complain to the DMV’s Tow Truck Oversight if they exist – but for your money back, JP Court is suitable. Provide receipts from the tow yard and photos of any vehicle damage. Note the timeline: these claims should be brought within 3 years[^8]. 

**Parking damage when you can identify the responsible driver:** Yes – if someone hit your parked car and you fortunately learned who it was (say through video or they left a note or were caught), you can sue them for the damage. This is just like any vehicle property damage claim. Often their insurance will pay if they cooperate – but if not, JP Court is your recourse. Another scenario: someone opened their car door and dented yours badly and refused to cover it – that’s minor property damage, sue them. Use any witness or notice they left as proof they admitted fault. If it happened in a parking lot with cameras, subpoena the footage if needed (though in small claims it can be tough to get video from third parties, but you can try). Usually these cases default if the person doesn’t show.

### Personal injury (small/minor)

**Minor personal injury cases (within the cap):** *Caution:* Delaware JP Court **cannot** hear cases seeking damages for “personal injuries” (bodily injury, pain & suffering)[^12]. Even if the dollar amount is small, the JP Court civil jurisdiction explicitly excludes claims for physical injuries to a person. Those must be filed in a higher court (Court of Common Pleas for claims up to $75k, or Superior Court). So, for example, a $5,000 medical bill from a minor car accident *cannot* be pursued in JP Court because it involves personal injury, even though $5k is under the monetary limit. The rationale is that injury cases often involve more complex evidence (medical testimony, etc.) and the law directs them to higher courts. Therefore, **personal injury cases do not belong in Delaware small claims**, no matter how small. 

If you attempt to file a bodily injury lawsuit in JP Court, the court will dismiss it for lack of jurisdiction. So if you slipped and sprained your ankle due to a store’s negligence and your urgent care bill was only $300 – you still must go to the Court of Common Pleas (or resolve informally), not JP Court. The only time an injury might come into JP Court is if it’s incidental to a property claim and you waive any compensation for it (e.g. “I’m only suing for the torn clothing, not my bruises”). But that is rare and not advisable; generally, separate the issues: property = JP, personal injury = higher court.

Thus, the “small” personal injury cases are effectively **not eligible** in Delaware small claims. They either get bumped up or not pursued. (When in doubt, consult an attorney – sometimes an injury that seems minor can later have complications, so handling it in the proper court is important.)

### Specific statutory claims (state-specific)

Delaware’s statutes create some specific claims that a person can bring in small claims court. We’ve touched on a few: bad checks, consumer fraud, etc. Here’s a summary of key ones:

- **Bad Check Damages:** (Covered above in Money-Owed disputes) – 6 Del. C. § 1301A allows $50 or triple-the-check statutory damages for NSF checks, with required 30-day demand[^10].
- **Consumer Protection/UDAP:** Delaware Consumer Fraud Act (6 Del. C. § 2513 & § 2525) – prohibits deceptive practices and allows the victim to sue for actual damages[^13]. *Note:* No automatic treble damages or fee award for private suits, but if the conduct was willful/malicious, one could argue for punitive damages (though JP Court is unlikely to award punitives).
- **Lemon Law:** 6 Del. C. § 5003 – If manufacturer fails to conform a new car to warranty after a reasonable attempts, the owner is entitled to a replacement or refund. You could seek the refund (prorated by usage) in court. However, these claims often exceed $25k or the manufacturer will insist on arbitration. Delaware lemon law does provide for attorney fee if consumer prevails, but in practice these are not done in JP Court.
- **Identity theft/fraud-related:** Delaware has an Identity Theft Passport program and some laws against identity theft, but for money damages, you’d use general tort (fraud) or consumer fraud act. There’s no special extra damages for identity theft victims aside from possibly punitive if malice is shown. You can sue the impostor for whatever money you lost or credit harm (though proving credit damage in dollars is tricky). Many times, identity theft issues (like unauthorized accounts) get resolved by federal law (FCRA disputes with credit bureaus, etc.), which might end up in federal court. But if, say, someone you know fraudulently opened a credit card in your name and charged $5,000 – you could sue that individual in JP Court for the money you had to pay or costs incurred. Bear in mind the criminal aspect (the person could be prosecuted, and restitution ordered – if so, you can’t double-recover, but you can sue if needed).
- **Bad faith insurance claims:** If you are suing your own insurance for refusing coverage (like property damage claim denial), those are typically contractual and could be in JP Court if under $25k (e.g., your homeowner’s insurer owes you $10k and won’t pay). However, **insurance bad faith** as a tort (seeking punitive damages) is beyond small claims – those belong in Superior Court and often yield >$25k if proven. So in small claims context, stick to the contract amount the insurer should have paid. Delaware does allow statutory interest if an insurer unjustifiably delays (in some cases), but there’s not a specific treble damage law for general insurance nonpayment in small claims.
- **Wage and labor statutes:** We mentioned Delaware’s wage payment law (which allows up to 100% liquidated damages[^20]). Another one: Delaware has a “Construction Payments” statute that gives contractors a right to prompt payment and 1% per month interest if not paid (and possibly attorney’s fees) – if you’re a contractor suing an owner or general contractor for unpaid work under $25k, you can use JP Court and cite Del. Code Title 6, § 3501 et seq. (Construction Payments Act). That act provides 1% monthly interest and attorney fee possibility for willful nonpayment. JP Court can award the interest (that’s straightforward math) but might not handle attorney’s fees unless you actually had an attorney and prove fees.
- **Residential landlord/tenant statutes:** Already covered – double deposit damages (25 Del. C. § 5514) and rent withholding/repair remedies (25 Del. C. § 5306-5308) are statutory claims you assert in small claims.
- **Towing without authorization:** Delaware law (21 Del. C. § 4505) requires signs for towing from private lots and sets maximum fees. If violated, that can fall under Consumer Fraud Act (misleading practice) or conversion. No special treble damages, but sometimes the statutes allow fee shifting to the prevailing party – not explicitly here though.
- **Debt collection violations:** Delaware has its Consumer Fraud Act as the main state analog to FDCPA. There isn’t a separate Delaware Fair Debt Collection Practices Act granting a private right – the AG enforces debt collection abuses under the Consumer Fraud Act. But as a consumer, you could sue a collector under the Consumer Fraud Act if they, for example, falsely represented the character or amount of a debt (e.g., trying to collect a debt not owed). Federal FDCPA (15 U.S.C. § 1692) gives $1,000 statutory plus fees to a successful consumer – you can bring an FDCPA claim in JP Court for up to $1,000 (statutory) + any actual damages (maybe lost wages for dealing with it, etc., if under $25k). The FDCPA allows fee-shifting (so if you hire an attorney and win, the collector pays fees).
