In JP Court, if you went pro se, no fees to shift; if you hire an attorney, it could exceed the $25k by their fees and might be more appropriate in higher court. So practically, most FDCPA go to federal or CCP. But JP is an option if you only want the $1,000 and to do it yourself or with minimal counsel help.
- **Small claims in Delaware are generally for compensatory damages only** – any statutory multipliers or fixed penalties (like the bad check triple or wage penalties) are considered compensatory (a form of liquidated damages), so JP Court can award those because the statute specifically authorizes it. Pure punitive or statutory penalties not tied to compensation are rarely available (JP Court tends to require a clear statute).

### Other state-specific claim types

*(Miscellaneous disputes one might bring in Delaware JP Court:)*

- **Homeowners Association (HOA) / Condo Association disputes:** If your HOA fines you or you believe the HOA owes you money (maybe you overpaid dues or they damaged your property), those monetary claims can be in JP Court if under $25k. Delaware’s Unit Property Act or Condo Act doesn’t mandate a different forum for small money issues. Note: If an HOA puts a lien on your property for unpaid assessments, to contest the lien’s validity or remove it, you’d typically go to Chancery. But if you just want a judgment that, say, “I don’t owe this $500 fine,” JP Court can effectively do that by hearing the HOA’s claim for the fine (if they sue you) or by you suing the HOA for a refund if you already paid under protest. Many HOA bylaws require arbitration or internal dispute resolution – check that first. But those usually aren’t binding if you choose court. 
- **Mold remediation or repair disputes:** If a landlord or seller failed to disclose mold and you had expenses, or you paid for mold remediation and think someone else should reimburse, etc., you can sue for those costs in JP Court as long as it’s a contract or tort basis and under $25k. Delaware has a Landlord-Tenant Code requiring landlords to maintain safe premises – if you had to pay for mold cleanup due to landlord’s negligence, that folds into habitability damages as discussed. 
- **Automatic renewal subscriptions (state law compliance):** Delaware has a statute (6 Del. C. § 2734) requiring clear disclosure for auto-renewing contracts and a reminder for long-term auto-renewals. If a company violated that and kept charging you, you may have a claim under Consumer Fraud Act to get those charges back. It’s a novel use, but possible. JP Court could handle the refund of the unauthorized renewal charges. 
- **Unauthorized towing or booting:** (See above in vehicle disputes.) 
- **Small business vs. vendor disputes:** e.g., you’re a small biz owner suing a supplier who didn’t deliver – absolutely, that’s just a contract claim. Delaware doesn’t require a business to have counsel in JP (they can use Form 50 as discussed), so small businesses often use small claims to collect debts or resolve vendor problems. 
- **Agricultural/farm disputes:** e.g., sale of a cow gone bad, crop damage, etc., as long as money and < $25k, JP Court is fine. 
- **Fishing/hunting issues:** If, say, a charter boat service didn’t provide what was promised (like a deep sea fishing trip got cancelled and no refund), you can small-claim it. If it’s about license fees or fines, those are handled administratively, not in small claims. 
- **Rideshare/gig economy disputes:** If you’re an Uber driver and you have a dispute with a local party (not Uber corporate, which likely goes to arbitration via their contract) – e.g., a passenger vomited in your car and the company didn’t reimburse cleaning though policy said they would – you could sue the responsible person (maybe the passenger) in small claims. Or if you’re a customer whose property was lost by a gig worker (like a delivery driver lost your package and the company refused refund), you could attempt small claims against the company or driver. Many gig companies have terms forcing arbitration though. If not, JP Court is an option under standard contract/tort law. 
- **Cell phone early termination fees** (ETF): Most carriers have contracts that would be hard to fight in small claims due to arbitration clauses. But if you did have a situation to recover an unfair fee, perhaps under consumer fraud, it’s possible but rare. 
- **Ticket scalping or event ticket disputes:** If you bought tickets on a resale and got scammed (fake tickets) – you can sue the seller for the money back. That’s fraud. If it’s through a platform like StubHub with a guarantee, they usually refund, but if not, the individual could be taken to JP Court. 
- **Airbnb or short-term rental deposit holds:** If you rented a vacation property and the host wrongly kept your security deposit or charged you for damage you didn’t do, you can sue for that amount. Airbnb’s terms push arbitration for disputes, but if you deal directly with a host (outside the platform), small claims works. Even with the platform, some users try small claims if the arbitration clause is opt-out or arguably not binding. But be aware of those terms. 
- **Storage facility auctions (if improper):** If a storage company sold your stuff allegedly for nonpayment without following notice procedures, and your items were worth under $25k, you could sue for their value. That’s under the Self-Service Storage Facility Act (Delaware requires certain notices before auction). If they breached it, JP Court can award damages (the value of items lost). 
- **Slander/libel (if under $25k)**: *Not allowed.* Defamation is considered an injury to reputation (not a physical injury, but still not tangible property). JP Court’s jurisdiction doesn’t explicitly list defamation, and “personal injuries” exclusion likely covers it as well (courts often treat defamation as not in small claims). So you cannot sue for defamation in JP Court – those go to Superior Court regardless of amount. 
- **Minor probate or estate claims:** JP Court does not handle estate matters (those go to the Court of Chancery or Register of Wills). If, say, someone owed money by a deceased person wants to sue the estate, it must be through estate claims procedure, not JP Court. So nothing estate-related in small claims beyond maybe if an estate or trust owes you from a contract, but normally, no. 
- **Taxes:** Tax disputes (state or local tax assessments) are not heard in JP Court. Those have their own tribunals or courts. 
- **Bankruptcy-related**: Once someone files bankruptcy, an automatic stay stops all lawsuits, including JP Court. JP Court cannot hear a case against someone in bankruptcy unless stay is lifted. Also, if a debt was discharged in bankruptcy, JP Court will not enforce it (the defendant can raise discharge as defense and it will be dismissed).

*(In summary, any civil claim seeking only money under $25k and not involving personal physical injury or a purely equitable matter is likely eligible for JP Court. The above lists cover the vast majority of real-world small claims scenarios in Delaware.)*

### Counterclaims

**Can a defendant file a counterclaim in the same case?** Yes – if you’re sued in JP Court, you have the right to file a **counterclaim** against the plaintiff to be heard at the same hearing[^11]. The counterclaim does **not** need to be related to the plaintiff’s claim (though often it is). For example: Landlord sues for rent; tenant counterclaims for property damage to their furniture from a leak. Or a contractor sues for unpaid bill; homeowner counterclaims for the cost to fix the contractor’s bad work. When you are served with a JP Court complaint, you’ll also receive a blank “Answer” form (Civil Form No. 7) and you can indicate a counterclaim on that or on a separate Counterclaim form (JP Civil Form 01-CC)[^11]. 

**Counterclaim dollar limit:** The counterclaim is subject to the **same $25,000 limit** as the main claim[^1]. You cannot counter-sue for more than $25k in JP Court. If your counterclaim would exceed $25k, you have two choices: waive the amount above $25k and stay in JP, or move to transfer the case to a higher court that can hear the larger amount. Delaware law allows transfer if a counterclaim over the jurisdictional limit is raised – essentially the case may be removed to the Court of Common Pleas or Superior Court (Superior if it exceeds CCP’s limit). The plaintiff (counter-defendant) can also ask for transfer rather than proceed with a higher amount. Typically, if your counterclaim is above the limit, the JP judge will not hear it; they’d ask if you want to **waive the excess** and cap it at $25k to keep it in JP[^1]. If not, the judge will likely dismiss the counterclaim (without prejudice) or refer you to file it in higher court – and the original claim might continue in JP or possibly be consolidated in higher court upon motion. Practically, many defendants choose to waive some damages to resolve everything in one forum.

**Procedure:** To counterclaim, ideally file an Answer and Counterclaim **within 15 days** of receiving the complaint (that’s the time to answer)[^11]. If you miss that, JP Court may still allow it up until the trial date if the plaintiff isn’t prejudiced (but it’s risky – best to file ahead). There is a filing fee for a counterclaim as well (usually the same as filing a new claim, determined by the amount of counterclaim). According to the fee schedule, a counterclaim is treated like a new claim for fee purposes (e.g. $35 if under $1k, etc.)[^11]. If you truly cannot afford the fee, you could apply for fee waiver (in forma pauperis). 

**Counterclaim forms:** JP Court has Civil Form 7 (Answer) which has a section to write in a counterclaim, and Civil Form 01-CC (a dedicated Counterclaim form) that you can use to type out a longer counter[^11]. The counterclaim document should state the amount and the reason you are counter-suing.

**Example:** John sues Mary for $5,000 over a loan. Mary believes John actually owes her $7,000 for unpaid work she did. Mary can counterclaim $7,000. But because $7k is within $25k, it stays in JP. Suppose it was $30,000 – she’d have to reduce it to $25k or else not counterclaim here. If she reduces it, she is effectively waiving the extra $5k; she can’t try to recover that later separately (since it’s the same transaction presumably).

**What if counterclaim exceeds cap?** As mentioned, Delaware allows removal in some situations. JP Court Civil Rule 13(j) says if a counterclaim exceeds JP jurisdiction, the defendant may move to transfer the action to a court of competent jurisdiction (e.g., CCP or Superior) before trial[^3]. If granted, the entire case goes to that court. If not moved, the counterclaimant is deemed to waive the excess over $25k[^3]. So it’s a strategic choice. 

**Effect on original claim:** Filing a counterclaim means both claims will be decided together. JP Court will either net them out (if both sides owe each other, they’ll offset in the judgment) or give separate judgments. Often, if a counterclaim equals or exceeds the plaintiff’s claim and defendant wins it, it may result in plaintiff getting zero and possibly owing the balance.

**If defendant fails to counterclaim now, can they sue separately later?** Generally, yes – Delaware’s Justice of the Peace Court rules do *not* appear to have a compulsory counterclaim rule as strict as higher courts. However, if it’s arising from the same transaction, it’s smarter to do it in one case. If a defendant chooses not to counterclaim and later sues the plaintiff on the same transaction in another JP case, the court might apply res judicata (claim preclusion) and say it should have been raised earlier. To be safe: raise it now if it’s related.

**Filing a counterclaim form:** You’ll submit it to the JP Court that’s handling the case (the address on your summons). You must also *serve* the counterclaim on the plaintiff. The court typically will mail it to them (since they’re already in the case, mailing suffices). There’s a specific “Notice to Plaintiff of Counterclaim” that goes out.

### What you CANNOT sue for in small claims (Delaware-specific exclusions)

Delaware JP Court is limited in the types of cases it can decide. **It cannot issue injunctions** or hear purely equitable matters. Here are things **you cannot file** in Delaware small claims, and must go to another court or forum:

- **Eviction / Possession of real property:** You cannot use a regular small claim to evict a tenant or anyone in possession of real estate. Evictions (summary possession actions under 25 Del. C. § 5702) are handled in JP Court *but on a separate track* (a landlord files a “Summons in Summary Possession” action, not a civil complaint for money). Those cases often include rent claims, but the process (time to answer, trial scheduling) is different from small claims. If you erroneously file a normal JP civil complaint asking “order tenant out and pay rent,” it will be dismissed or redirected – you need to file under the landlord/tenant docket. Likewise, foreclosure of a mortgage or lien (to get title to property) is not JP Court – those go to Superior Court. JP Court can only do money judgments or orders for return of personal property. So for **any issue about ownership or the right to possess real estate**, do not use small claims.

- **Family law matters:** JP Court does not handle divorce, child support, child custody, alimony, adoption, or anything under Family Court jurisdiction. You cannot sue your ex-spouse in small claims for things properly part of a divorce decree (like property division or unpaid child support – those are enforced through Family Court). Example: if an ex owes you $5,000 per a Family Court property division, enforce it in Family Court, not via JP Court (JP would dismiss it as Family matter). Similarly, disputes arising from a separation agreement typically go to Family Court or Superior (depending on how it’s enforced). 

- **Defamation (libel/slander):** As discussed, **personal injury** includes reputational harm. Delaware JP Court has **no jurisdiction over defamation** claims. Even if you only want $5,000 for slander, it must be filed in a higher court (Superior Court, since defamation is a tort beyond JP competence). The reasoning is that defamation cases involve complex issues of law and often non-economic damages, which JP Court cannot award. So do not file a slander lawsuit in JP Court – it will be dismissed.

- **Title to real estate / boundary disputes:** JP Court cannot determine title or ownership of real property. Those cases go to the Court of Chancery or Superior Court (for ejectment, quiet title, etc.). If your case fundamentally requires the court to declare who owns land or where a boundary lies, JP Court won’t do it. (Exception: JP Court can handle “trespass” in the sense of an entry causing damage – for example, someone building on your land – you can sue for damage from trespass, but not for an injunction to remove the structure or a legal decree about the boundary). So, **no adverse possession claims, no easement fights, no partition of real estate** in small claims. 

- **Malpractice (medical, legal, etc.):** Professional malpractice claims are typically for personal injuries or large financial harm and require expert testimony. They cannot be properly presented in JP Court even if under $25k. For example, if a dentist’s negligence caused you $10,000 in remedial treatment costs, it’s technically a personal injury and beyond JP. Also, Delaware Superior Court has exclusive jurisdiction over medical negligence claims (and Delaware law requires an affidavit of merit from an expert to even file one). So malpractice is firmly out of small claims. The same is true for legal malpractice – those go to higher court regardless of amount.

- **Federal claims exclusively in federal court:** JP Court cannot hear cases that are under exclusive federal jurisdiction: e.g., federal tax disputes, federal civil rights claims against a government (42 USC §1983 claims for constitutional violations must go to Superior or federal court), bankruptcy (only Bankruptcy Court handles bankruptcy proceedings or violations of stays/discharges), patent or copyright infringement (federal courts only). If you try to sue someone for patent infringement for $10,000 in JP Court – no, it will be dismissed for lack of subject matter jurisdiction.

- **ERISA or other federal statutory cases requiring federal venue:** e.g., suits regarding ERISA-governed benefits often are federal-exclusive or at least beyond small claims expertise. Those usually end up in U.S. District Court. 

- **Workers’ compensation:** Claims for on-the-job injuries are not lawsuits against the employer but handled through the Delaware Workers’ Compensation system (administrative process). You cannot sue your employer in JP Court for an injury at work – that’s barred by comp exclusivity. Any disputes about comp benefits go to the Industrial Accident Board, not regular court.

- **Wrongful termination or discrimination:** These are not small contractual disputes; they involve statutory rights and often non-economic damages. They are handled in Superior Court or through the Delaware Department of Labor (for discrimination, you must go to DOL first). You cannot, for instance, sue your employer in JP Court for $5,000 for “firing me because of age discrimination.” That must follow the proper channels (and likely be in Superior Court if it gets filed in court at all). A narrow exception: if it’s a simple breach of an employment *contract* (not union) for unpaid salary or severance, that portion is a wage claim which you *can* bring as we discussed. But stuff like emotional distress or punitive damages related to a firing – not in JP.

- **Specific performance or injunctive relief:** JP Court has no equitable powers to make someone do or not do something (aside from ordering return of property via replevin). So you cannot ask JP Court to enforce a contract by making the other side perform – you can only ask for money. For example, if a contractor breached by not building your deck, you can’t ask JP to order them to finish the deck; you sue for the cost to hire someone else. Or if a neighbor’s fence is over the line, you can’t ask JP Court to make them move it (that’s injunctive); you could only sue for any damage it caused or diminution in your property’s value (and even that gets tricky – better handled in Chancery with an injunction).

- **Government as defendant (sovereign immunity):** You generally cannot sue the State of Delaware or its agencies in JP Court for damages due to sovereign immunity, unless the State waives immunity for that type of claim. For instance, if a state employee vehicle hit you, you’d usually have to bring a claim under the State Tort Claims Act in Superior Court (with many limitations). JP Court is not the venue for claims against the State or U.S. government. Similarly, you can’t sue the City of Wilmington in JP for a slip-and-fall on a sidewalk; there are notice requirements and it goes to Superior Court (and immunity may apply). If you have a small claim against a local government (say the city trash truck dented your mailbox), often the city will just pay it on a claim form – but if not, you’d have to check statutes; often suits against municipalities *can* be in state courts, but usually higher court and subject to formal notice of claim within 1 year etc. JP Court doesn’t handle those by default. And you absolutely can’t sue the federal government in state court without going through the Federal Tort Claims Act process (administrative claim first, then federal court if denied). So no suing the USPS in JP for losing your package; that’s a federal issue.

- **Bankruptcy stay or discharge issues:** If someone owes you money but they filed bankruptcy, JP Court cannot proceed with the case due to the automatic stay. If they already got a discharge, JP Court will not allow you to collect a discharged debt. Those matters are exclusively handled by the Bankruptcy Court. So if you inadvertently file suit on a discharged debt, the defendant just needs to show their discharge order and the case will be dismissed (and you risk sanctions in bankruptcy court). Always check if someone who defaulted may have filed Chapter 7/13. 

- **Class actions or multiple plaintiffs beyond joinder rules:** JP Court has no mechanism for class action lawsuits. You cannot represent a class of people – each must file their own case. You can have multiple plaintiffs join if it’s the same transaction (like a joint lease and both tenants suing together – that’s fine). But anything like a class or mass tort is out of scope.

*(In summary, Delaware small claims is for straightforward money disputes. If your case involves land, family matters, injury to body or reputation, government defendants, or needing a court order that isn’t just “pay money” or “return item,” then JP Court is not the right venue.)* 

### Damages — what you can and cannot recover in small claims

**Compensatory damages:** These are the bread-and-butter of small claims – money to compensate your actual loss. JP Court can award compensatory damages for financial losses, including: out-of-pocket costs, unpaid debts, contractually owed sums, property repair or replacement costs, medical bills (if they were in a case JP can hear), lost wages (if proven and within scope of the claim). Essentially, any monetary loss directly resulting from the wrong can be awarded, up to the $25,000 limit. You need to prove the amount with reasonable certainty (bills, receipts, estimates). JP judges often ask, “How did you get to this figure?” and expect documentation or at least a credible explanation.

**Punitive/exemplary damages:** JP Court generally **does not award punitive damages**. Punitive damages (meant to punish the wrongdoer beyond compensating the victim) are typically decided in higher courts, and Delaware’s JP Court rules and jurisdiction do not explicitly authorize them. Even if you have a case where punitive might be justified (say a fraud done willfully), the small claims judge is unlikely to grant an extra amount beyond your actual loss **unless** a statute effectively includes it as a multiplier. For example, the bad check law’s triple damages or wage law’s double damages are forms of liquidated damages authorized by statute – those are allowed and not considered “punitive” in the legal sense (they are statutory compensation). But asking the judge, “I want $5,000 actual and $5,000 punitives for the egregiousness” – that won’t fly in JP Court. Punitive damages above actual loss are the domain of Superior Court where a jury might be available. So, assume you can’t get punitives in small claims. (The Consumer Fraud Act allows the AG to get civil penalties in Superior Court, but a private plaintiff in JP can’t just get a penalty.) If you truly want punitive damages for a small tort, you’d have to file in a higher court (and meet the standards for punitive, which are high – malice or outrageous conduct).

**Statutory multipliers or penalties:** As noted, some statutes effectively give you more than your actual damages – those **are recoverable in JP Court if the statute applies**. We have several in Delaware:
  - Double damages for wrongfully withheld security deposit (by statute)[^15].
  - Triple damages (up to $250) for repeat bad checks, or $50 for first bad check[^10].
  - Up to 100% liquidated damages on unpaid wages[^20].
  - Possibly 1% per month interest on contractor’s unpaid invoices (that’s interest rather than multiple of principal).
These are considered additional damages authorized by law, and JP Court will award them if you prove entitlement. Just be sure to cite the statute in your complaint or at trial so the judge is aware. For example, say: “Under 19 Del. C. § 1103, I’m asking for liquidated damages equal to the unpaid wages”. The judge should then include that in judgment if criteria are met. Remember, any statutory cap (like the $250 max on bad check triple) still applies.

**Attorney’s fees and court costs:** 
  - **Attorney’s fees:** Generally, each party pays their own lawyer in small claims (and many go without lawyers to save cost). JP Court will **only** award attorney’s fees if a statute or contract specifically provides for it. E.g., if a contract says “prevailing party gets reasonable attorney’s fees,” the court can enforce that. Or if you sue under a statute that includes fees (like FDCPA or Delaware’s landlord tenant code for some specific things), you could request it. But these situations are not common in JP cases. One common scenario: suits on **promissory notes or credit card debts** often have a contract clause for attorney’s fees (like 15% of balance). Delaware law requires that to get fees on a note, the plaintiff had to include a copy of the note and demand an affidavit of defense[^6]. If the defendant doesn’t submit a sworn defense, the note holder can get a default including fees as stated. JP Court will honor such clauses up to reasonableness. Also, Delaware by statute gives a fixed attorney fee for certain debt collections (called “Attorney’s commission” of 20%) but I believe that’s in Superior Court on certain judgments, not JP. So short answer: don’t expect attorney’s fees unless a clear entitlement exists. 
  - **Court costs:** Yes, if you win, JP Court will typically award you your **court filing fee and service fees** as part of the judgment (they’ll be added to what the defendant owes)[^22]. Delaware law (10 Del. C. § 5101) says the prevailing party is entitled to costs[^22]. In JP Court, “costs” include the filing fee, any mailing or constable service fee, any witness subpoena fees you paid, etc. The judge usually asks, “Did you have costs?” and will include those. If you only partially prevail, the court might split costs. But generally, the losing party pays the costs of the suit. Note: if you used eFiling, there’s a $1.25 tech fee – that might or might not be passed on; likely it is since it’s part of filing fee. If you hired a private process server by choice, the court may or may not tax that cost (since JP Court would serve by mail/constable for you normally). It’s safer to use the court’s service methods to ensure costs are recoverable.

**Interest (pre- and post-judgment):** 
  - **Pre-judgment interest:** If you are seeking interest on the amount from the time of loss until judgment, you should explicitly demand it in the complaint. Delaware law allows pre-judgment interest as a form of compensatory damages especially in contract cases (the Legal rate is 5% over Federal Reserve Discount Rate)[^22]. In JP Court, judges often award pre-judgment interest on liquidated claims (like unpaid loan or invoice) as a matter of course if asked. If you don’t ask, they might not add it. They might calculate from the date each payment was due or a single date of demand. You can also specify a contract rate if the contract had one, provided it’s not usurious (Delaware has no strict usury cap for loans between individuals except consumer credit has some regs). So, for example: “I ask for 6% annual interest from June 1, 2025 to judgment.” The judge can include that. It just cannot push the total over $25k – but interest accruing doesn’t count toward the jurisdictional cap (the code even says interest may be added even if it causes total > $25k[^1]). So yes, you can get pre-judgment interest and it won’t violate the cap since the cap is on the matter in demand *excluding* interest and costs.
  - **Post-judgment interest:** Once you get a judgment, Delaware law sets the post-judgment interest at **5% over the Federal Reserve Discount Rate (including surcharges)** or the contract rate, whichever is less[^22]. As of recent times, Fed discount rate ~0.75%, so legal rate ~5.75%. It fluctuates with Fed policy. JP Court judgments accrue interest at that legal rate by default, even if not mentioned in the judgment. (Many judgments will cite “interest at the legal rate from date of judgment”). If your contract had a lower rate, that might apply instead if you bring it up. This interest continues annually until the judgment is paid or expires. 
  - Delaware does not allow “interest on interest” (no compounding unless contract specified periodic compounding). So it’s simple interest on principal. If you partially collect, interest continues on the balance.

**Filing fees and service fees as recoverable costs:** Yes, as noted, the winner can get reimbursed for:
   - Filing fee ($35–$45 depending on claim amount)[^11].
   - Service of process fees (e.g., $40 constable fee for a summary possession writ; in regular cases, initial service by certified mail is included in filing fee in JP, but if you needed an alias or special process server, that additional $20 alias fee or server cost can be added)[^11].
   - Subpoena fees ($10 each) if you paid to subpoena witnesses[^11].
   - If you had to do an **alias summons** because defendant couldn’t be served initially, there’s a $20 alias fee[^11] – if you eventually win, you can ask for that $20 back from defendant as part of costs.
   - **Certified mail costs**: If service was by certified mail and you bought postage, normally JP Court does the mailing and charges a fee in costs. If you did it manually under long-arm, you could list those mailing costs.
   - Note: If the defendant had filed a **counterclaim and they win net, they might get costs**. If both have some wins, the court can offset or decide who is “prevailing.” Prevailing usually means the party who ends up with a money judgment in their favor or successfully defends all claims.

In summary, you can recover:
- Actual damages (up to $25k).
- Plus any statutory extra damages (double, treble etc. as applicable).
- Plus court costs and possibly pre- and post-judgment interest.
- Possibly attorney’s fees if provided by statute/contract (rare in JP).
All combined, the total judgment can exceed $25k *only because of added interest or costs*, which is allowed[^1]. The principal amount in controversy must be ≤ $25k.

Lastly, note that JP Court judgments are plain money judgments. They do not automatically create liens on real estate (unless you transfer it to Superior Court docket). So collecting the judgment is another step (addressed next in Post-Judgment).

## 3. Where to file

**Court Name:** In Delaware, small claims cases are filed in the **Justice of the Peace Court** (abbreviated JP Court). There is no separate “Small Claims Court” by name – JP Court is the entry-level court that handles minor civil cases. Delaware has multiple JP Court locations (numbered Courts) across the state, but all operate under the same rules. When you file, you will choose a specific JP Court location (by number) as the venue.

**Venue (which county/court to choose):** Delaware JP Court has state-wide jurisdiction, meaning a JP Court in any county can technically hear a case arising anywhere in Delaware[^2]. **However, as a practical matter, you should file in a court that is convenient and proper based on the defendant’s location or where the dispute happened.** The rule of thumb: file in the county where the **defendant resides or does business**, or where the contract was made/performed or where the dispute arose. For example:
- If the defendant is a person, file in the county where they live. If you sue a New Castle County resident in a Sussex County JP Court, it’s legally allowed (since JP Court jurisdiction “extends throughout the State” and process can go into any county)[^2], but the defendant might request transfer for convenience.
- If it’s a business defendant: use the county of its principal office or where the services were provided.
- For auto accidents or property damage: you can file where the accident occurred or where the defendant lives.
- For landlord/tenant: residential landlord-tenant cases are usually filed in the county where the rental property is (and indeed in a specific court designated for landlord/tenant). (Eviction cases must be in the county of the property).
While Delaware’s law doesn’t rigidly force venue by geography, you risk inconvenience or even dismissal for forum non conveniens if you deliberately choose a far-flung court with no connection. The court may not dismiss for improper venue per se (since statute says statewide), but the defendant can petition for a change of venue to a nearer JP Court citing fairness.

**Venue within county:** Each county has multiple JP Court numbers for different regions/functions. For a debt or trespass case, you can pick any JP in that county. Usually, you’d pick one closest to the parties. For instance, New Castle County has JP Court #13 (Wilmington), #12 (Newark), etc. If you file online, the system may guide you to appropriate courts.

**Wrong venue – consequences:** Since jurisdiction is statewide, filing in the “wrong” place (like a distant court) isn’t jurisdictionally fatal, but the case might be transferred. If a case would cause undue hardship (e.g. all parties are in Wilmington and you filed in Georgetown), the defendant can raise that. The JP Court might then transfer it to a closer JP Court or at least require you to show cause. To avoid any issues, follow the common-sense venue suggestions above.

**Online filing (e-filing):** Yes, Delaware JP Court offers **electronic filing** through a system called **eFlex**[^23]. In fact, certain filers (like attorneys or high-volume filers) are *required* to e-file in JP Court per administrative directive[^23]. Individuals (self-represented parties) are not required, but you may do so if you create an eFlex account. The Delaware Courts website has an e-Filing portal for JP Court with instructions[^23]. E-filing lets you submit the complaint and pay fees by credit card or court debit account. After filing, you can also e-file additional documents and check case status online. If you prefer, you can still file in person or by mail on paper at the JP Court.

To use eFlex: you register for an account, then follow the prompts to file a new case. You’ll need to scan and upload the Complaint form and any attachments as PDFs. The system will calculate the filing fee and technology fee ($1.25) and take payment. One advantage: the system will serve the defendant by mail for you unless you indicate otherwise, and you can track service.

**Physical filing:** If not e-filing, you can file by going to the JP Court clerk’s office or mailing in your forms. You must use official JP Court forms (Complaint form CF01, etc.) available on the court’s website or at the court. When filing by mail or in person, include an original plus one copy of the complaint for each defendant (the court will serve them).

**Address for defendants:** Make sure you have a **valid street address** for each defendant for service. JP Court will mail a summons and complaint to that address via certified mail and first class. They prefer a street address over a PO box for personal service[^11]. If the defendant is an out-of-state resident or company, you can still file in JP Court if jurisdiction is proper (like they did business in DE or the claim arose in DE). For out-of-state defendants, Delaware’s “long-arm” statute (10 Del. C. § 3104) allows service by certified mail to their out-of-state address[^11]. JP Court will often instruct *you* to arrange that mailing and then file an affidavit of service if successful. The **venue** for an out-of-state defendant technically can be any JP Court (since no county is “home” for them – often plaintiffs choose JP Court 13 (Wilmington) for such cases out of custom). 

**If wrong venue is chosen:** A case filed in the wrong court location may be **transferred** to the correct JP Court location by a court order. It likely won’t be outright dismissed unless there’s a real issue (like you filed an eviction in the wrong county – they’d transfer to a JP in the correct county authorized for evictions). Filing in the wrong county might delay your case significantly, so it’s best to pick wisely to begin with.

**Summary:** Delaware is flexible on venue but as a best practice:
- File in defendant’s county or locus of dispute.
- Use eFlex to find appropriate JP Court or call the JP Court clerk if unsure.
JP Court’s **Jurisdiction and venue statute** explicitly says process can be issued from any county into any county[^2], effectively meaning you won’t get thrown out solely for being in a different county, but courtesy and tradition lean toward proper locale.

## 4. Filing fees

The Justice of the Peace Court charges filing fees based on the amount of the claim. The fee must be paid when you file the case (or a fee waiver requested). Below is the fee schedule (as of 2026) for typical JP Court civil filings[^11]:

| **Claim Amount** (in controversy)         | **JP Court Filing Fee** (Complaint) |
|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| $0 to $1,000                              | $35[^11] |
| $1,000.01 to $5,000                       | $40[^11] |
| Above $5,000 (up to $25,000 max)         | $45[^11] |
| Landlord/Tenant Summary Possession (eviction) | $45 (for possession or rent only cases)[^11] |
| Counterclaim (same tiers by amount)      | Same fee as if filing a new claim of that amount[^11] (e.g. $35 for counterclaim up to $1k, etc.) |
| *Alias* (second attempt) Summons issue   | $20[^11] |
| Distress for Rent (pre-judgment seizure for unpaid rent) | $60[^11] |
| Replevin action (to recover property)    | $55 filing fee[^11] |
| Wage or Property Execution (garnishment or levy) | $30[^11] |
| Motion to Vacate Judgment (default or nonsuit) | $15[^11] |
| Subpoena (to compel witness)             | $10 each[^11] |
| Transcript of Judgment (to transfer to higher court) | $10[^11] |
| Certified Mail Service (if done by court) | Included in filing fee for initial service (no separate charge for first mailing) |
| *Technology Fee* (for e-filing)          | $1.25 (added to e-filed cases)[^23] |

*(Fees current as of Nov 2021 per court schedule; always verify on Delaware Courts site for any updates.)*

Explanation:
- The **filing fee is determined by the amount you are suing for** (not counting court costs or interest). For example, if suing for $8,000, the fee is $45. If counterclaiming for $800, fee is $35. These fees cover the court’s processing and the initial service attempt(s) by mail or constable.
- **Multiple defendants:** You generally pay one filing fee per complaint, no matter how many defendants, as long as it’s one case. However, if service on additional defendants requires extra steps (like certified mail to each), the court may include that in the cost – usually the initial fee covers one defendant’s service; additional defendants might incur a small extra mailing cost. Often though, $45 is flat even if 2 defendants – JP Court doesn’t explicitly list an add-on per extra defendant in civil fees.
- **Service of Process fees:** In JP Court, initial service is typically by mail or constable and *is included in the filing fee*. If the court has a constable personally serve, there’s no separate fee beyond what you paid. If that fails and you need an alias or special process server:
  - An **alias summons** (2nd attempt) costs $20 to issue[^11]. That covers sending another certified mail or constable out again. You’d file a request for alias and pay $20.
  - If you opt to hire a **private process server** or the sheriff for service (for out-of-state, etc.), you pay them directly. Those costs can later be claimed as costs (with proof of expense) if you win, but the court doesn’t collect it upfront.
  - For **out-of-state service by certified mail**, you may have to purchase the postal services and then file an affidavit. That cost (postage, etc.) is not part of filing fee; you’d bear it, but can recover it if you win.

- **Fee waivers (In Forma Pauperis):** If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can file a motion to proceed *in forma pauperis* (IFP) asking the court to waive fees due to indigence. There is a specific JP Court form for IFP, usually needing a sworn statement of your income, assets, and expenses. The court may grant it if you are truly unable to pay. Delaware courts often look at things like receiving public assistance or income under the poverty line. If granted, your case can be filed without paying the fee, and possibly service will also be covered. Note that if you win and recover money, the court might order the fees paid out of that or by the other side. If you lose, the waived fees just remain unpaid (the state eats the cost). You can get the IFP forms from JP Court or the website. **Important:** IFP is a privilege; frivolous claims won’t be excused fees. The court reviews for serious financial hardship.
  
- **Other costs:** If you need to subpoena a witness to ensure they show up, it’s $10 per subpoena[^11]. If you want a certified copy of a judgment or any record, there might be small copying fees. 
- **Recoverability of fees:** As mentioned, if you win, the court will add your filing fee (and alias fee, etc.) to the judgment for you[^22]. So eventually the defendant reimburses it. If you lose, you’re out the fee (and could be ordered to pay the other side’s filing fee if they had a counterclaim you lost on).

- **Payment methods:** In person, JP Courts accept cash, money order, or maybe credit cards (some do for fees). E-filing requires credit/debit or a pre-approved court account. If mailing in, use a check or money order to “Justice of the Peace Court”. Do not send cash by mail.

**Fee changes:** Delaware’s JP fees are set by court rule and require Supreme Court approval[^11]. They changed slightly in 2019 (tech fee added) and earlier (fees used to be $30/£35/£40 tier and got raised). The last update on record was Nov 5, 2021[^11]. Always double-check the current fee schedule on the Delaware Courts website or call the JP Court clerk, as these can change.

**Are fees recoverable?** Yes, as noted in Section 2 Damages, the winning party can get back filing and service fees[^22]. So, while you have to front the fees, you should ask for them in judgment. Typically the court’s judgment form has a line awarding costs to the prevailing party.

## 5. Statute of limitations

The statute of limitations (SOL) is the deadline by which you must file your lawsuit. After the SOL expires, the defendant can raise it as a defense to dismiss the case. In Delaware, limitations periods for civil claims are mostly found in Title 10, Chapter 81 of the Delaware Code. Here’s a breakdown by type of claim (assuming Delaware law applies):

| **Type of Claim**                      | **Limitations Period**        | **Legal Authority**                      | **Notes (When it starts, tolling)**            |
|----------------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Written contract (not under seal)      | 3 years                      | 10 Del. C. § 8106(a) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=,the%20accruing%20of%20the%20cause)) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=unaccompanied%20with%20force%20or%20resulting,8110%2C%208119%20and)) | Most contracts (written or oral) are 3 years. Clock starts on date of breach (when payment was due or duty not performed). If the breach was hidden, *maybe* tolling until discovered (courts allow in fraud, but less so in plain contracts). |
| Promissory note, bill of exchange, or written acknowledgment of debt (signed by party) | 6 years | 10 Del. C. § 8109 ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=When%20a%20cause%20of%20action,of%20such%20cause%20of%20action)) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=When%20a%20cause%20of%20action,of%20such%20cause%20of%20action)) | This covers negotiable instruments and written IOUs. Time starts when the payment was due or note was defaulted. (E.g. a note due on Jan 1, 2020 – SOL runs Jan 1, 2026). If it’s an installment note, breach occurs at each missed installment (but Delaware law might require suing all due at once after acceleration). |
| Oral contract (and implied contracts)  | 3 years                      | 10 Del. Oral agreements often have no clear end date – if it was a one-time exchange, from non-performance; if open-ended, arguably from when one party ended the arrangement or reasonable time passed. |
| Open account (mutual, running account) | 3 years                      | 10 Del. C. § 8106(a) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=chattels%2C%20no%20action%20to%20recover,8110%2C%208119%20and))         | An open book account or store credit account is subject to 3 years. In practice, the clock may restart at the date of the last transaction or last payment on the account (Delaware law references “mutual accounts” under §8106). If a partial payment is made, it can reset the SOL from that payment date (acknowledgment of debt can toll). |
