**When does the clock start?** Generally:
- For a breach of contract: when the breach occurs (e.g., when payment was due and not made, or when services weren’t delivered as scheduled).
- For tort/property damage: when the injury happens or is discovered (if inherently unknowable, then when it was or should have been discovered with reasonable diligence).
- For fraud: when discovered (the limitation is 3 years but tolled until discovery if plaintiff was blamelessly ignorant of the fraud) ([legis.delaware.gov](https://legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731#:~:text=This%20Act%20would%20both%20raise,Currently%2C%20if%20a%20party%20is)).
- If a contract involves ongoing duties, each breach may start its own clock. But often a single transaction has one SOL.

**Tolling exceptions:** Delaware recognizes a few:
- **Minority or incapacity:** If the plaintiff is a minor or legally incompetent at the time of cause, SOL is tolled until the disability is removed. For minors in personal injury, they get until age 18 plus 2 years (so effectively until age 20) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=infants%2C%20persons%20insane%2C%20imprisoned%20or,of%20the%20land%20in%20controversy)). For minors in contract or property matters, typically tolled until 18 then normal period. (There’s an old rule: minors have 3 years after turning 18 for general claims if SOL expired during minority, but personal injury is specifically 2 years after 18).
- **Fraudulent concealment:** If defendant actively concealed the cause of action, SOL tolled until plaintiff discovers or should have discovered the wrong.
- **Absent defendant:** Delaware law tolls if defendant is out of state and not subject to service in Delaware during the period of absence ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=out%20of%20the%20State%2C%20the,such%20person%20departs%20from%20and)) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=resides%20or%20remains%20out%20of,the%20commencement%20of%20the%20action)). However, since Delaware long-arm allows service by mail out-of-state, the tolling for absence may be less relevant except for those who truly cannot be found. Nevertheless, 10 Del. C. § 8117 provides that if after a cause accrues, the person is out of state, the time of absence isn’t counted in SOL ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=out%20of%20the%20State%2C%20the,such%20person%20departs%20from%20and)).
- **Acknowledgment or part payment:** If a debtor acknowledges a debt in writing or makes part payment, Delaware resets the SOL from that date for the remaining balance ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=instrument%20under%20seal%2C%20no%20action,and%208127%20of%20this%20title)). (Must be an unequivocal acknowledgment).
- **Victory of related litigation:** Delaware has a “savings statute” of sorts – not a broad one, but if you file in time and the case is dismissed not on the merits (like for lack of jurisdiction or improper venue), you might be able to refile within a certain time (usually the remainder of SOL or 1 year whichever longer) under equitable principles. Delaware doesn’t have a specific codified 1-year refiling grace (some states do), so be careful.

**Bottom line:** File well before the statute runs out. If you are even close to the deadline, file first and sort out negotiation later.

In JP Court, the SOL is an affirmative defense – meaning the defendant has to raise it to win on that basis. If you file late and the defendant doesn’t show up to raise it, you could technically win by default. But that’s risky and not ethical to rely on – and if the judge notices obvious lateness, they might question it or even deny the claim on their own (judges can, though technically they shouldn’t raise SOL if not raised by defendant). Many JP Court defendants are pro se and might not know to assert SOL, but you shouldn’t bank on that. Better to file within time.

## 6. Pre-filing requirements

Before filing a small claim, consider if Delaware law imposes any **mandatory steps** (like a demand letter or alternative dispute resolution). Delaware generally does **not require a formal demand letter** in most cases, but there are some specific **pre-filing notices or efforts** required in certain types of disputes:

- **Demand letters (not strictly required, but advisable):** Delaware doesn’t have a blanket demand-letter requirement for small claims. However, sending a written demand for payment or performance is **highly recommended** in almost every case. It can sometimes avoid the need for court and, if not, it documents that you gave the other party a chance. In Delaware JP Court, you won’t be penalized for not sending one (except in a few instances below where statute requires it), but judges appreciate seeing that you tried to resolve it first. For example, in a contractor dispute, a polite demand might prompt a settlement or reveal the defense ahead of time. Always keep a copy of any demand letter/email and proof of delivery (certified mail or at least regular mail + email) to show the court if needed.

- **Statutory pre-suit notice requirements:** 
  - **Bad checks:** *Yes*, as covered: you must send a **written demand at least 30 days** before suing to claim the statutory damages for a bad check[^10]. The letter should demand the amount plus up to $40 fee, and state that failure to pay will result in a lawsuit seeking the statutory additions. If you don’t send this, you can still sue for the base amount of the check, but you can’t get the $50 or triple damages penalty.
  - **Residential security deposit:** *Technically no pre-suit demand required beyond what the statute mandates the landlord to do.* However, **for tenants**: Delaware law expects tenant to provide a **forwarding address** and to make a **written claim within 1 year** for any deposit return[^15]. While not a “demand letter” per se, if a tenant fails to give a new address, the landlord may not be liable for not sending the deposit. Also, if a landlord doesn’t refund, the tenant can go straight to suit (after 20 days). It’s wise for the tenant to send a letter after the 20-day period stating “You failed to return my deposit, now I demand double as per §5514.” This isn’t required by law (the law itself creates the right to double after day 20 without further notice), but it underscores the claim before litigation.
  - **Rent claims by landlord (without eviction):** *Yes*, Delaware requires that before filing a **suit for rent only**, the landlord **must have demanded the rent and given notice of intent to sue**[^9]. Usually this is a simple written notice: “As of [date], $X rent is overdue. If not paid within [e.g., 5 days], I will initiate legal action.” If a landlord files a rent suit without that prior demand/notice, the tenant can move to dismiss or the court might continue the case to allow notice. It’s an easy step: ensure you can truthfully say in your complaint (there’s often a check-box or you can state in affidavit) that “On [date], I demanded payment of this rent and notified Defendant of my intent to file suit.” If you haven’t, consider sending a quick demand and perhaps attach a copy when you file – at least it’s on record.
  - **Home improvement contractor disputes:** Delaware does not have a pre-suit mediation requirement like some states (e.g., some states require giving a contractor 30 days to fix defects before suing under a construction defects statute – Delaware has no such statute for typical home improvements). Unless the contract itself mandates arbitration or mediation (some home improvement contracts do), you can directly file. If the contract has an arbitration clause, that’s not a pre-*filing* requirement by law, but it means if you file, the contractor can move to compel arbitration. So check your contract for any ADR clause. If it exists, send the required notice to initiate arbitration rather than suing. If you ignore an arbitration clause, you risk the case being stayed or dismissed.
  - **Auto defects (lemon law):** Before suing under Lemon Law, you must have given the manufacturer or dealer written notice of the defect and an opportunity to repair (at least 4 attempts or 30 cumulative days out of service) as per statute. Also, many manufacturers have an informal dispute resolution program (e.g., BBB Auto Line) that you might have to try first if it’s certified under federal law. Delaware’s lemon law says a consumer must resort to the manufacturer’s informal arbitration if it’s provided and certified, before exercising the right to a lawsuit (6 Del. C. § 5008). So if you skip that and file, the manufacturer could argue the suit is premature. In practice, since small claims can’t handle these well, you likely go through arbitration or file in Superior Court anyway. But be aware of that requirement.
  - **Credit card or debt collection suits (by creditors):** There’s no statutory requirement of a pre-suit notice to consumer for general debts (some states have “demand letter before suing on consumer debt” – Delaware doesn’t). However, for **claims under the Consumer Fraud Act**, one must give the AG a copy of the complaint when filed (not before, but at filing) – not applicable to small claims usually because private CFA suits not requiring that notice except for class actions.

- **Mandatory mediation or ADR:** Delaware JP Court does **not require mediation** before trial, but it often offers voluntary mediation on the day of trial with court mediators (especially in New Castle County). There’s no pre-filing mediation program. Family Court and Chancery have mandatory mediation in some cases, but JP Court doesn’t. The only exception: some JP Courts (like those hearing homeowner association cases or certain disputes) might encourage the parties to mediate through NCCO Justice Center or the Community Dispute Resolution Program voluntarily. But it’s not mandated by law. If both parties agree, the court can refer you to a mediation session, often free, on the trial date or before. Many small claims get resolved via mediation in the courthouse hallway or with a volunteer mediator. **Bottom line:** no requirement to mediate first, but it will be available at court if you want to try settlement.

- **Notice rules specific to landlord-tenant or consumer:**
  - For **landlord-tenant**, aside from deposit and rent demands noted, also if you are suing for property damages, it’s good form to have done a move-out inspection and given the tenant a chance to dispute the charges (the law doesn’t require you to do more than the 20-day list, but if you did an inspection with tenant, that helps).
  - Some **HOA bylaws** require giving the board notice and a chance to cure an issue before suing the HOA. So if you’re an owner suing your HOA for say not repairing something they should, check the bylaws for any dispute resolution requirement.
  - **Insurance claims:** If you plan to sue an insurance company in JP for a small amount (say your own insurer for a claim), check your policy – most insurance contracts require the insured to provide notice of the claim and proof of loss, etc., and often to engage in an appraisal process for disputes on value. You must comply with those conditions precedent. For example, a homeowner’s policy may require you to submit a sworn proof of loss within 60 days of request – failing that could bar your claim. Those aren’t statutory, but contractual pre-suit requirements.
  - **Breaches with cure periods:** Some contracts give the breaching party a right to cure. E.g. a service contract might say “If client is dissatisfied, provider shall be given 30 days to remedy before client may pursue legal action.” It’s not law, but that clause will be enforced by JP Court. So if you had such an agreement, send a written notice and wait the 30 days before suing, or else the other party can argue the suit is premature.

- **Special notice for default judgments:** If you are suing on a **debt or account stated**, Delaware law (10 Del. C. §3901) allows you to attach a copy of the instrument and include a specific notation that an affidavit of defense is required[^6]. This isn’t exactly pre-filing, it’s part of the complaint. If you do that, the defendant must file a sworn answer detailing defenses, or the court can fast-track a judgment. This is more used in Superior Court, but it can apply in JP Court as well for, say, a promissory note. It’s a procedural tool to get default if no real defense.

- **Optional demand letters and their benefits:** Even when not required, a well-crafted demand letter (sent via certified mail, detailing your claim, the amount, a deadline maybe 10-14 days to respond, and stating you will sue if not resolved) can often lead to a settlement or at least flush out the defendant’s excuses. Some defendants, upon receiving a formal letter on paper, will pay to avoid court. Also, if the case proceeds, you can show the judge you tried. Delaware judges, being human, appreciate when plaintiffs act reasonably and give fair warning.

- **Pre-suit settlement attempts:** Delaware has a “offer of judgment” rule in higher courts to shift costs if a party refuses a settlement then does worse at trial. In JP Court, no formal rule like that, but you can always negotiate before filing. If the amount is small, sometimes simply filing is what gets the other party to pay (they see you’re serious). You have to balance that with SOL and facts: don’t negotiate so long that you blow the limitation period.

- **Arbitration clauses:** If your dispute arises from a contract that has an arbitration clause (common in consumer and business contracts), that is essentially a required pre-judicial step. If you file in JP Court despite an arbitration clause, the other side can file a motion to compel arbitration under the Federal Arbitration Act or state law. The JP Court will likely dismiss or stay the case in favor of arbitration if the clause is valid and encompasses the dispute. So check all agreements for phrases like “any dispute will be resolved by binding arbitration.” If it’s there, you can either attempt arbitration or hope the other side ignores it (if they don’t compel arbitration, they waive it, and JP can proceed). But if they invoke it, your case can’t continue in JP. Some arbitration clauses carve-out small claims (like “except claims under $5,000 may be in small claims court”) – in that scenario, it’s explicitly allowed for you to choose small claims. So read carefully. For example, many cell phone contracts do allow small claims for individual disputes. If so, you’re fine filing in JP.

**To summarize pre-filing needs:**
- Usually send a demand letter except in clear futility or statutory exceptions wherein you either must or it doesn’t matter.
- Ensure any required statutory notices (bad check, wage claim to employer, etc.) have been done.
- For specialized or licensed areas (e.g. if a government agency offers a remedy), consider using them if required (not usually in small claims except vehicles or discrimination).
- Confirm there’s no contract clause barring you from court (arbitration).
- For landlord/tenant, follow proper notice (5-day demand for rent, etc.) if applicable.
- In any case, by the time you file, gather your documentation, since JP Court moves fast (trial may be in a month or two). Pre-filing is a good time to assemble evidence and attempt one last amicable resolution.

## 7. Forms required

Filing a Delaware small claim means completing the correct **Justice of the Peace Court forms**. The forms are available on the Delaware Courts website and at JP Court clerk offices. You’ll generally need:

- **Civil Complaint form (JP Civ. Form No. 1):** This is the primary form to start a case. It asks for plaintiff and defendant information, the amount claimed, and a brief statement of the claim. Delaware’s JP Civil Form No. 1 is a multipart form; Form 1A is often the instructions. On the form, you’ll check whether it’s a debt action, tort (trespass) action, replevin, etc., and provide a narrative of what happened. *Example:* “Plaintiff claims Defendant owes \$2,500 for unpaid loan given on 5/1/2025, due 8/1/2025, not paid ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=chattels%2C%20no%20action%20to%20recover,8110%2C%208119%20and)). Demand for payment made and refused. Claiming principal \$2,500 + court costs + interest.” You must sign the complaint (if e-filing, an electronic signature). [^11]

- **Information Sheet (Form JP Civ. 7 or CF01 Information Sheet):** Delaware’s JP Courts require an “Information Sheet” for each party for the court’s records. It includes each party’s name, address, phone, and if known, date of birth or business EIN, and email. This is not served on the opponent; it’s for court use. The civil instruction booklet references that you need to provide an information sheet and suggests Form 7 (for parties’ contact info) or a combined sheet [^11]. When e-filing, you input these details into the system, fulfilling this requirement. On paper, some JP Courts have a cover sheet to fill out with addresses and any attorney representation details.

- **Certificate of Representation (JP Civ. Form No. 50) – for businesses or organizations:** If the plaintiff or defendant is a corporation, LLC, partnership, or other artificial entity *and* will not be represented by an attorney, that entity must file a Form 50 **Certificate of Representation**. This form basically certifies that the entity has authorized a specific officer or employee (who is not a lawyer) to represent it in JP Court ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=or%20estate%2C%20must%20file%20the,A%20Form%2050%20may%20be)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)). It includes an agreement to be bound by that representative’s actions. It must be notarized and filed with the Chief Magistrate of JP Court and in the case file, and *renewed annually* with a $20 fee ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=If%20you%20are%20a%20corporation,you%20may%20only%20appear%20in)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=for%20Civil%20Form%20No,filing%20the%20complaint%20or%20answer)). **So, if you are a business filing without an attorney, include Form 50**. Example: “ABC LLC” wants its manager to handle the case – attach a notarized Form 50 signed by a member of LLC. If you don’t, the court will not let the non-lawyer appear. (Attorneys, of course, don’t need that; but if an employee is going, it’s mandatory). If you’re a defendant being sued and you’re a business, you likewise need to submit Form 50 with your answer to respond without a lawyer ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=or%20estate%2C%20must%20file%20the,A%20Form%2050%20may%20be)). The form is available on the court’s site and was updated effective 11/28/2023[^150].

- **Summons (JP Civ. Form 2, 3, or 4):** A Summons is the official notice to the defendant to appear/answer. In many states, the plaintiff prepares a summons. In Delaware JP Court, the clerk typically generates and issues the Summons for you once you file the complaint. The forms are pre-made: Form 2 for in-state individual, Form 3 for in-state corporate, Form 4 for out-of-state service ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L578%20,2%2C%203%20or%204)). If you’re filing, you usually do **not** need to fill out the Summons – the court does it and will mail it out. Just be aware that a Summons will be sent to defendant commanding them to answer within 15 days (or to appear at trial if an answer isn’t required, e.g. in an eviction or replevin it might schedule a trial). If you e-file, the system handles creation of the summons electronically.

- **Answer form (JP Civ. Form No. 7) for defendant:** When a defendant is served, they receive a blank Answer (Form 7) to fill out and return within 15 days ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L314%20Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)). As a plaintiff, you don’t file this; it’s for defendants. But if you’re the defendant, you should use it. On it, they can admit or deny the claim and also assert any **counterclaim** or affirmative defenses. Notably, if a counterclaim is presented, the Answer form has space, but if lengthy, one can attach a **Counterclaim form (JP Civ. Form 1-CC)** ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L524%20figure,the%20filing%20has%20been%20completed)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L524%20figure,the%20filing%20has%20been%20completed)). That form is essentially formatted like a complaint but filed by defendant as part of answer. As a defendant in JP Court: file the Answer (and counterclaim if any) within **15 days** of receiving the summons/complaint ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_startcivil.aspx#:~:text=Once%20you%20have%20filed%20your,service%20of%20process)).

- **Motion or affidavit forms (various):** If certain issues arise, there are forms:
   - **Motion to Vacate Default/Nonsuit (JP Civ. Form No. 13):** If you got a default judgment against you, use this form to ask for it to be set aside within 15 days (or 30 days if service was by mail) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=In%20most%20cases%2C%20a%20party,In%20seeking%20to%20vacate%20a)). Form 13 must be notarized and explains why you didn’t appear ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L336%20,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)). Similarly, a plaintiff can use it to vacate a nonsuit (case dismissed because plaintiff no-show) within 10-15 days ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=cases%2C%20a%20party%20seeking%20to,entry%20of%20the%20default%20judgment)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=vacate%20a%20non,or%20if%20service%20was%20made)).
   - **Bill of Particulars (Demand & Response forms):** If a defendant doesn’t understand the claim, they can file a **Notice of Demand for Bill of Particulars (JP Civ. Form 10BP)**. The court will then order the plaintiff to give more detail. The plaintiff would respond with **Form MISC 27C** (certificate of service of their Bill of Particulars) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L471%20Form%20No,%28For%20Plaintiffs%20that%20are)). These are used if the initial complaint is vague. You as plaintiff might preemptively supply particulars as an attachment to avoid delays.
   - **Application for Default Judgment:** If the defendant fails to answer in time, in JP Court the case typically gets set for trial anyway ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)) or you may have to apply. In debt cases, if no answer, the court may require you to submit an **Affidavit of Military Service** (stating defendant is not in military, to comply with federal law) and an **Affidavit of Amount Due (JP Civ. Form No. 14)** itemizing principal, interest, costs, etc. Delaware’s court often will send you a “Notice to Plaintiff to file for default” (Form 13 perhaps) which you fill out and notarize indicating the defendant is not in active military and the sum certain is correct ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L336%20,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=%28Civil%20Form%20No,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)). This ensures compliance with the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act. If you provide that, the judge can enter default without a hearing in a sum certain case.
   - **Praecipe and Writ forms for post-judgment (execution):** Not needed at filing but later if you win: **Form 17 (Request for Garnishment)** for wages or bank, and **Form 17A/17B** for answers from garnishee ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=a%20Civil%20Form%20No,The%20plaintiff%20should)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=After%20the%20request%20for%20garnishment,are%20any%20reasons%20why%20the)). **Form 16** for levy on personal property (venditioni exponas sale). **Form 15A** to revive a 5-year-old judgment[^24].
   - **Notice of Appeal forms:** If a losing party wants to appeal to the Court of Common Pleas, they need to file a **Notice of Appeal (Common Pleas form)** and a **JP Civil Form A (Certification of Transcript)** to get the record sent up. This happens after judgment and is obtained from the JP Court or online instructions – not needed at initial filing.

- **Supporting attachments:** While not “forms,” you should attach copies of key documents to your complaint if possible:
   - If suing on a **written contract or note**, attach a copy of it. There’s a rule that if you attach the instrument and specifically require an affidavit of defense, it can expedite default (10 Del. C. §3901) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c039/#:~:text=,must%20be%20answered%20by%20affidavits)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c039/#:~:text=,%C2%A7%E2%80%821738)). It’s optional, but attaching helps prove your case up front.
   - If your claim is on an **account or itemized charges**, consider attaching an account statement or invoice as a Bill of Particulars from the start.
   - For **security deposit** claims, attach lease sections on deposit and any communications.
   - For **damage claims**, you might attach a repair estimate or photos (JP Court will accept attachments; in eFlex you upload them as exhibits).
   - Every document you attach should also be provided to the defendant (the court will serve attachments along with the complaint when you file them together).

**County-specific forms differences:** The forms are uniform state-wide. The only slight variation: certain JP Courts that handle specialized dockets (like Court 13 in New Castle deals with debt cases heavily) might have local preferences but they still use the same numbered forms. So, no need to fret about different counties having different forms – Delaware JP uses standardized forms.

**Where to get forms:** 
- Online: Delaware Courts website’s **Forms** database (choose Justice of the Peace Court -> Civil Forms). JP Civil Form No. 1 (Complaint) is available in PDF to print and fill ([cocodoc.com](https://cocodoc.com/legal-catalog/civil-procedure-forms/south/6#:~:text=257%20Civil%20Procedure%20Forms%20,in%20the%20united%20states%20court)). They often have them at the clerk’s office if you walk in – they can help you fill it out (to some extent; clerks can’t give legal advice, but they’ll give procedural guidance).
- E-Flex: If e-filing, you might not fill a PDF complaint – you’ll enter data in fields. Some e-filing platforms still require uploading the actual complaint form as PDF, but Delaware eFlex may have a guided interface. It’s good to prepare a PDF complaint anyway in case.

**Completing the Complaint (Form 1):** Key things:
  - Fill in names: If suing a person, use their full legal name (and any aliases or d/b/a). If suing a business:
    * If it’s an LLC or corporation, use the exact registered name and add the entity type (Inc., LLC, etc.). Businesses must be represented by an attorney or Form 50 as noted.
    * If suing a sole proprietor or partnership under a trade name, list the owner: e.g. “John Doe d/b/a Doe’s Plumbing” or “Doe’s Plumbing (John Doe, owner)”. Delaware’s rule is you should sue the actual person behind a fictitious name.
  - Address: A street address for defendant is crucial for service. Include apartment/unit numbers.
  - Amount: State sum certain amounts. If you have multiple elements (principal, interest, fees), you can break it down in the narrative but the form will ask one total.
  - Type of action: check the box (Debt, Trespass (tort), Replevin, Landlord/Tenant (rent), etc.). “Trespass” in JP Court context means any tort (property damage or personal injury, though personal inj. not permitted – so basically property tort).
  - Military status of defendant: The complaint form or subsequent default forms may require you to indicate if defendant is in active military service (for default protections under SCRA).
  - Signature: You (plaintiff) or your attorney must sign. If you’re an officer for a company, you sign and print name/title (and ensure Form 50 on file).

**Filing and service forms process:**
Once you submit the complaint (with info sheet and Form 50 if needed), the court will create a Summons (Forms 2/3/4) and send it, along with a copy of your complaint and any attachments, to the defendant(s). Service is typically by:
  - Certified mail, restricted delivery, return receipt, + first class mail as backup. If the green card comes back signed or the first class isn’t returned undeliverable, that counts as service.
  - If mail fails, the court may assign a constable to attempt personal service (especially if first class comes back undeliverable).
  - If those fail, you may request alias service and perhaps provide a better address.

**Forms for after filing (defendant):**
If you’re the defendant given the **Answer (Form 7)**, fill it out:
  - Admit or deny each part of the claim.
  - List any defense (e.g. “Statute of limitations” or “Paid in full on 1/5/2025”).
  - If counterclaim, write it or attach “Counterclaim (Form 01-CC)” as needed, stating your own claim against plaintiff with amount and reasoning. The court provides instructions in the summons about the 15-day answer deadline ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)).
  - Sign and send to court (and copy the plaintiff).

**Special case forms:**
- **Landlord/Tenant (Summary Possession) forms:** If you were doing an eviction, you’d use **Summons in Summary Possession (Form A)** etc., but that’s a different track – not covered here as we focus on money claims. But note, if you inadvertently file a normal complaint for eviction, the court likely won’t accept it – you’d need to refile on eviction forms. If you’re just suing for rent after tenant left, use normal Complaint Form 1 as a debt action.

**Revisions:** Delaware updates forms occasionally (like Form 50 updated late 2023). Always try to use the latest version from the official site to avoid rejection. The court usually doesn’t reject for minor older format, but best practice is current forms.

In summary, to commence a Delaware small claim, you will generally need:
- JP Civil Form No. 1 (Complaint) – completed with your claim.
- JP Civil Information Sheet (with parties’ contact info).
- If applicable, JP Civil Form 50 (Certificate for non-attorney representation of a corporation/LLC).
- Any supporting docs attached (not mandatory but wise).
Then the court generates Summons and serves it. Defendants then use Form 7 (Answer) and optionally Form 1-CC (Counterclaim) to respond.

## 8. Service of process

After filing your case, you must ensure the defendant(s) are properly **served** with the summons and complaint. In Delaware JP Court, the court clerk typically handles service for you using the address you provide, but ultimately it’s the plaintiff’s responsibility to see it done correctly. Here are Delaware’s rules on service of process in small claims:

**Who can serve:** 
- **Justice of the Peace Court itself** – JP Court will attempt service via certified mail and/or assign a court **constable** to serve the papers[^11]. Constables are officers of the JP Court authorized to deliver summonses within Delaware. 
- **Sheriff or Private Process Server** – Delaware permits service by any **disinterested adult (18+)** or by the County Sheriff. However, in JP Court cases, the default is using court constables or mail. You, as a party, can request to use a special process server (like if the defendant is evasive or out-of-state). If you want a private process server, you must notify the court and arrange it yourself (and pay their fee)[^101]. The court will typically still need proof of service filed (affidavit from that server). 
- **Certified Mail by Clerk:** The common method. The JP Court clerk will send the summons and complaint to defendant via **Certified Mail, restricted delivery (addressee only)**, with **return receipt requested**[^143]. They also send a copy by first-class mail at the same time[^101]. A court clerk or constable technically initiates this, so that counts as service by the court.
- **For out-of-state defendants:** Delaware’s long-arm statute (10 Del. C. § 3104) allows service by **certified mail to the defendant’s out-of-state address** or by serving Delaware’s Secretary of State in some cases. In practice, JP Court will instruct the plaintiff to send the mail. The plaintiff or attorney may receive a packet (Form 31A or instructions) to mail the complaint certified to the out-of-state party and then file an **affidavit of mailing** (JP Civ. Form 31A) along with postal receipts to prove it was done  . Alternatively, the plaintiff can ask a process server in the other state to personally serve the defendant, then file that affidavit (but Delaware law typically uses certified mail for non-residents).
- **Alternate service:** If normal methods fail, you may motion the court for alternative service (e.g., posting on property and mailing, or service by publication in a newspaper). Delaware courts prefer actual notice, so alternative service is a last resort. In JP Court, publication service can be used especially in landlord/tenant evictions or if defendant cannot be found. You’d have to demonstrate diligent attempts were made. For a standard money claim, service by publication is rare but possible if defendant disappeared entirely. It requires a court order.

**Methods of service allowed:**
- **Personal service** – delivering the papers directly to the defendant (or someone of suitable age at their residence). A JP constable can do this. If defendant is an individual, they can leave it with them or with a resident over a certain age (usually 18) at the home (This is **“substituted service”**). For a company, serving a managing agent or officer at the business or registered agent is acceptable.
- **Certified mail (with restricted delivery)** – as noted, Delaware JP often relies on this. “Restricted delivery” means only the addressee (defendant

content.text:
**Who can serve:** In Delaware JP Court, the **court itself usually handles service** of your summons/complaint. When you file, the Clerk of Court issues a summons (JP Civ. Form 2, 3, or 4) and will **attempt service by mail or court constable by default** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_startcivil.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L371%20serve%20,including%20a%20corporation)). So unlike higher courts where plaintiffs often must hire a process server, in JP Court you typically do *not* need to separately hire someone initially. Specifically:
- A court **constable** (an officer of the JP Court) may personally serve the papers ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_startcivil.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L371%20serve%20,including%20a%20corporation)). Constables are authorized to serve within the state.
- The clerk will also send the summons and complaint via **certified mail (restricted delivery)** to the defendant’s address, and by regular first-class mail as a backup ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=by%20his%20or%20her%20employer,to%20withhold%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=After%20the%20request%20for%20garnishment,are%20any%20reasons%20why%20the)). If the certified mail is delivered (green card signed by the defendant or their agent) *or* if the regular mail isn’t returned as undeliverable, Delaware treats it as successful service ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=After%20the%20request%20for%20garnishment,are%20any%20reasons%20why%20the)).
- You **can** request to use a **private process server or the county sheriff** if you prefer or if the court’s attempts fail. By JP Court rule, if you want to hire a special process server, you must notify the court so they don’t duplicate efforts ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_startcivil.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L371%20serve%20,including%20a%20corporation)). The server must be a neutral adult (over 18) and not a party to the case. They will need to file a sworn **Proof of Service** (affidavit) detailing whom they served, when, where.
- **Out-of-state defendants:** Delaware’s “long-arm” statute allows service on non-residents by **certified mail to their out-of-state address** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L77%20Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). JP Court often directs the *plaintiff* to effect service in such cases. For example, the court may issue an instruction for you to send the documents by certified mail and then file an **“Affidavit of Mailing”** (JP Civ. Form 31A) with the postal receipt attached as proof  . Alternatively, Delaware permits serving an out-of-state defendant by serving the **Delaware Secretary of State** as the statutory agent (commonly for non-resident motorists in auto accident cases or foreign businesses with no registered agent). If your case falls under a statute like 10 Del. C. § 3104 (non-resident motorist), you would serve the Sec. of State and then send notice to the defendant by certified mail ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L77%20Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). The JP clerk can guide you on this process. Always check with the court if special procedures apply for an out-of-state party.

**Methods of service allowed:** Delaware law favors personal or mail service:
- **Personal service:** Delivering the summons and complaint directly to the defendant. A constable or private process server can hand it to the defendant at their home or workplace. **Substituted service** is also allowed – e.g., leaving the papers with a **“person of suitable age and discretion”** at the defendant’s dwelling house or usual abode ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_startcivil.aspx#:~:text=serve%20,including%20a%20corporation)) (usually an adult family member or roommate). The constable will then note that on the return (e.g., “served Jane Doe by leaving with John Doe, husband, at residence”). For a business defendant, personal service can be on an officer, managing agent, or the registered agent of the company.
- **Certified mail:** As noted, the court often uses certified mail, restricted delivery (meaning only the named addressee or their authorized agent can sign) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=by%20his%20or%20her%20employer,to%20withhold%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=After%20the%20request%20for%20garnishment,are%20any%20reasons%20why%20the)). If the return receipt (“green card”) comes back signed by the defendant or someone authorized, that’s good service. If the postal notice comes back marked “Refused” or “Unclaimed,” JP Court will not consider that served ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). However, Delaware has a rule that if certified mail is unclaimed but the simultaneous first-class mailing was not returned, the court *may* deem service effective after 15 days ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L86%20,Judge%20and%20the%20other%20party)). (The logic: defendant likely received the regular mail even if they dodged the certified). The summons itself instructs the defendant to respond within 15 days of mailing if served by mail ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)).
- **Posting or publication:** These are last-resort methods. In **landlord/tenant eviction** cases, JP Court commonly allows posting on the rental unit door plus mailing. For a regular civil case, if other methods fail, you can motion the court for service by **publication** (in a newspaper) or **posting** at a last known address. You must show that personal and mail attempts were unsuccessful and that you have no better address. The court may then order publication in an approved newspaper once a week for a set period. Service by publication is rarely used for money claims because Delaware is small and usually defendants can be found, but it’s available if needed (especially if defendant has left the state and no known address). If you must do publication, you’ll cover the cost, and the court will likely schedule a new hearing date allowing time for the notice to run. (There’s no specific rule in JP Court civil for publication, but 10 Del. C. § 3104 permits it if mail fails, and JP Court can follow Superior Court practices under JP Civ Rule 18 if necessary.)

**Timeframes:** If service is done by certified mail, the defendant is considered “served” on the date they sign the receipt or the date regular mail was delivered. The **defendant then has 15 days** (from the date of mailing or service) to file an answer (for most debt/tort cases) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). The summons will specify this deadline. If personally served by a constable, the 15 days likely runs from the personal delivery date. In summary possession (eviction) cases, the timeline is shorter (answer due by trial date which might be a week or two out), but that’s a different track.

Delaware doesn’t require a certain number of days before hearing for service because the hearing isn’t scheduled until after an answer or the answer period passes. So you won’t get ambushed by a hearing before service is done – JP Court waits for service and then either schedules trial (if no answer in 15 days) or, if an answer is filed, will set a trial date and notify parties.

**Proof of service:** If a **constable** serves the papers, they file a **Return of Service** with the court (noting date, time, method). If service was by **mail**, the signed green card or any returned envelope is filed. JP Court uses **Civil Form 9 (Return of Non-Service)** to notify you if service failed ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=you%20will%20receive%20JP%20Civil,served%2C%20you%20will%20need%20to)). For example, if certified mail comes back “Unclaimed” and the first-class letter is returned, the clerk will docket a Form 9 (“Non est” = not served) stating unable to serve and why ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=you%20will%20receive%20JP%20Civil,served%2C%20you%20will%20need%20to)). Likewise, a constable who can’t find the person will file a return “non est inventus” (not found). The court will mail you a copy of that Form 9. At that point, it’s on you to request another attempt or provide a better address.

**What to do if defendant not served (Alias Summons):** If you get notice that service was unsuccessful (no contact, wrong address, defendant dodging mail), you can file a request for an **Alias Summons**. An alias is essentially a second summons issuance. JP Court charges $20 for issuing an alias ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Alias%20,1%2C000%3A%20Filing%20Fee)). You should provide any new address or method for the alias attempt. For example, you discover the defendant moved to a new apartment – give that address. The court will then have a constable try again or send new mail. If the first attempt was via mail, you might ask the next be by personal service, or vice versa. There’s also provision for a **pluries summons** (third attempt) if needed. Each alias or pluries costs $20 and must be requested promptly. Delaware’s rules require diligent effort to serve within a reasonable time – if service drags on for multiple months with no success, the court might eventually dismiss for want of prosecution (but usually they’ll allow quite a while as long as you’re making attempts).

**Service on business entities:** If you’re suing a corporation or LLC, Delaware requires service on either an officer/director, a managing agent at the company’s place of business, or the company’s **registered agent**. Many Delaware companies have a registered agent (often CT Corporation, etc.). If the company is Delaware-registered, serving the registered agent (e.g. via certified mail or constable) is effective. If a company failed to maintain a registered agent, service can be made on the Delaware Secretary of State. JP Court will follow the same principles – if you provide the registered agent’s address, they’ll mail or have constable serve that agent. For out-of-state corporations that did business in Delaware with no agent, Delaware’s long-arm (10 Del. C. § 3104) may allow serving the Sec. of State plus mailing to the corporation’s main office. This is a bit above JP Court’s typical scenario, but it can happen for, say, a foreign contractor who did work in Delaware.

**If defendant evades service:** Delaware law says that **actual notice trumps technicalities**. If you can prove the defendant actually received the papers even if they avoided signing (for example, you have text messages where they acknowledge the lawsuit), you might bring that up at court. But formally, you still need proper service. Some defendants refuse certified mail – one strategy is send via **Certificate of Mailing (regular mail with a certificate from USPS)** in addition to certified; if the regular mail isn’t returned, courts may presume it delivered. The JP Court patterns after Superior Court Civil Rule 4(d)(1) which states certified mail refusal doesn’t defeat service if other evidence shows it was delivered. The JP clerk might automatically do the first-class mailing as mentioned.

**If address is unknown:** It’s the plaintiff’s duty to exercise due diligence to find an address. Search internet, consider hiring a skip-tracer if the sum is worth it. If truly no address can be found, service by publication in a Delaware newspaper (or in last-known locale’s newspaper if out of state) for a few weeks might be permitted upon motion. This is expensive ($hundreds in newspaper fees) and usually a last resort. The court must approve it. It works best when the defendant’s whereabouts are a mystery (e.g. old friend who vanished). If you win by publication default, the judgment might not be easily collectible unless the person resurfaces.

**Service fees:** The initial filing fee covers the first attempt (mail and constable) at service[^11]. If you request an alias, you pay $20[^102] which covers the next attempt. If you decide to use a private server, you pay them separately (could be ~$50-100 depending on difficulty). These costs can be added to your judgment as recoverable costs if you win ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Party%20Action%20%20,%2435)) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Service%20of%20Writ%20of%20Summary,controversy%20equals%20or%20is%20between)). Keep receipts or proof of what you spent on service (e.g. invoice from process server) to present to court.

**After service – proof and next steps:** If service is successful, the defendant has 15 days to respond (for most cases) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). If they don’t, you can seek default (see next section). If they do, the case moves to scheduling a trial. The JP Court will mail both sides a “Notice of Trial” with the date/time once an answer is in or the answer period passes without one ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)). The lead time to trial is often a few weeks to a couple of months. Ensure you or your server file the proof of service with the court docket. In JP eFlex, you can check if the green card came back, etc. The court often waits at least ~20 days after mailing to see if any answer or undeliverable happens.

**If defendant avoids service to delay:** Delaware does have a rule that the statute of limitations is tolled upon filing if service is made within 60 days (or within a time “reasonable” after filing). If someone is actively evading, you can inform the court and they might allow alternate service. The key is to keep the court updated by requesting alias promptly; otherwise, a case not served for 1 year could be dismissed for failure to prosecute (administratively). So do not sit on a non-served case; use those alias tools.

**Summary:** Provide a good address, let JP Court do its thing with certified mail and constables (it works in many cases), and be ready to step in with alias service or private service if necessary. Always confirm that a **Return of Service** is filed – that’s your proof the clock for answer started. If you haven’t seen proof of service after a few weeks, call the clerk to check status.

Remember, you cannot simply hand the papers to the defendant yourself and call it service – as a party, you’re not a disinterested server. Use the court or a third party.

## 9. Defendant’s response and default

Once the defendant is served in a JP Court case, they must **respond within a set time** or risk a default judgment. Here’s how it works in Delaware:

**Time to Answer:** In most JP Court civil cases (debt, contract, tort claims), the defendant has **15 days** from the date of service to file an **Answer** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). The summons will clearly state this deadline. For example, if served by mail, the summons might say: “You are required to serve an answer within 15 days of the date this summons was mailed to you” ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). If served personally by a constable on July 1, the answer is due by July 16. If the 15th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the deadline extends to the next business day.

**How to Answer:** The JP Court provides an **Answer form (JP Civ. Form No. 7)** with the summons ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L314%20Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)). The defendant (now called the “Defendant Below”) should fill it out, stating:
- Which allegations they admit or deny.
- Any defenses they have (e.g. *“I paid this debt on June 5, 2025”*, or *“Statute of limitations has expired”*, or *“Item was damaged due to plaintiff’s fault, not mine.”*).
- **Counterclaim:** If the defendant has a claim against the plaintiff, they can assert it in the same answer form. The form has space for “Counterclaim amount and reason.” If the counterclaim narrative is lengthy, the defendant can attach **Counterclaim Form 1-CC** or a separate sheet ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L524%20figure,the%20filing%20has%20been%20completed)). (We discussed counterclaims in Section 2 – they are permitted up to $25k and must be filed by the answer deadline.)
- The defendant (or their attorney) signs the answer, and files it with the JP Court (by mail, e-Filing, or in person). They should also mail a copy to the plaintiff (the court rules expect the parties to exchange filings).

**No formal “appearance” separate from answer:** In JP Court, filing the answer constitutes the appearance. There is no separate requirement to file a notice of appearance. If the defendant simply wants to **contest the case without a formal written answer**, can they? Technically, the rules expect an answer in writing within 15 days. If the defendant fails to answer in writing but then shows up at the trial, the court *may* allow them to be heard to avoid unjust default (JP Court tends to be forgiving, but it’s risky to rely on that). It’s best to submit the answer timely.

**What happens after an Answer:** The court will review the answer. If a **counterclaim** was filed, the court will typically mail the plaintiff a copy of it along with a **“Notice to Plaintiff of Counterclaim”** instructing them to file a written answer to the counterclaim within (likely) 15 days as well ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Form%20No,Peace%20Court%20without%20an%20attorney)). (The plaintiff, now “Plaintiff Below/Counterclaim Defendant,” should respond to the counterclaim allegations; failure to do so could result in default on the counterclaim). Once any counterclaim answer is in, the case will be scheduled for trial on all claims. If no counterclaim, the filing of the answer triggers the scheduling of a **pre-trial or trial date**. JP Court typically will send a **“Notice of Trial”** with the date, time, location. That notice might come within a few weeks after answer. According to one JP Court instruction, if an answer is filed within 15 days, the court will notify parties of a pre-trial or trial, sometimes a pre-trial telephone conference in debt cases, or a straight trial date ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L86%20,Judge%20and%20the%20other%20party)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Judge%20and%20the%20other%20party)).

**If defendant ignores the lawsuit (no answer):** If the 15-day answer period passes with **no answer filed**, the plaintiff can seek a **default judgment**. Delaware JP Court does not automatically enter default the day after the answer was due – often, the court will:
  - Check if the mailing came back undeliverable (ensuring defendant likely got notice). If mail was unclaimed but not returned, they might delay a bit or require additional steps.
  - If everything seems in order, the court may mail the plaintiff a notice like **“Notice of Eligibility for Default Judgment”** or a scheduling of a “Default Hearing.” In many JP Courts, the **plaintiff must file an affidavit or motion** to get default. For example, the court might send you JP Civ. Form 13 (“Motion and Affidavit to obtain Default Judgment”) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L336%20,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)). This form asks you to swear that the defendant is not an active-duty military member (per the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act) and that the amount is due and owing ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L336%20,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=%28Civil%20Form%20No,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)). The form must be notarized. You may also need to provide an **Affidavit of Non-Military Service** (federal requirement) if not included in Form 13. If the defendant is in the military, default cannot be entered unless certain protections are addressed (they get a court-appointed attorney, etc., under SCRA).
  - If your claim is for a **sum certain** (a fixed or readily calculable amount, like a debt or contract price), the court can enter default judgment administratively upon your affidavits ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L336%20,judgment%20interest%2C%20attorney%E2%80%99s%20fees%20or)). If your claim is unliquidated (e.g. damages for property damage without a clear invoice), the court might schedule a **“default hearing”** where you present proof of damages to a judge, who then determines the award. JP Court default hearings are usually brief; the judge will place you under oath to verify the claim’s basis and amount, perhaps requiring estimates or receipts. The defendant typically isn’t there (since they defaulted).
  - Once satisfied, the court will issue a **Default Judgment** in your favor, specifying the principal sum, court costs, and possibly pre-judgment interest (if you asked for it and proved entitlement). They’ll send a copy of the judgment to both parties.

**Setting aside default (relief from judgment):** If a default judgment is entered, the **defendant has a limited time to ask the court to vacate (open) that judgment**. In JP Court, a **Motion to Vacate Default Judgment** generally must be filed **within 15 days** of the mailing of the judgment notice (or 30 days if service was by certified mail and the defendant never appeared) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=In%20most%20cases%2C%20a%20party,In%20seeking%20to%20vacate%20a)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=entry%20of%20the%20default%20judgment%2C,In%20seeking%20to%20vacate%20a)). The defendant should use JP Civ. Form 13 (the same number form, but it can be used by either party for vacating different judgments – the context differs) or simply a written motion, stating the reasons for not responding and any meritorious defense ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=In%20most%20cases%2C%20a%20party,In%20seeking%20to%20vacate%20a)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=vacate%20a%20non,or%20if%20service%20was%20made)). Acceptable reasons might be: lack of proper service, excusable neglect (e.g., “I was in the hospital and couldn’t answer”), or that they never received notice due to address issues. The motion to vacate will be set for a brief hearing in JP Court; if granted, the case is reopened and both sides get to argue on the merits at a new trial date. If the motion is filed after the 15-day window, JP Court may lack jurisdiction to reopen (i.e., they become stricter). However, Delaware courts also recognize that if a judgment is void (for lack of jurisdiction or service), it can be vacated at any time on motion. So if service was defective, the defendant can challenge the judgment well beyond 15 days as “void.” But for things like “I forgot to answer,” the 15-day rule is enforced. It’s short – compare to Superior Court which allows 1 year for Rule 60(b) motions – JP Court is much shorter by rule.

**If plaintiff doesn’t show or prosecute:** Note, if the plaintiff fails to appear at a scheduled trial, the case can be dismissed as a “nonsuit” (essentially default against the plaintiff). The plaintiff then similarly has 15 days to file a Motion to Vacate Nonsuit Judgment (essentially to reopen) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=obtain%20the%20judgment,10%20days%20of%20its%20entry)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=vacate%20a%20non,or%20if%20service%20was%20made)). If they miss that, the case is considered dismissed with prejudice (though sometimes nonsuit is without prejudice, but JP Court rules treat it akin to a final judgment unless vacated). So both sides need to attend or timely move to reopen.

**Partial default – Counterclaims:** If a plaintiff’s claim is answered but the plaintiff then doesn’t answer a counterclaim, the counterclaim can go default. For example, defendant files a $500 counterclaim and plaintiff fails to reply within 15 days, the court could award the counterclaim by default. So plaintiffs must remember to answer counterclaims just like defendants answer complaints. The deadline for a counterclaim answer is typically 15 days from when notice of the counterclaim is mailed to the plaintiff (JP Court often includes that instruction).

**Judgment by agreement:** Sometimes a defendant might not want to formally contest but also not want a default on record. They can reach out to plaintiff to settle or agree on a judgment. In JP Court, the parties can file a **Consent Judgment** (both sign off on an agreed amount). The court then enters that as a judgment. Or they can **stipulate to dismissal** if resolved. If a payment plan is desired, parties can also put that in writing and ask the court to approve it as a **“stipulated judgment”** which activates if payments aren’t made. Absent a stipulation, the court generally expects full payment of a judgment immediately (though the court can grant a **stay of execution** or payment plan for good cause, see Post-judgment section).

**Key points for defendants:** Do *not* ignore a JP summons. The 15-day window is short. If you need more time, you can reach out to the court within that period to request an extension for filing an answer – JP Courts may grant a brief extension if there’s a good reason and the plaintiff doesn’t object, but there’s no guarantee. There is no automatic discovery process in JP Court, so the answer is your main chance to lay out defenses. You can, however, use the **Demand for Bill of Particulars** if the complaint is vague, which effectively pauses the requirement to answer until you get more detail ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L471%20Form%20No,%28For%20Plaintiffs%20that%20are)). If a Bill of Particulars is demanded, the plaintiff must furnish a detailed statement of claim (Form 10BP, etc.), and then the 15-day clock to answer begins after service of that Bill.

**If neither side responds (inactive case):** If somehow service was good but defendant doesn’t answer *and* plaintiff doesn’t move for default, the case might sit idle. Delaware’s civil procedure allows dismissal for want of prosecution if a case is inactive for a long period. JP Court might send a notice after a few months of no action, warning that the case may be dismissed if plaintiff doesn’t proceed. So plaintiffs should not delay seeking default once eligible.

In summary, **defendants should answer within 15 days** to avoid default; **plaintiffs should vigilantly pursue default** after 15 days if no answer is received (including filing any required affidavits). Default judgments can be opened only on a quick timeline and with good reason, so it’s much better to participate on time than to try undoing a default later.

## 10. Hearing procedure

If the case is contested (the defendant filed an answer denying liability, and/or the plaintiff is pursuing a counterclaim), the JP Court will schedule a **hearing** (trial). Here’s what to expect in the Delaware small claims hearing process:

**Type of hearing – bench trial:** Small claims cases in Delaware JP Court are heard by a **judge (Justice of the Peace)**, not a jury. There is no option for a jury trial in JP Court; if a jury trial is desired, a party would have to remove the case to a higher court (and in debt/trespass actions under $50k, even Superior Court in Delaware doesn’t offer jury – but Court of Common Pleas does for certain cases). So, you will have a **bench trial**, meaning the judge is the fact-finder and decision-maker.

**Who presides – judge or magistrate:** In JP Court, the judicial officer is called a Justice of the Peace. They may or may not be lawyers by background (JP judges in Delaware are not required to be attorneys; many are lay judges who undergo training). But they have full authority to conduct the trial, rule on evidence, and render judgment. Occasionally, a court might use a “volunteer magistrate” for mediation or preliminary matters, but the final trial will be before the JP judge assigned.

**Informality and rules of evidence:** JP Court trials are relatively informal compared to higher courts. By rule, they are not strictly bound by the Delaware Rules of Evidence if both parties are self-represented – the judge can relax evidentiary rules “to assure a fair hearing” ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_typeclaim.aspx#:~:text=A%20civil%20trespass%20action%20involves,letter%20attached%20to%20the%20complaint)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_typeclaim.aspx#:~:text=Please%20note%20that%20the%20Justice,personal%20injuries%2C%20mental%20anguish%2C%20etc)). This means the judge might allow hearsay or unauthenticated documents if they appear reliable and neither side objects strongly. However, **do not assume anything goes** – it’s still wise to bring proper evidence. If one party is represented by an attorney and the other isn’t, the judge might still be lenient for the pro se side. The key is the judge’s discretion to balance fairness. You should be prepared to explain the relevance and source of any evidence you present. 

**Length of hearing:** Most JP Court hearings are scheduled in small time blocks (often 30 minutes to 1 hour). Complex cases might get a bit longer (maybe 2 hours if it’s a multi-witness property damage case). Because time is limited, come organized and focused on the main points. The court might schedule multiple small claims on the same morning or afternoon docket. So you might wait a bit if earlier cases run long. Plan to be available for a couple of hours, though your case might only take 20-30 minutes of actual hearing time if straightforward.

**Opening and procedure:** When your case is called, both parties (and any witnesses) will be sworn in. The judge will usually ask the **plaintiff** to present their case first (since plaintiff bears the burden of proof). You’ll present evidence and witnesses. Then the **defendant** can ask questions (cross-examination) of the plaintiff or their witnesses. Then the defendant presents their side (defenses and any counterclaim), and the plaintiff can cross-examine. The judge may interject with questions at any time – JP judges often ask direct questions to clarify facts because the process is informal.

There isn’t a strict formal opening statement or closing argument phase like higher courts, but the judge might ask each side for a brief summary or any final comment. Use that opportunity to emphasize key points and what relief you seek (e.g., “Your Honor, I’m asking for judgment of $2,000 plus costs, due to the unpaid loan and $50 bad-check fee under the statute”).

**Burden of proof:** The plaintiff must prove their claim by a **preponderance of the evidence** (more likely than not) for civil cases. For counterclaims, the counter-claimant (defendant) has the burden on those. The judge will weigh evidence and credibility to see who has the stronger case. If it’s a tie or too close to call, the one with the burden loses (so if evidence is evenly balanced, the defendant wins on the main claim, or conversely on a counterclaim the plaintiff would win if counter evidence is equal). Preponderance is a low bar compared to criminal beyond reasonable doubt; essentially tip the scales just over 50%.

**What evidence to bring:** **All relevant evidence that supports your story and damages.** Since no formal discovery is done in JP Court, the hearing is your one shot to introduce documents or other proof.
- **Documents:** contracts, receipts, invoices, canceled checks, emails, text message printouts, photographs, repair estimates, bank statements, etc. Bring **two copies** of each document (one for the judge, one for the opponent) plus the original if available (the judge might want to see an original signature or clearer print). For electronic evidence like texts or emails, print them out with timestamps and relevant sender info. The court typically will accept printouts or even viewing on a phone if necessary, but printed is better. Delaware allows business records to be admitted often without a formal custodian if they appear authentic (especially in small claims), but if the other side objects to authenticity, the judge will rule on reasonableness. Tip: organize documents in chronological order or labeled by issue for easy reference. You can also prepare a simple list of exhibits for yourself to ensure you don’t forget any.
- **Witnesses:** Eye-witnesses or expert witnesses can be critical. If someone observed the relevant events (e.g., a friend who heard the loan agreement, or a mechanic who inspected faulty work), ask them to testify. If they are unwilling or can’t come voluntarily, you can subpoena them. **JP Court Subpoena** (JP Civ. Form 30) can be obtained from the clerk and served (with a $10 fee) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Service%20of%20Writ%20of%20Summary,controversy%20equals%20or%20is%20between)). It must be served at least a few days in advance (no specific rule given, but give reasonable notice). Witnesses should have first-hand knowledge; “character witnesses” or people who only repeat what you told them (“hearsay”) are less useful. Delaware JP judges often allow hearsay if not contested (e.g., a letter from a mechanic who could not attend), but if the other side objects, the judge might give it less weight or exclude it. **Tip:** If an essential witness cannot attend in person (say, an out-of-state witness), ask the court in advance if they permit testimony by phone or Zoom. JP Courts sometimes allow telephonic testimony for convenience – you’d typically file a motion or call the clerk to request permission. Especially post-2020, courts have become more flexible with remote appearances for witnesses. If allowed, make sure that witness is sworn in over the phone and speaks clearly.
- **Expert witnesses:** In small claims, hiring a paid expert (like an engineer or accountant) can be expensive and is rare. But if your case warrants it (e.g. suing a contractor for structural defects – an expert report or testimony could make the difference), you may do so. Delaware doesn’t require expert affidavits or disclosures in JP Court, but courtesy suggests you inform the other side if you plan to use an expert. Alternatively, **written reports or estimates** from professionals, while technically hearsay, are often accepted if the other side doesn’t object or if getting the person to court is overly burdensome relative to the claim. Example: a written car repair estimate from a mechanic – judges often accept that as evidence of damage cost if it seems legit and the other side has no contrary evidence. If the other party objects, the judge might demand the mechanic testify or might still allow it under relaxed rules. It’s case-by-case.
- **Photos/videos:** Visual evidence is great for property damage, conditions of rental, etc. Bring printed color photos (the judge may keep them as exhibits). Ensure you can testify to when and what the photo depicts. Videos can be shown if you have something to play them (some JP courtrooms might have DVD players or allow you to show on a laptop). Check with the clerk ahead of time if special equipment is needed. Always have a backup like still frames or a written transcript of key parts in case technology fails.
- **Denials/Defenses:** If you’re a defendant, bring anything that disproves the claim: receipts showing payment, correspondence where the plaintiff acknowledged satisfaction, witnesses who can alibi or counter the plaintiff’s story. Also, if you have legal defenses (statute of limitations, etc.), be prepared to articulate them and perhaps provide any supporting law or documents (e.g., proof of last payment date to show SOL expired). JP judges do know the basics like SOL durations, but it never hurts to have a copy of the code section to reference.

**Language and interpreter:** If you or a witness are not fluent in English, you can request a court interpreter **in advance** (for free). Delaware Courts provide interpreters for many languages if notified. It’s important to notify the JP Court as early as possible (ideally when filing the case or answer, or at least a few weeks before trial) so they can schedule an interpreter. If a party appears without having requested an interpreter and can’t understand proceedings, the judge will likely postpone the trial and get one – which delays resolution. So plan ahead. Sign language interpreters for deaf or hard-of-hearing parties are also available and should be requested beforehand.

**Representation by attorneys:** Delaware JP Court *allows* lawyers to represent parties (unlike some states’ small claims where attorneys are barred). If one party has an attorney and the other doesn’t, the hearing still proceeds, but the judge may be a bit more permissive with the unrepresented side to ensure fairness. The presence of an attorney doesn’t change evidentiary rules formally, but practically, the attorney will object to inadmissible evidence, etc., and the judge will rule. Non-lawyers might not know to make such objections. The judge, however, can ask questions to get needed facts even if a party doesn’t present them clearly. Both parties (or counsel) will have the chance to speak, present evidence, ask questions, and make a closing argument if they wish. Keep your arguments concise and focused on the facts and law that support your case. If you have an attorney, they will handle all this for you – you may only need to testify when called.

**Subpoenas:** As mentioned, you have the right to subpoena witnesses (Form 30) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Service%20of%20Writ%20of%20Summary,controversy%20equals%20or%20is%20between)) and documents (JP Court might allow a subpoena duces tecum for someone to bring documents). Subpoenas should be served at least several days prior. The $10 fee and service cost (if any) can be added to costs. If a witness is subpoenaed and doesn’t show, the judge can issue a **capias** (a bench warrant) or continue the case. In practice, for small claims, judges might not issue immediate warrants but could penalize if key subpoenaed witness ignores it (possibly hold in contempt). It’s rare to go that far in minor cases, but the power exists.

**Courtroom etiquette:** Dress neatly (business casual is fine; suits not required, but avoid shorts/flip-flops or graphic t-shirts). Address the judge as “Your Honor.” Don’t interrupt the judge or the other party; you’ll get your turn. Delaware JP courtrooms are small and usually only a few people present. Stand when speaking to the judge (some JPs might say you can remain seated, but standing is respectful when making your initial presentation or when judge enters). Keep your tone respectful, even if the other side gets heated. The judge may ask questions in a conversational style – answer directly and honestly. 

**Presenting your case:** Generally:
- The **plaintiff** goes first, as they have the burden. Outline the story chronologically: what agreement or duty existed, how the defendant breached it or caused harm, and what loss resulted. Reference your evidence as you go (“As you can see in Exhibit 1, that’s the signed contract where he agreed to pay by June.”). The judge may or may not let you narrative at length; some will cut in to clarify points. Be prepared for questions at any time.
- Then the defendant (or their attorney) can **cross-examine** plaintiff’s witnesses. In small court, this might be less formal: the judge might simply turn to defendant and say, “What would you like to ask or say in response?” The defendant can then ask the plaintiff questions or directly explain their side (judges sometimes blend cross-exam and testimony for pro se litigants).
- Next, the **defendant presents their case** (defenses and any counterclaim). They can call witnesses and introduce exhibits. The plaintiff (or their counsel) can cross-examine those witnesses and challenge evidence.
- After that, the judge often asks each side if they have **anything further to add or any additional evidence**. This is a good time to mention any small point overlooked or to clarify damages (e.g., “I’d also like to claim the $35 court fee as costs, Your Honor.” The judge will include costs automatically if you win, but no harm noting it).
- **Closing statements:** Some JP judges explicitly invite each side to make a closing argument summarizing why they should win. Others might simply move to decision or say, “I have what I need, thank you.” If you’re given a chance to close, keep it brief (1-3 minutes). Emphasize how the evidence met each element of your claim or defense. For example: “Your Honor, the evidence shows a valid contract (Exhibit 1), defendant’s non-payment (his own admission he didn’t pay), and thus a breach that cost me $2,000.
