There’s no credible defense, so I respectfully ask for judgment in that amount plus statutory interest.” Or from defendant: “Your Honor, I believe plaintiff did not meet their burden. They have no signed agreement – just an alleged conversation – and I testified I never agreed to pay. Therefore, no contract was proven. Also, the claim is filed after the 3-year limitations period (the loan was from 2019), which I raised as a defense. On those grounds, I ask you to rule for the defendant.”

**Judge’s decision:** The JP judge may announce a decision immediately from the bench or may take it “under advisement” and mail a decision later. In many straightforward cases, the judge will state the judgment right after hearing both sides. They might say: “I find in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of $__, plus court costs and post-judgment interest. I do not award the additional repair cost because that was not sufficiently proven,” for instance. Or, “Judgment for defendant – plaintiff did not carry his burden.” Take notes on the judge’s ruling if delivered orally. A written **Judgment Order** will be entered on the docket and mailed to each party regardless. If the judge wants to think about it or research a legal point, they might say, “I’ll reserve decision and issue a written judgment in a few days.” That’s not uncommon in more complex disputes. 

The judgment will specify who must pay whom, how much, and it will add the court costs (filing fee, etc.) and any interest if applicable. The losing party is expected to pay voluntarily. If they don’t, the winning party can proceed to **post-judgment collection** (see Section 12).

**Attendance:** It is crucial to attend the hearing. If plaintiff doesn’t show, likely a nonsuit/dismissal results. If defendant doesn’t show (and they didn’t answer or even if they did), the court can do a default judgment either on the spot or by later default process. Sometimes if a defendant answered but fails to appear at trial, the judge may proceed with the trial **in absentia** – basically, the plaintiff still presents evidence to ensure a prima facie case, and then the judge rules (often in plaintiff’s favor by default since no defense is presented). That is effectively a default judgment, but some judges like to have some record of proof rather than pure default.

**Continuances/Postponements:** If a party needs a continuance of the trial date (due to emergency, schedule conflict, or needing more time to gather evidence), they should file a **Motion for Continuance** as soon as the conflict is known. JP Courts will grant continuances for good cause, especially first requests. If, for example, you realize a key witness can’t make that date, file a motion (writing a letter to the court typically suffices, or a formal JP motion form if available) stating the reason and proposing alternative dates. If the other side objects, the judge will weigh prejudice. Try to avoid last-minute requests (like the day of trial) as judges may deny those absent truly exigent circumstances (e.g., sudden illness). Each party might get one free continuance if requested in advance; multiple requests raise suspicion of stalling.

**Settlement at hearing:** If the parties settle the case in the hallway just before the hearing, inform the court. They can mark the case as settled and either dismiss it or enter a consent judgment if you prefer an enforceable judgment. Delaware encourages settlement; sometimes a **court mediator** may be present at the courthouse offer to mediate. In New Castle County, for example, JP Court often has volunteer mediators on site. If both parties agree, you might sit with a mediator to attempt a resolution. Mediation is confidential and if it fails, you still get your trial. If it succeeds, you sign a settlement agreement (which can be turned into a consent judgment or simply cause a voluntary dismissal if payment is immediate). This process is voluntary – you can decline mediation if you want the judge to decide.

**Behavior and Pitfalls:** Common pitfalls at hearing include:
- **Not bringing evidence**: e.g., plaintiff says “I don’t have the receipt with me” or defendant says “the witness couldn’t get off work.” This can sink your case. Judges decide on evidence presented, not just your say-so. If you find you forgot something crucial, you can ask the judge for a **continuance of the trial** to gather it – but don’t count on it. Better to come fully prepared.
- **Getting emotional or arguing with the other party directly:** Always direct your statements to the judge, not the opponent. Don’t interrupt the other side during their turn; wait and then point out discrepancies calmly. Personal attacks or venting anger won’t help your case and may annoy the judge.
- **Failing to object or address evidence:** If the other side introduces evidence that you believe is false or improper (like pure hearsay), you can object. In an informal setting, you might say, “Your Honor, I object – that’s a letter from someone who isn’t here to verify it.” The judge will rule to either consider it or not. Even if you don’t formally object, you can in your turn argue “This letter should carry little weight because the author didn’t testify and wasn’t cross-examined.” The judge will factor that in.
- **Not making your legal points known:** The judge knows general law, but if you have a specific statute or case supporting your position (like a limitations statute or consumer law), mention it or bring a print-out. For instance, “Under 6 Del. C. § 1301A, I’m entitled to $50 extra for this bad check ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title6/c013a/index.html#:~:text=,the%20amount%20of%20the%20check)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title6/c013a/index.html#:~:text=costs%2C%20damages%20in%20the%20amount,plus%20court%20costs%2C%20provided%20that)).” The judge should know that if you pleaded it, but raising it at hearing reinforces your claim.
- **Time management:** With limited time, focus on key facts. Don’t spend 10 minutes on irrelevant background. Judges often appreciate a concise, structured presentation: “There are 3 key issues, Your Honor: first, was there a contract – here’s evidence there was; second, did defendant breach – here’s proof of non-performance; third, damages – here’s how I calculated them.” This helps ensure you cover everything important before time runs low.
- **Challenging the judge:** If the judge rules against an objection or seems skeptical, don’t argue with the judge. You can respectfully clarify (“Understood, Your Honor – for the record, I maintain that...”). But ultimately, the judge’s evidentiary rulings stand. Save legal disagreements for a possible appeal, rather than trying to re-litigate with the judge mid-hearing.

**After the hearing:** The judge will issue a judgment as discussed. If you win, congrats – you become a “Judgment Creditor” and can proceed to collection if not paid (next section). If you lose, you have the option to **appeal** to the Court of Common Pleas within **15 days** of the judgment (Section 11 covers appeals).

In sum, the hearing is your day in court – be prepared, be polite, stick to facts and relevant law, and you’ll maximize your chance of success.

## 11. Appeals

If you are unhappy with the outcome in JP Court, you may have a right to **appeal** to a higher court. In Delaware, appeals from JP Court civil cases go to the **Court of Common Pleas (CCP)**, except in certain landlord/tenant possession cases (those go to a special 3-judge JP Court panel) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=All%20decisions%20from%20the%20Justice,3%28a)). Here’s what to know about appeals:

**Who can appeal:** Either party – plaintiff or defendant – can appeal a final judgment in a JP Court civil case (except default judgments have some special rules). Notably, **if a default judgment was entered** and the defendant didn’t participate, that defendant has the option to either:
  - File a motion to vacate in JP Court within 15 days (as discussed above) or
  - Appeal to CCP within 15 days. However, CCP might dismiss an appeal of a default if the defendant didn’t first try to vacate in JP (sometimes higher courts expect you to exhaust the easier remedy). It’s often better to move to vacate in JP first. If that fails, the defendant can still appeal the default to CCP, but they may have to show excusable neglect to CCP as well. On the flip side, a plaintiff who obtained a default cannot appeal their own win (they won!). If a plaintiff thinks the award was too small (e.g. judge didn’t give all damages), generally only a **defendant** can appeal a default judgment (because plaintiff “won” albeit not fully). Plaintiffs typically cannot appeal a judgment in their favor to seek more money – Delaware doesn’t let you appeal just the amount if you won on liability (except maybe in CCP itself or Superior, but not from JP to CCP). In JP appeals, *any final judgment* can be appealed by the “aggrieved” party (the one who lost or is dissatisfied).

**Deadline to appeal:** **15 days** from the date of the JP Court judgment (not from when you receive it, but from the judgment’s entry date) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)). This is jurisdictional – a late appeal will be dismissed. So mark that date. The JP Court judgment notice itself often says “You have 15 days to appeal to CCP.” If the 15th day falls on a weekend or holiday, you get the next business day. There is no extension for mail delays – you must file by then.

**How to appeal:** You must file a **Notice of Appeal** with the Court of Common Pleas in the county where the JP case was heard ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=landlord%2Ftenant%20possession%20cases%2C%20may%20be,3%28a)). There is a specific form or format; CCP provides an “Appeal from JP Court” packet. Steps:
  1. Go to the CCP Prothonotary (clerk’s office) and file a *Notice of Appeal*. Include the JP Court case caption and number, your name as appellant and other party as appellee, and a statement that you appeal from the judgment of JP Court No.__ in case ____. 
  2. Pay the **appeal filing fee**. As of recently, CCP’s fee is around $135 (which includes a $125 filing fee + security assessment) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=At%20the%20same%20time%2C%20or,days%2C%20the%20Plaintiff%20Below%20must)). If you cannot afford it, you could ask CCP for an IFP status (fee waiver) – that may be harder at appellate level, but possible.
  3. **Serve notice on the other party.** The rules require the appellant to send a copy of the notice of appeal to the other party (appellee) within 10 days of filing the appeal ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L64%20,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). You then file proof of that (a certificate of mailing) with CCP.
  4. CCP will also require a **copy of the JP Court record**. Usually, the appellant must file a **praecipe (request)** for the JP Court to send up the record (transcript) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). Delaware has a specific requirement: within 10 days of filing the appeal, the appellant should file a copy of the Notice of Appeal with the JP Court that issued the judgment ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L64%20,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). The JP Court will then transmit the record to CCP.
  
Delaware’s Court of Common Pleas Civil Rule 72.3 governs these appeals. It states:
- The appellant must, within 10 days of filing the appeal in CCP, **file a copy of the Notice of Appeal with the JP Court** and pay a $10 transcription fee to JP Court ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L64%20,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). This essentially orders the JP Court to prepare the record.
- If appellant fails to do these steps (notifying JP Court or paying fees), the appeal can be dismissed.

The JP Court will send the case file and any electronic recording of the trial to CCP. If the JP trial was recorded (some are digital audio recorded), that recording serves as the transcript. Often, in appeals, the CCP doesn’t rely on the JP record because appeals are **de novo** (see below), but the rule still requires transferring the record in case needed for reference (especially if a legal issue arises about JP proceedings).

**Type of appeal – trial de novo:** An appeal of a JP Court civil case to CCP is a **“trial de novo”** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=landlord%2Ftenant%20cases%20MUST%20be%20appealed,3%28a)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=All%20decisions%20from%20the%20Justice,the%20case%20starts%20over%20in)). This means the case is heard fresh in the Court of Common Pleas as if it had not been tried before. In other words, the CCP does not just review the JP Court for errors; it allows the parties to present evidence and arguments anew before a CCP judge. You get a brand-new trial at the higher court:
- You can (and should) bring all your witnesses and evidence again.
- The CCP judge will not defer to the JP judge’s findings – they decide afresh on facts and law.
- New evidence that wasn’t presented in JP can be introduced (generally yes – de novo means you’re not limited to the old record, though practically you should have presented your best evidence in JP; but if you didn’t, you can still bring it in CCP).
- The pleadings can be expanded: typically the appellant will file a new complaint or answer in the CCP action. Actually, CCP Civil Rule 72.3 says the appellant (if plaintiff below lost, they can file a complaint anew; if defendant below lost, their notice of appeal often acts as a complaint in the sense they’re seeking reversal). In practice, the CCP often treats the papers from JP as initial pleadings. However, the **appellee must file an answer in CCP**. The CCP might issue a scheduling order for discovery, etc., making it more formal.

**No increased claim amount:** Note that if the plaintiff below appeals, they generally cannot increase the amount of their claim on appeal. The appeal is for the same cause of action. For instance, if you sued for $5,000 in JP and got $0, you can’t turn around and sue for $7,000 in CCP; you’re limited to what was in controversy below. However, sometimes if time has passed and damages continued (like additional rent accruing), CCP might allow an amendment to include those; but typically, it’s the same claim.

**Jury trial on appeal:** In the Court of Common Pleas, parties have the right to request a **jury trial** in civil cases (for matters exceeding $2,000) *except* that CCP’s jurisdiction maximum is $75k and they allow juries in most cases unless waived. On appeal, since it’s de novo, either party can demand a jury trial in CCP (assuming the claim is of a type triable by jury – contract and tort claims qualify). To get a jury in CCP, a party must file a **jury demand with the appeal or answer** (within 10 days of the appeal docketing, per CCP Rule 38). If neither asks for a jury, the trial in CCP will be a bench trial before a CCP judge. Many small claims appeals proceed without jury because of the added cost and time of jury trial (and attorneys are usually involved by then).

**Bond or supersedeas:** Filing an appeal **does not automatically stay** enforcement of the JP Court judgment, unless a **bond** is posted. This is important: if you’re a defendant who lost and you appeal, the plaintiff can usually still attempt to collect (wage attach, etc.) while appeal is pending unless you get a stay. To get a stay, the appellant can file a **supersedeas bond** in JP Court (or CCP) to cover the judgment amount and costs ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4,must%20file%20a%20motion%20with)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4.%20Stay%20of%20Execution%2023,of%20the%20Peace%20Court%20Debt)). JP Court Rule 20 says a bond equal to judgment + costs + maybe interest is needed for supersedeas. In practice, CCP Rule 72.3(b) references that to stop execution during appeal, appellant must file a motion in CCP for stay and likely post bond as CCP directs ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4,must%20file%20a%20motion%20with)). So if you’re a judgment debtor appealing, be proactive: either negotiate with appellee to hold off, or file a motion to stay execution when you appeal, and be ready to put up a bond or cash deposit. If you don’t, the appellee can proceed to enforce the judgment. If they succeed in collecting and then you win the appeal, obviously the money would have to be returned, but that can be messy. So better to secure a stay. The CCP typically requires a bond for the full judgment amount plus perhaps 15% to cover interests and costs (similar to Superior Court rules). If you can’t afford a bond, you can request the court to approve an alternate security or to waive due to indigence, but waiver is unlikely if it prejudices the appellee’s ability to recover later.

**Procedure in CCP:** After the appeal is docketed, it essentially becomes a new case in CCP. The CCP will assign a new case number and the title will be “John Doe (Appellant) vs. Jane Smith (Appellee)”. The appellant (if plaintiff below) might file a fresh complaint elaborating their claim. The appellee (if defendant below) will then answer (and can assert counterclaims if they had any – likely they'd have done in JP already). If the appellant was defendant below, effectively they should file a complaint (or the Notice of Appeal can serve as one) explaining why they seek relief (usually that the JP judgment was wrong and they owe $0 or have a counterclaim for $X). The roles can flip – e.g., if defendant appeals, they become “plaintiff” in CCP with a complaint that might say “I seek judgment in my favor, reversing the JP decision, plus costs” and reassert any counterclaim they had.

The case then proceeds like a normal CCP case: there may be an opportunity for **discovery** (exchange of documents, depositions) if needed. CCP may issue a scheduling order for small claims appeals fairly quickly. Often, because amounts are small and evidence is straightforward (already presented in JP), parties may choose not to do extensive discovery. But the right exists – e.g. you could send interrogatories to the other side. Many small appeals still involve pro se parties, though often if a party went to the trouble of appealing, they might hire counsel for CCP. 

**Hearing at CCP:** At the CCP trial, it’s essentially a (possibly more formal) re-run of the case. Each side presents witnesses and evidence to the CCP judge or jury. The CCP will not consider what the JP judge ruled, except maybe if there’s an evidentiary dispute (like, if there’s an objection about SOL, the CCP judge decides it anew). 

**Outcome of appeal:** The Court of Common Pleas will issue its judgment. That judgment **supersedes** the JP Court judgment. For example:
- If JP Court ruled for plaintiff $5,000, and on appeal the CCP rules for defendant, the CCP judgment (perhaps “in favor of defendant, plaintiff takes nothing”) replaces the JP one. The plaintiff can no longer enforce the JP judgment (and if they collected during appeal, they must refund it).
- If CCP rules for plaintiff but for a different amount (say JP gave $0, CCP gives $3,000 to plaintiff), then the new amount is what counts.
- If CCP returns essentially the same result as JP, it just confirms it with a new judgment. 

Once the CCP judgment is entered, the losing party in CCP (now called appellant/appellee accordingly) has further appeal rights: they can appeal the CCP judgment to the **Delaware Superior Court**, but *not* for a new trial – that appeal would be **on the record** (meaning the Superior Court reviews for legal errors, not factual re-determination). And some small claims might not reach Superior because of amount-in-controversy limits (though I believe any CCP final order can be appealed on the record to Superior, regardless of amount, as long as procedure was proper – but Superior’s scope is limited and they often defer heavily to CCP on factual issues).

However, practically, very few small claims go beyond one round of appeal because of the costs involved relative to the claim.

**Bond release or execution after appeal:** If the appellant posted a supersedeas bond and they lose the appeal, the bond will be used to satisfy the judgment (the appellee can move to have the bond funds released to them to cover the judgment). If the appellant wins the appeal, any bond is released back to them. Also, any execution efforts from the JP judgment should cease. Actually, when an appeal is noted (and especially if a supersedeas is granted), the JP Court judgment is stayed. If the appellee had levied property during the appeal and then loses, they must undo that (e.g., return any seized property or money).

**Appeal example timeline:** 
- JP trial Jan 10 → judgment Jan 10.
- Appeal filed Jan 20 in CCP (10 days later, within 15-day window).
- Notice mailed to other side by Jan 25 (within 5 days of appeal).
- Appellant files copy in JP Court by Jan 30 and pays $10 (within 10 days) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=At%20the%20same%20time%2C%20or,days%2C%20the%20Plaintiff%20Below%20must)).
- JP Court transmits record in Feb.
- CCP schedules trial for, say, May.
- Meanwhile, appellant posts a bond to stay enforcement.
- CCP trial May 5 → new judgment entered May 5.
- If further appeal: Notice to Superior Court within 30 days (CCP to Superior appeals are 30 days).

**Important:** The **15-day appeal window is strict** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)). If you miss it, JP Court’s decision becomes final and binding. There is no right to appeal after 15 days (Superior Court can’t hear it because CCP never got it in time). The only potential remedy for a missed appeal might be if you can reopen in JP for some extraordinary reason (JP Court rarely can reopen after 15 days unless judgment is void). So diarize that date.

**Appeals of specific cases:**
- Landlord/tenant possession (eviction) appeals must be filed in **5 days**, not 15, and they go to a special 3-JP judge panel ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=landlord%2Ftenant%20possession%20cases%2C%20may%20be,3%28a)) (except for rent money judgment portion, which could go to CCP). That’s a unique scenario beyond our main scope, but worth noting if someone tried to appeal an eviction, it’s a different process.
- Some JP judgments (like in favor of State in criminal fines or something) might not be appealable, but civil ones are.

In short, **an appeal gives you a “do-over” in a higher court**, but be mindful of timing, fees, and the fact that the judgment can be enforced unless you secure a stay. Many parties choose not to appeal minor sums due to cost; but if the principle or amount justifies, Delaware provides that second chance, culminating in CCP’s final say.

## 12. Post-judgment collection

Winning a judgment is a victory, but collecting the money is often the real challenge. Delaware JP Court doesn’t collect the money for you; it provides legal tools for you (the judgment creditor) to enforce the judgment against the debtor’s assets or income. A Delaware small claims judgment is enforceable for **5 years** from its entry (and can be renewed for 5-year increments) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). Here are the main collection methods and rules:

**Transcription (“abstract”) of judgment / lien on real property:** 
A JP Court judgment, by itself, is not an automatic lien on the debtor’s real estate. However, Delaware law allows you to “transfer” or “transcribe” a JP judgment to the **Superior Court** Prothonotary to create a lien on real property ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=entries%20of%20the%20judgment%20with,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=entries%20of%20the%20judgment%20with,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)). The process: after 30 days from judgment (when appeal period over), you can request a **Certified Transcript of Judgment** from JP Court (Fee $10) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Service%20of%20Writ%20of%20Summary,controversy%20equals%20or%20is%20between)). Then you file that transcript in the Superior Court of any county where the debtor owns (or may own) real estate. Under 10 Del. C. § 4781 et seq., a judgment filed in Superior Court acts as a lien on any real property of the debtor in that county from the date of filing ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=entries%20of%20the%20judgment%20with,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=county%20in%20which%20property%20of,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)). The lien lasts 5 years (because Superior Court judgments last 5 years as liens and can be renewed up to 20 years total) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=entries%20of%20the%20judgment%20with,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). This means if the debtor tries to sell or refinance property, they must pay off the judgment. 
  - Example: You win a $5,000 JP judgment. Debtor owns a house in New Castle County. You get a transcript and file it with Superior Court in New Castle. Now there’s a lien. If the debtor sells the house, the lien must be satisfied from proceeds at closing. You can also potentially force a sale (see **levy** below) but that’s rare for small amounts.
  - Filing the transcript in Superior Court requires a prothonotary fee (around $25–$35) and you’ll get a Superior Court judgment number. This does not move the case to Superior for enforcement; it just makes it into a lien of record. You still execute via JP mechanisms unless you fully “transfer” the case to Superior execution (which usually likely not allowed since amount is within CCP/JP realm – but transcription for lien is specifically allowed by statute).
  - **Note:** Transcribing up to Superior Court also extends the enforceability to 20 years if you keep renewing in Superior Court (since law says if you do it within 5 years and then before 20 years, etc.) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). 
  - If the debtor’s name on the judgment differs from property records (e.g., nickname vs formal), you might need to clarify or file alias names.
  - **Caution:** Once you have a Superior Court lien, if the debtor still doesn’t pay, *execution on real property is done through Superior Court via writs of levari facias* (sheriff sale of property). That is typically for larger judgments. For small claims, creditors rarely force the sale of a home given costs and homestead rights (Delaware doesn’t have a broad homestead exemption statute for general judgments, aside from limited personal property – meaning a primary residence can technically be sold to satisfy a judgment, but it’s drastic and the sale process incurring sheriff fees might not be worth it for a small amount).
  
**Wage garnishment (attachment of wages):** 
Delaware permits judgment creditors to garnish a portion of a debtor’s wages (earned income from an employer). To do this in JP Court:
  - Wait until 30 days after judgment (the time allowed for voluntary payment or appeal). If no appeal or payment, you can file a **Request for Writ of Attachment (garnishment)**, which in JP Court is done by submitting **Civil Form No. 17** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=a%20Civil%20Form%20No,The%20plaintiff%20should)). On that form, you list the **defendant’s employer name and address** and the amount still due on the judgment (principal + interest + costs).
  - The JP Court will issue a **writ of attachment FIERI FACIAS** (Fi. Fa.) to the employer (the “garnishee”). This essentially orders the employer to withhold a portion of the debtor’s wages and send them to the court.
  - The employer upon receiving the writ must file an **Answer to Garnishment (Form 17A for wages)** within 20 days stating whether the person works there and if they have any existing garnishments ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=After%20the%20request%20for%20garnishment,are%20any%20reasons%20why%20the)). Delaware law only allows **one garnishment on a person’s wages at a time** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L77%20Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). If the debtor’s wages are **already being garnished** by another creditor, your garnishment will be returned *unsatisfied* – essentially, you get in line. The rule is one at a time, not multiple concurrent ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L77%20Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). If the existing one finishes or is satisfied, then yours can start.
  - The amount garnished from wages: Delaware law protects **85% of wages** from attachment ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%28a%29%20Eighty,exempt%20from%20mesne%20attachment%20process)), meaning you can only take up to **15% of the debtor’s “disposable earnings”** (after tax, etc.) per paycheck. This is more favorable to debtors than the federal law (which allows 25%). Specifically, 10 Del. C. § 4913 exempts 85% of wages from “mesne attachment or execution” ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%C2%A7%204913,of%20wages)). In practice, the employer will withhold 15% of the net wages (or the amount above 30 times the federal minimum wage per week, whichever is less, as per federal baseline – but Delaware’s 15% rule is stricter). So, a $500 weekly take-home yields max $75 garnished.
  - The employer will remit the withheld wages to the court periodically (often each pay period or once a month). JP Court will then forward the funds to you (sometimes minus a $1 processing fee).
  - If the employer fails to answer or comply, you can petition the court to hold the employer liable for the amount they should have withheld (the court can enter judgment against a non-compliant garnishee).
  - **Exceptions:** Some income is completely exempt by law – e.g., Social Security benefits, many pensions, disability payments, child support payments, etc., are not considered garnishable “wages.” Delaware specifically exempts **workers’ compensation benefits** and **unemployment compensation** by statutes (these cannot be attached by creditors). If the debtor is unemployed or self-employed, wage garnishment won’t apply. You’d use other methods.
  - Once your judgment plus interest is satisfied through garnishment, you must file a **Satisfaction of Judgment** with the court, and the employer will stop the withholding.
  - If debtor changes jobs, you’d have to get a new writ for the new employer. The court’s forms (Form 17) you’d have to refill with new employer info.

**Bank account garnishment (attachment of bank accounts or other property):** 
Delaware also allows garnishment of a debtor’s bank accounts or other personal property held by a third party. The procedure:
  - You file a similar **Writ of Attachment (Form 17)** but indicating the garnishee is a bank or entity holding funds ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L144%20person,the%20top%20of%20the%20Form)). For example, “Bank of America, acct in name of John Doe.” If you don’t know the account number, just list branch/address and name.
  - The writ is served on the bank. The bank must then “freeze” the debtor’s account up to the amount of the judgment, and respond with an **Answer to Garnishment (Form 17B for other than wages)** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=person,the%20top%20of%20the%20Form)) within 20 days stating what funds they have. 
  - Delaware **exemptions**: There are certain protections. For instance, under federal law, banks must automatically protect Social Security or VA benefits directly deposited in the last 2 months – they cannot turn those over; they must leave a certain amount of those benefits untouched by garnishment. Delaware law also exempts **85% of wages** even after deposit (some states once wages are in a bank, they lose exemption; Delaware tends to allow debtor to claim traceable wages as exempt to 85%). Also, Delaware exempt property list (10 Del. C. § 4902, § 4914 etc.) covers certain amounts of cash or bank deposits in lieu of exempt personal property – specifically, Delaware allows a **head of family** an exemption in personal property up to $500 (or $25 if not head of family – an old, low limit) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%C2%A7%204903,exemption)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=Every%20person%20residing%20in%20this,addition%20to%20the%20exemptions%20in)). However, Delaware has opted out of federal bankruptcy exemptions and created its own list for insolvency – which includes a wildcard of $25,000 of any property for bankruptcy ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=debtor%20domiciled%20in%20Delaware%20shall,aggregate%20fair%20market%20value%20of)), but that does not automatically apply to judgment executions outside of bankruptcy. So practically, outside wages and specific benefits, a creditor could grab funds on deposit. The debtor can file a claim of exemption if the seized funds come from an exempt source (like Social Security) or fit an exemption category (like “tools of trade” or certain life insurance proceeds).
  - If the bank’s answer indicates money is available (e.g., “Debtor has $2,000 in checking, no other holds”), JP Court will typically order that amount to be paid to the court (through the constable or directly from bank). The bank essentially sends the money to JP Court, which then disburses to the creditor, similarly to wages.
  - If multiple people are on the account (joint account with spouse, etc.), Delaware presumes each owner’s interest in a joint account equally, unless evidence shows it’s someone else’s money. A non-debtor joint owner can come to court to object and prove the funds belong solely to them to get that share released. 
  - Non-liquid assets: You can also attach things like debts owed to the defendant (if, say, a third party owes the defendant money, you can garnish that – not common but possible). You’d name that third party as garnishee, and the process is similar.
  - If the debtor has a safe deposit box, you could theoretically get a court order to open and seize contents (rare, but could be done through a constable levy rather than garnishment per se).

**Levy and execution on personal property (assets):** 
Another route is a **writ of execution (Fi. Fa.)** directed to the county sheriff or a constable to seize the debtor’s tangible property and sell it to satisfy the judgment.
  - In JP Court, after judgment you can request a **Writ of Execution (Levied)** by filing a **“Praecipe for Fi Fa”** or checking the execution option on Form 17 (JP distinguishes garnishments from levies, but often it’s a combined form).
  - A constable (or the county sheriff, depending on property type and value) will go out and attempt to locate leviable assets of the debtor. Leviable assets include vehicles, equipment, jewelry, electronics, etc. **Homestead and household goods exemptions:** Delaware law exempts certain personal property from execution up to a small value. Specifically, 10 Del. C. § 4902 lists personal goods like bedding, clothing, tools of trade, etc., generally up to $500 aggregate for a head of family (and $500 in tools of trade separately) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=,the%20following%20articles%20of%20personal)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=section%2C%20each%20person%20residing%20in,and%20%2450%20in%20Kent%20County)). This exemption list is antiquated ($500 is very low). But practically, constables often refrain from levying basic household necessities. They aim for things of clear value beyond minimal exemptions.
  - If they find a car free of liens, they can levy it (meaning legally seize it – often they might not physically take it immediately, but they tag it or serve papers indicating it’s levied, and the debtor is warned not to remove or sell it). Then the court would advertise and sell it at public auction. The proceeds pay the sheriff’s fees and then your judgment.
  - The debtor can **select** the property to exempt up to the allowed value ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%C2%A7%204903,exemption)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=designated%20and%20set%20apart%20by,or%20included%20in%20the%20schedule)). In practice, a debtor might claim certain items are under the $500 exemption – often, because most used household items have garage-sale value, a lot can be exempted. Only more valuable items (like a second car, or expensive electronics) might yield net money.
  - If the constable cannot find non-exempt property (“nulla bona” return), you may get nothing from a levy.
  - Execution via levy is less common for small claims because the cost/effort might outweigh the benefit, unless you know the debtor has something valuable and free of liens (like a paid-off vehicle, boat, etc.).
  - **Constable sale**: If items are levied, JP Court will arrange a sale (auction). Delaware law requires notice to debtor and public posting of sale. If you choose levy, be prepared for some upfront costs (constable may require a moving/storage fee, ads for auction – these costs can be added to judgment but you might have to front them). 
  - After sale, the constable pays your judgment from proceeds. If proceeds exceed the judgment, excess goes back to debtor. If it’s insufficient, you can continue collection efforts on the balance.
  - Some property can’t be seized: *Certain exempt categories include* most government benefits (already covered), professionally prescribed health aids, and recently, Delaware law bars execution on the primary residence via judgment under $50k (not 100% but in some contexts like mechanic’s liens under 25k cannot lead to forced sale of primary home). Typically, unsecured creditors don’t force sale of a debtor’s home on a small judgment – it’s not cost-effective and courts might find it unconscionable if other ample personalty and wages are available.

**Judgment debtor examination (discovery in aid of execution):** 
If you don’t know what assets the debtor has, you can ask the court to order the debtor to appear for a **“judgment debtor exam”** (also called an “order in the nature of interrogatories” or “supplementary proceedings”). Delaware’s JP Court rules don’t explicitly provide for debtor exams like Superior Court does, but *Common Pleas and Superior* allow post-judgment discovery (written interrogatories or oral exam under oath) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4.%20Stay%20of%20Execution%2023,of%20the%20Peace%20Court%20Debt)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4,must%20file%20a%20motion%20with)). In JP Court, you might have to transfer the judgment to CCP to utilize formal discovery. However, JP Court can issue a summons for the debtor to appear for a **capias ad satisfaciendum** hearing (civil hearing on ability to pay) if the debtor is not paying – essentially a debtor’s exam. They rarely do that unless the debtor is evading or possibly in contempt of a court order (like ignoring garnishment or refusing to turn over levied property). 
  - Another approach: If you’ve docketed the judgment in Superior Court, you can use Superior Court’s Rule 69, which allows you to conduct discovery – including sending the debtor interrogatories about assets, or noticing a deposition. In practice, forcing a deposition can be expensive and often not worth it for small amounts. But sending interrogatories or a request for production of documents (bank statements, pay stubs) via Superior Court might get info.
  - Delaware does not jail people for not paying civil debts (no debtor’s prison). However, if a debtor defies a court order to appear for an exam or to answer interrogatories, the court can issue a **civil contempt** and in rare cases a **capias** (arrest warrant) to compel attendance ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/Appeals/jpappeals.aspx#:~:text=4,must%20file%20a%20motion%20with)). This would be for willful non-compliance, not for simply not having money.

**Interest on judgment:** In Delaware, a judgment accrues **post-judgment interest at 5% over the Federal Reserve discount rate** (same as legal rate) unless the underlying contract had a lower rate ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title6/c023/#:~:text=this%20Code%2C%20any%20judgment%20entered,contract%20rate%2C%20whichever%20is%20less)). As of 2026, Fed discount ~0.75%, so interest ~5.75% per annum. The JP Court judgment form typically says “with interest from date of judgment.” If you transferred it to Superior Court, it accrues interest at the legal rate there (which is the same formula) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title6/c023/#:~:text=this%20Code%2C%20any%20judgment%20entered,contract%20rate%2C%20whichever%20is%20less)). So as you enforce, you can collect interest up to the date of payment. For example, if $1,000 judgment sits 1 year at 5.75%, you can claim ~$57.50 interest. The court will include interest in the total when issuing writs if you specify the amount through a certain date.

**Judgment lifespan and renewal:** A JP Court judgment is valid for **5 years** ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). If not fully collected in that time, you must renew (“revive”) it to keep it enforceable. Delaware uses a **Scire Facias** procedure to revive – essentially, file a motion or application (JP Civ. Form 15A) to revive within the last 90 days before expiration ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=judgment%20is%20revived%20by%20scire,with%20the%20%205%2C%20to)). The JP Court will re-open the case just to extend the judgment another 5 years. You must pay a fee ($20) for revival ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=judgment%20is%20revived%20by%20scire,with%20the%20%205%2C%20to)). You can do this repeatedly. However, note if you transcribed to Superior Court, you might instead use Superior Court’s 20-year allowances – typically, one would renew at 5-year intervals in Superior to maintain the lien. 
- If 5 years passes without revival, the judgment becomes dormant – JP Court will not issue new executions on it. You’d have to sue again on the judgment (a new action on judgment, which is allowed within a 3-year limit after dormancy, but that’s more complicated). So don’t let it lapse; renew if needed.

**Exempt assets (summary):** Under Delaware law, key exemptions a debtor can claim:
  - **85% of wages** (so only 15% reachable) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%C2%A7%204913,of%20wages)).
  - **$500 of personal property** (head of family) or $200 if not head, plus $75 of business tools in NCCo/Sussex ($50 in Kent – those low figures are antique; likely not enforced strictly by region now) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=section%2C%20each%20person%20residing%20in,and%20%2450%20in%20Kent%20County)) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20%2475,and%20%2450%20in%20Kent%20County)), plus sewing machines and pianos specifically if leased (weird old exemptions) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=match%20at%20L44%20person%20residing,piano%2C%20piano%20playing%20attachment%20or)).
  - **Bankruptcy-only wildcard $25k** (only in bankruptcy, not in normal judgment collections) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=in%20%C2%A7%E2%80%824915%20of%20this%20title%2C,aggregate%20fair%20market%20value%20of)).
  - **Retirement plans, life insurance, annuities** are exempt from execution (Delaware explicitly exempts those) ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=%C2%A7%204915,153%2C%20%C2%A7%205)).
  - **Social Security, SSI, VA benefits** are protected by federal law from garnishment (except for certain debts like child support or federal tax).
  - **Homestead (primary residence):** Delaware has no general homestead exemption outside bankruptcy. That means, theoretically, a judgment lien can attach to a primary home and force a sale. But practically, forcing sale of a home for a small claim is extreme and rarely attempted. Also, Delaware’s “head of family” $500 exemption could be applied to equity in the home, which is negligible. Most creditors just use lien to get paid on sale, not force sale.
  - **Vehicles:** Not explicitly exempt aside from falling under the $500 personal property aggregate. Typically, one car of moderate value might be considered part of that $500. In bankruptcy, Delaware grants a $15,000 motor vehicle exemption ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c049/sc01/index.html#:~:text=debtor%20domiciled%20in%20Delaware%20shall,aggregate%20fair%20market%20value%20of)), but again, that’s not automatically applicable outside bankruptcy. Outside bankruptcy, if a debtor’s only car is needed for work and modest in value, sometimes courts are lenient calling it necessary personal property. But legally, not protected beyond that $500 bucket. In practice, if the car is expensive or a second car, definitely attachable.

**Priority of liens**: If multiple creditors have judgments:
  - **Wage**: Only one wage garnishment at a time by law ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=Form%2034%29,to%20garnish%20the%20defendant%27s%20wages)). Others wait their turn by filing subsequent attachments which queue in order received.
  - **Bank accounts**: First come, first served – if Bank A gets a garnishment and cleans the account, a later one finds nothing. If two garnishments hit at same time (rare), the one served first has priority.
  - **Real estate**: Judgment liens take priority by filing date. A Superior Court judgment docketed earlier has priority over one docketed later on the same property.
  - **If debtor files bankruptcy**: Once a bankruptcy is filed, all collection must cease (automatic stay). Secured judgment liens may be voidable by the bankruptcy if they impair exemptions. Many small claim judgments get discharged in Chapter 7 – leaving you with no recourse (unless it was for fraud or intentional tort, etc., nondischargeable in some cases). So, if you catch wind the debtor is insolvent, sometimes pushing too hard triggers them to bankruptcy and you might recover less. It’s a strategic consideration.

**After full payment:** If the debtor pays the judgment (whether voluntarily or via these methods), the creditor must **file a Satisfaction of Judgment** with JP Court so the record is updated (and any lien can be cleared) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=entries%20of%20the%20judgment%20with,county%20in%20which%20the%20Prothonotary%27s)). If the judgment was transcribed to Superior, a satisfaction should also be filed there to release the lien. Delaware law requires you to mark judgment satisfied within 90 days of payment or risk a penalty (for Superior judgments).

**Costs of collection:** Fees for writs, constable commissions (a constable may take a 4-7% commission on money they collect via execution), storage or towing fees – these can often be added on top of the judgment under “costs of execution.” The debtor ultimately pays those if collection is successful. For instance, Delaware constable rules allow a percentage commission on amounts levied. 

**Bottom line:** Use the method that fits the debtor’s situation:
- If they have a steady job → wage attachment is usually the surest way (steady trickle of payments).
- If they have a bank account with funds → bank garnishment for a possibly quicker payout.
- If they have property (car, etc.) but no job or cash → levy and sale might be needed.
- Often, just obtaining the writ or starting garnishment will motivate a debtor to set up a payment plan or pay to avoid further hassle.
- Always respect exempt limits – taking e.g. essentially all of someone’s paycheck or their basic household goods is not allowed.

**Judgment Debtor (Defendant) considerations:** Debtors can file a **“Motion to Quash”** a garnishment or execution if an exemption applies or procedure wasn’t followed. For example, they can claim their wages are already being garnished by child support (which has priority over other garnishments) or that the funds in a bank were Social Security. They should raise those with the court promptly. The court may hold a hearing on exemptions. The debtor should bring proof (e.g., bank statements showing deposit source).

If a debtor is truly judgment-proof (no job, no assets, only exempt income), the creditor might eventually have to let it lie or consider settlement for less. Judgments accrue interest, so sometimes creditors wait in hope the debtor’s circumstances improve (new job, inheritance, etc.).

**Renewal (Scire Facias) details:** Use JP Civ Form 15A (Application to Revive) which the form instructions say to file and pay $20 within 5 years ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). That triggers a scire facias writ served on debtor to show cause why judgment shouldn’t be revived – usually they default (no reason not to) and court revives it. If debtor objects (e.g. claims already paid – that should have been recorded as satisfied; or claims judgment was void – unlikely successful unless jurisdiction issue), the court might hold a hearing.

**Self-help no, Court-help yes:** A creditor cannot harass or seize property without these legal writs – doing so would violate fair debt collection laws or constitute conversion. So always use the court mechanisms.

In summary, **to collect your JP judgment**:
1. Consider transcribing it to Superior for a lien on real property (especially if debtor owns real estate).
2. Choose one (or multiple sequentially) of execution methods: wage attachment, bank attachment, or property levy.
3. File the appropriate form with JP Court (and pay small execution fees).
4. Pursue until satisfied. If partial payments come in (like garnished wages), interest still accrues on the remaining balance, but the garnishment will slowly chip away at it.
5. Once done, mark satisfied.

Remember to **renew the judgment at 5 years** if not paid by then ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). But also note, interest stops accruing once the judgment is dormant (if you forget to renew, interest technically can’t accrue after dormancy). Renewing resumes the enforceability (some argue interest during dormancy doesn’t run, but because you can’t enforce then, usually interest is not counted in dormant period – better to renew on time to avoid that issue).

Ultimately, Delaware provides the necessary tools, but the onus is on the creditor to utilize them. Many judgments go uncollected because defendants have no reachable assets at the time – but they remain enforceable for years, so keep monitoring the debtor’s situation (asset checks, credit reports, etc.) and try again if circumstances change. A judgment can also show on the debtor’s credit report (especially if you transcribed it to a higher court or if they apply for loans the lien pops up), which pressures many to pay eventually.

## 13. Special rules for businesses

Small claims involve not just individuals but often businesses as parties. Delaware has some particular rules when a **business entity** (corporation, LLC, etc.) is involved in JP Court:

- **Representation by non-lawyer (Form 50):** In Delaware JP Court, a business **does not have to hire an attorney**; it can be represented by a non-lawyer officer or employee, but only if it files a **Certificate of Representation (JP Civ. Form No. 50)** ahead of time ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=or%20estate%2C%20must%20file%20the,A%20Form%2050%20may%20be)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=Complaint,to%20the%20parties%20if%20the)). Supreme Court Rule 57 permits this for JP Court and certain courts. So, a corporation, LLC, partnership, or similar can appoint e.g. an owner, partner, or a salaried officer/employee to handle the case. That person must be authorized via a board resolution or consent (the Form 50 essentially is that). The form has to be **notarized** and filed in the JP Court case *and* with the Chief Magistrate of the JP Court system ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=or%20estate%2C%20must%20file%20the,A%20Form%2050%20may%20be)). It also requires the entity to **register annually** and pay a small fee (currently $20/year) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=or%20estate%2C%20must%20file%20the,A%20Form%2050%20may%20be)). If a business fails to file this, a non-lawyer can’t speak for it in court – meaning the case could be dismissed or a default entered if no proper appearance. For example, if an LLC’s owner shows up without counsel and no Form 50, the judge could refuse to let them advocate, since technically they’re engaging in unauthorized practice of law without that certificate. Usually the court will point it out and might allow a brief continuance to get it filed. But it’s critical to do it early (preferably at or before filing if you’re the plaintiff, or with/shortly after answer if you’re the defendant). Note: The representative must be a principal of the company or a full-time employee – you cannot just pick a friend or someone. And one person can represent only one entity at a time unless they have multiple as job (like an officer of two related companies can, but a non-lawyer can’t do this commercially for many).
- **Attorney vs. Form 50:** Alternatively, a business **may hire an attorney** (admitted to Delaware Bar) to represent it, in which case Form 50 isn’t needed (the attorney files a notice of appearance). Many larger businesses or those repeating cases (like collection agencies, landlords) do use attorneys. But Delaware provides the Form 50 route to ease access for small businesses in small claims to avoid counsel costs.
- **Number of cases rule:** Some states (like CA) restrict how many small claims a business can file per year. Delaware has no specific limit on JP Court filings by a business. However, if a business is very litigious, JP Court might ensure all their Form 50 registrations are in order and that they’re not engaging in the unauthorized practice of law en masse. Delaware’s Supreme Court Rule 57 says an **artificial entity cannot be represented by an out-of-state lawyer** not admitted in DE – they must get DE counsel or use Form 50 lay representation. So a national company’s staff counsel who isn’t DE-licensed can’t appear unless they do Form 50 as an employee (and even then, if that in-house counsel is an attorney, weirdly they’d have to be admitted or disclaim acting as attorney – usually Form 50 reps are non-lawyer roles).
- **DBA and naming convention:** When suing or being sued, a business should be correctly named:
  - If it’s a corporation or LLC, use the exact registered name (e.g., “ABC Enterprises, LLC”). If a trade name is used, include it (“ABC Enterprises, LLC d/b/a Quick Cash Loans”).
  - If it’s a partnership or sole proprietorship, the individuals should be named. For example, “John Doe, d/b/a John’s Plumbing.” Delaware does not have a statewide DBA registration (except filings in Prothonotary for trade names by sole props), but even if a fictitious name is used in business, legally the party is the individual or entity behind it. So ensure the actual legal entity is named to enforce the judgment properly. 
  - If you sue just “Joe’s Garage” without listing the owner or corporate status, you risk a scenario where you get a judgment that’s hard to enforce if “Joe’s Garage” is simply a trade style. So proper naming is key. Likewise, if you’re a business plaintiff, make sure you sue in your exact name (especially if you’re an LLC or Inc., add the suffix).
- **Filing requirements for out-of-state businesses:** An out-of-state company (foreign corporation) can sue in Delaware courts even if not registered to do business in Delaware, as long as the suit is over a permitted transaction. *However*, Delaware law (like many states) says a foreign business operating in DE without registration cannot maintain a lawsuit in Delaware courts until it registers (8 Del. C. § 383 for corporations, etc.). This often applies to companies who should have a Delaware business license or registration but don’t. In practice, for a straightforward one-off transaction, the court may not enforce that requirement strictly, especially in JP Court. But a *pattern* of doing business in DE without registering – the defendant could move to dismiss on that ground (“lack of legal capacity to sue”). The business can then register and typically retroactively be allowed to maintain the suit. So if you’re an out-of-state business making multiple claims in DE, it may be wise to register with Delaware Division of Corporations and get any required business license, to avoid that defense. JP Court likely doesn’t check this proactively, but a savvy defendant might.
- **Multiple filings by collection agencies or debt buyers:** Delaware does not limit how often they can sue, unlike some state small-claims courts. But **assignments:** If a business (like a debt buyer) is suing on an assigned claim, Delaware’s JP Court will scrutinize they have proper assignment documents. It’s allowed (Just need to show chain of assignment). The assignee can use Form 50 to appear via an employee or hire counsel. 
- **Agents vs. employees:** Only a bona fide officer, partner, member, or employee can be the Form 50 representative. You cannot hire a non-lawyer collection agent to go to JP Court for you – that would be unauthorized practice of law. For example, a property management company employee can represent the landlord entity if properly authorized (since that person is effectively an employee/agent of the entity). But an outside collection agency cannot send its own employee under Form 50 for the original creditor – they are not an employee of the creditor. They’d have to be an assignee (i.e., they bought the debt and are now the actual party).
- **Cost recovery for businesses:** If a business hires an attorney and the contract or statute allows attorney’s fees, and they win, they can request those fees. E.g., a lease might say tenant pays landlord’s legal fees if default – JP Court can award reasonable fees (though in small claims, often limited). If a business uses a non-lawyer rep, obviously no attorney fee to recover. They can only recover statutory costs (filing fees, etc.). 
- **Fictitious name in caption:** Delaware’s JP Civil Rule says if defendant is sued under a trade name, the judgment should identify the real name behind it to enforce. If you only know the trade name, you might have to do a small amount of research (e.g., check city business license records or ask in discovery pre-trial). If you get a judgment vs. “Joe’s Garage” and find later it’s owned by Joe Smith, you might have to move to amend judgment to add “Joe Smith individually.” It’s easier to get it right initially.
- **Internal formality:** Businesses should pass a resolution or have an officer sign the Form 50 authorizing the rep. If a dispute arises about the rep’s authority, the court could void actions if you didn't properly authorize. So ensure appropriate company official signs the Cert. of Representation (which includes acknowledging you understand the limitations – e.g., you can’t do appeals in Supreme Court with non-lawyer, etc., and you’ll notify if rep changes).
- **Annual renewal Form 50:** If your case lasts more than a year (rare in JP) or you have multiple cases over time, renew the certificate each January (the form gets filed with Chief Magistrate and a copy in each case). If you fail to renew and your case comes for trial, technically your rep’s authority lapsed. In practice, the court might allow renewal on the spot. But better to keep current. The $20 fee is statewide, covers all JP courts for the year.
- **Proprietors vs. partnerships:** A sole proprietor is essentially an individual – they don’t need a Form 50 to represent themselves (that’s just themselves). They appear as the natural party. A general partnership could have a partner appear (which is fine, as a partner is essentially an owner with full authority; but strictly should still do a Form 50 to clarify representation if the partner is not the one named on caption, or if an employee of the partnership is appearing). The rule specifically lists partnerships as needing Form 50 for an employee or agent to appear. A partner might arguably not need one since they are like the entity itself, but safest to file anyway listing the partner or managing partner.

**Special limits on businesses as defendants:** There aren’t explicit ones in Delaware small claims, but note:
- **Service on businesses** must go to their registered agent or an officer (discussed in Service of Process section).
- **Default for businesses:** If a business defendant fails to answer, default judgments occur as with individuals. If a business had no Form 50 or attorney, they essentially gave no proper answer, and will be defaulted. 
- **Arbitration clauses:** Many business contracts have arbitration clauses forbidding court suits. JP Court will enforce those if raised. E.g., a consumer sues a business in JP but contract had arbitration, the business can file a motion to compel arbitration (virtually all arbitration clauses cover small claims unless explicitly carved out). Delaware courts favor arbitration if contract is valid. So businesses should timely invoke that if they prefer arbitration (some large companies may, though for small-dollar might not bother).
- **Counterclaims by businesses:** If a consumer sues a business, the business can counterclaim in small claims up to $25k. If their counterclaim would exceed $25k, they can either trim it or ask to remove entire case to a higher court (which JP Court could allow by transferring to CCP if fairness demands – not a usual scenario, but possible if the counterclaim dwarfs plaintiff’s claim). Often businesses will just assert up to the jurisdictional cap and consider suing separately for the rest or waiving the remainder.

**Summary:** Delaware’s small claims process is reasonably business-friendly by allowing non-lawyer representation (saving legal fees on routine collections or disputes). Just ensure to follow the formalities (Form 50, annual fees) and correctly identify your business in filings. If you operate under a fictitious name, consider registering it (so it’s searchable) and always sue/be sued in your proper legal name. No special limit on number of cases means a high-volume filer (like a property management company for many properties) can file many JP suits – but they better have that Form 50 for each entity they represent (some property managers file under the property owner’s name and have an authorization to appear for each owner via form 50 – each distinct owner entity needs its own certificate). 

**Out-of-state counsel note:** If a business did want to use an attorney not licensed in Delaware for JP Court – e.g., corporate in-house counsel from NY – Supreme Court Rule 57 does not allow pro hac vice at JP-level for civil cases; they either hire DE counsel or go the employee route via Form 50. So many bigger companies either use local DE attorneys or send a managerial employee to do the case (with help prepping from counsel behind scenes perhaps). 

Thus, Delaware preserves some informality and cost-saving for businesses in small claims, while requiring registration to prevent abuse.

## 14. County-level differences

Delaware is a small state with only three counties (New Castle, Kent, Sussex). The JP Court system is statewide and generally **uniform** in procedures, forms, and fees. However, there are a few minor **county-level or court-specific differences** worth noting:

- **Court Locations & Numbers:** JP Courts are numbered and assigned to regions. For example:
  - *New Castle County* (northern, most populous) has JP Court #13 in Wilmington (which handles a lot of civil cases), JP Court #12 in Newark, and others (JP Court #11 is 24-hr criminal court in NCCo, not civil). New Castle County’s civil filings mostly go to Court 13 (Wilmington) or Court 12 (Newark) depending on the location of parties or where contract signed. There’s also a JP Court #10 (Prices Corner) but civil might be consolidated at #13 now. **Venue** in New Castle: you can file at #13 or #12; the court will route it if needed.
  - *Kent County* (central, capital Dover) has JP Court #16 in Dover for civil filings, and possibly JP Court #8 (but #8 might be mostly criminal). Court #16 (Kent) is likely the main civil court.
  - *Sussex County* (southern, more rural/beach) has JP Court #17 in Georgetown for civil cases, and possibly JP Court #3 in Georgetown (24-hr) but #17 is civil-focused, and maybe JP Court #2 in Lewes for some civil. The Delaware Courts website indicates JP Court 17 is “Civil Court” for Sussex.
  
  These differences basically mean if you file in the wrong JP location, they might transfer it to the correct one within the county. It’s more of an administrative routing. For example, all New Castle County debt cases might be scheduled at Court 13 Wilmington (I know Court 13 is designated as the site for all landlord/tenant panel appeals and many civil).
  
- **Local court hours & scheduling:** The JP Courts in each county maintain different hours of operation and possibly different “court days” for trials. For instance, Court 13 in Wilmington might have civil trials on specific weekdays. In Sussex, Court 17 might cluster civil hearings on certain dates. This can affect how soon you get a trial date depending on county backlog. New Castle (more populous) may have a longer wait or more cases per day. Sussex might schedule quicker. E.g., a Sussex case might get a trial in 4-6 weeks, New Castle maybe 8-10 weeks – hypothetical, but possible differences.
  
- **Jury trial possibility:** Not applicable in JP Court – none in any county (only CCP appeals as discussed).
  
- **Filing methods differences:** All counties use eFlex for e-filing if desired, and paper filings similarly. There’s no evidence of separate filing fees by county – fees are state-standard. The only note: some older references said Kent and Sussex required attorneys to e-file starting earlier than New Castle, but now eFlex is statewide for JP.
  
- **Landlord/Tenant summary possession appeals:** This is a bit odd – if you appeal an eviction in Sussex or Kent, the 3-judge panel might sit in Dover or Georgetown. In New Castle, the panel sits in Wilmington. But that pertains to eviction appeals. For normal small claims, appeals go to CCP in the same county.
  
- **Constables and enforcement differences:** Each county has assigned constables or uses the county sheriff for certain writs. For instance:
  - In New Castle County, wage and bank garnishments are often handled via central processes, but a constable still serves them. For property levies, the *Sheriff of NCCo* might conduct sales if large (the law says sheriff handles Superior Court writs; JP writs are often executed by constables or forwarded to sheriff).
  - In Sussex and Kent, constables tend to handle JP executions fully. But if property is to be sold, it may involve the sheriff as auctioneer especially if real estate.
  Differences here mainly in who executes the writ; these differences do not significantly affect the creditor – aside from cost (each might have slightly varied fee schedules).
  
- **Variance in scheduling or mediation programs:** Some courts have on-site mediators for civil cases (especially in New Castle’s Court 13, which historically had a mediation program for debt cases). Kent and Sussex might not have formal mediation, but might allow negotiation in hallway. The **percentage of cases mediated vs. straight trial** could differ. For example, Court 13 may automatically refer some credit card cases to a volunteer mediator on trial day. Smaller courts might straight call the case before a judge.
  
- **Local forms or habits:** The forms are uniform statewide (provided by Justice of the Peace Court administrative office). You should not find differing versions of Form 1 or Form 50 by county – they’re the same. However, one county’s clerk might be more proactive in checking Form 50, whereas another might only ask for it at trial. For example, New Castle’s civil clerk might send a reminder if a corporate plaintiff hasn’t filed Form 50; a smaller county might not until it’s raised. So be mindful yourself.
  
- **Language access:** New Castle courts in Wilmington might have interpreters readily available for Spanish, etc., given population. In Sussex, with a sizable Spanish-speaking community, Court 17 likely also arranges interpreters frequently. Kent too. The availability is statewide but actual presence can vary by location. If you need an interpreter in a particular county, notify that court’s clerk early. They often share interpreter resources across counties.
  
- **Case volume/backlog:** New Castle’s JP Court likely handles the most cases, so your hearing might be scheduled further out and grouped with multiple cases per session (leading to waiting). Sussex and Kent are less populous, possibly quicker scheduling and shorter dockets on hearing day. For instance, a Court 17 docket might have 5 cases in a morning; Court 13 might have 15. This could mean in NCCo you get less time per case or more pressure to mediate to clear dockets, whereas in Sussex the judge might give more patient hearing. This is anecdotal but plausible.
  
- **Attorneys frequency:** In New Castle, more parties might lawyer up (being near Wilmington’s legal community). In Sussex, it’s possible more individuals appear pro se. This might change the dynamic – e.g., JP Court judges in Kent/Sussex might be more accustomed to guiding pro se litigants, whereas in New Castle they might see attorneys regularly on certain dockets (like collection agencies often have counsel in NCCo).
  
- **Appeals path location:** Appeals from JP in each county go to that county’s Court of Common Pleas (which sits in Wilmington for NCCo, Dover for Kent, Georgetown for Sussex). If you had an in-person trial in JP and then appeal, you’ll be traveling to the county seat’s CCP. Not really a rule difference, just geography.
  
Summing up: The JP Court operates under uniform rules in all counties. Differences are mostly logistical: where to file, how busy the court is, availability of mediation. So the process described in sections above applies equally whether your case is in Wilmington (NCCo) or Georgetown (Sussex). Always double-check the address and phone of the JP Court you need to deal with:
- NCCo Civil JP: likely Court 13 (37-41 Wilmington).
- Kent Co Civil JP: Court 16 (Dover).
- Sussex Co Civil JP: Court 17 (Georgetown).

One minor difference: **Local custom on continuances** – e.g., Sussex judges might be more lenient if farmers ask to reschedule around harvest, etc.; New Castle might be stricter about the number of continuances given the heavy docket. All subject to judge’s discretion but environment influences these things.

Finally, note Delaware’s small size sometimes blurs lines – an address in southern New Castle might be closer to a Kent JP Court. But officially, you file in county of venue guidelines, not whichever is nearest. The state being so small, though, means none of the differences are very profound – it’s one unified system under the Chief Magistrate.

## 15. Common pitfalls and dismissal reasons

Even in a relatively straightforward small claims process, parties often make mistakes that can lead to delays, dismissal of the case, or an unwinnable outcome. Here are **common pitfalls** in Delaware small claims (JP Court) and how to avoid them:

- **Suing the wrong defendant or name:** This is perhaps the #1 cause of problems. Example: You intend to sue “John Smith’s Plumbing, LLC” but you sue “John’s Plumbing” or just John Smith personally. If the contract was with the LLC, you need to sue the LLC (and serve the LLC’s agent). Suing the wrong name can result in:
  - **No judgment or an unenforceable judgment.** If you get a judgment against “John’s Plumbing” (a non-entity), you may not be able to collect because technically that’s not the legal person who owes you. The real business could ignore it, and you’d have to try to amend post-judgment (not guaranteed).
  - **Dismissal for lack of capacity:** The defendant might appear and say, “I, John Smith, didn’t contract with them – my LLC did, and they didn’t name it.” The court might dismiss without prejudice or allow an amendment. But if beyond SOL by then, you’re stuck. 
  **Solution:** Do your homework on whom to sue:
    - If it’s a business, check the Delaware Division of Corporations online database to get the exact legal name and entity type, and the registered agent for service.
    - If it’s an individual using a trade name (d/b/a), list the individual’s name followed by the trade name. Example: “John Smith d/b/a John’s Plumbing.”
    - If multiple parties are involved (e.g., two co-signers on a loan), sue both. If you only sue one and they’re only, say, 50% liable, you limited your recourse. Delaware is a joint-and-several liability state for contracts – you can sue one or all, but if you omit someone, you can’t later add them after SOL if needed.
    - If you’re not sure, it’s safer to list all potential responsible parties and let the judge sort out who (if any) is liable. For instance, if you aren’t sure if you contracted with John personally or John’s LLC (because perhaps the paperwork was unclear), you might sue both John Smith and John’s Plumbing, LLC in the alternative. The court can then dismiss the wrong one if needed. This costs a bit more (maybe extra service fee) but prevents a total miss.
    - Also, name designations: e.g., suing a married person – the spouse is not automatically liable unless signed or the claim is e.g., a family necessity. So don’t add someone just because they’re the spouse, unless legally relevant (like a joint account). Conversely, if the contract was with “Jane and Bob Doe,” make sure to name both. 
    - If there’s a guarantor or co-debtor, include them as a defendant.

- **Improper venue / jurisdiction:** While Delaware’s JP jurisdiction is broad, filing in the completely wrong court or state is a showstopper. Pitfalls:
  - **Suing in the wrong state:** e.g., a dispute that actually belongs in another state’s courts. If the defendant has no ties to Delaware and all events occurred elsewhere, the JP Court might dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction (though Delaware’s threshold is low if they were served here or did business here).
  - **Venue**: If you sue in the wrong county (say, you file in Sussex but defendant lives and contract signed in New Castle), the case might be transferred to New Castle JP. This is not fatal, but causes delay and extra hassle. 
  - Also, **subject matter**: If you inadvertently bring a case that is not allowed in JP (like a slander claim or request for equitable relief), the court will dismiss it (JP Court can’t hear defamation or order injunctive relief). E.g., if someone files a small claim asking “the defendant must come do specific performance of a contract,” JP Court will likely dismiss because they can only award money or possession in landlord cases. 
  **Solution:** Check that the **type of case is allowed** (see Section 2’s “What you cannot sue for”). And file in the correct Delaware county (where defendant or cause is).
  - Delaware’s statewide JP jurisdiction saves many from a venue dismissal because any JP can technically hear it, but still, the court may move it to a more convenient venue or the defendant can argue inconvenience (forum non conveniens).
  - For multi-state issues, consult whether Delaware has proper jurisdiction (e.g., if accident was in MD and defendant in MD, Delaware likely lacks jurisdiction unless defendant can be served here or has some connection).

- **Statute of limitations expired:** A very common reason plaintiffs lose is they file too late and the defendant asserts the statute of limitations (SOL).
