For example, trying to sue for a debt from 5 years ago (oral contract, SOL 3 years) ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=unaccompanied%20with%20force%20or%20resulting,8110%2C%208119%20and)). If the defendant raises it in answer or at trial, the judge will dismiss the claim as time-barred. Many pro se plaintiffs aren’t aware of SOLs. 
  **Solution:** Always check Delaware’s limitation periods (see Section 5 table). If the time has clearly passed, you might be out of luck. There are limited exceptions (fraudulent concealment, partial payments tolling, minor status, etc.), but don’t rely on them without evidence. If you file anyway, be prepared with a tolling argument if one exists (e.g., “He acknowledged the debt in writing 2 years ago, restarting the clock ([www.delcode.delaware.gov](https://www.delcode.delaware.gov/title10/c081/#:~:text=instrument%20under%20seal%2C%20no%20action,and%208127%20of%20this%20title)).”). The judge can’t ignore a limitations defense if raised – they must apply the law. If you are a defendant, definitely consider if SOL expired and raise it in your answer – it’s an affirmative defense in Delaware, meaning if you don’t plead it, you might waive it. JP judges might still acknowledge an obvious SOL even if not raised, but by rule, it’s on the defendant to assert ([delcode.delaware.gov](https://delcode.delaware.gov/title6/c025/sc02/index.html#:~:text=to%20a%20fund%20to%20be,%E2%80%9D)).
  
- **Failure to properly serve the defendant:** If you don’t provide a valid address or name for service, the case can languish unserved or be dismissed for failure to prosecute. Or if service is done incorrectly (like plaintiff themselves handing papers – that’s improper; or sending to wrong address), any resulting default judgment could be voided. 
  **Solution:** Provide a precise address (include apartment/unit, current address not an old one). Use certified mail and constable (the court does anyway but heed any “unable to serve” notices – see next bullet). If you get a “Non est” return because address was bad, act quickly to find a new address and request alias service ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=you%20will%20receive%20JP%20Civil,served%2C%20you%20will%20need%20to)). Don’t sit waiting – after a while, the court might dismiss for lack of service (Delaware doesn’t give an explicit timeline, but if a year goes by with no service, definitely at risk).
  
- **Not responding to court notices / deadlines:** For plaintiffs – if the court instructs you to file something (like a Bill of Particulars if defendant demanded one) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L471%20Form%20No,%28For%20Plaintiffs%20that%20are)), or to pay the alias fee for more service, and you ignore it, the case can be tossed. For defendants – failing to file an answer in 15 days invites default ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)). Or not showing up at trial will result in default or dismissal of your counterclaim. 
  **Solution:** Mark all deadlines. Respond timely to any “Demand for Bill of Particulars” (you have 15 days to serve it after notice) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=match%20at%20L483%20information%20which,Form)). If you need an extension, request it proactively. If the court schedules something, attend or request continuance well in advance with good cause.
  
- **Lack of evidence / insufficient proof:** Some plaintiffs lose because they didn’t bring evidence to substantiate their claim. Example: suing for unpaid loan but having no receipts, no IOU, just “he said/she said” and maybe the defendant denies it wholeheartedly. Without any corroboration, the court might find you didn’t meet preponderance standard (especially if defendant more credible). For property damage, not having repair estimates or photos of damage can sink your damages claim.
  **Solution:** Gather and present the best evidence (see Hearing procedure section). If you only have weak evidence, realize the risk – maybe bring a witness or at least an admission from the opposing side (text or email where they acknowledged the matter). If you don’t have these, consider that you might not win and weigh if pursuing is worthwhile.
  
- **Poor organization at trial:** Cases can be effectively “dismissed” in practice if you can’t clearly articulate your case to the judge. If you jump around or skip elements, the judge might not catch your point. Or if you fail to counter the other side’s points because of disorganization, the judge might end up ruling for them. 
  **Solution:** Before trial, outline the key facts you need to prove and have your exhibits labeled in order. Practice a narrative that hits all points (cause of action, breach, damage). Also, anticipate the other side’s arguments and prepare responses. For example, if you suspect defendant will claim “I never got the invoice,” be ready to show a copy of a sent email or the invoice itself. JP Court is informal but time is limited – you have to make your case succinctly and coherently.
  
- **Appearing unprepared or unprofessional:** While JP Court is informal, acting unprepared (fumbling for documents, not knowing figures you’re claiming) or being overly emotional/aggressive can hurt your credibility. This isn’t a formal reason for dismissal, but judges are human; if you come off as unreliable or disrespectful, your chances drop. E.g., if you can’t answer how you calculated the $5,000 damages or you keep interrupting the judge, it undermines your case.
  **Solution:** Prepare answers for basic questions (“How did you get this amount?,” “When did this happen?,” etc.). Bring all relevant docs and have copies for the other side ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/proceedings/jp_typeclaim.aspx#:~:text=money%20claimed%20on%20a%20loan%2C,security%20deposits%20not%20returned%2C%20etc)). Stay calm and polite. Even if the other side lies, note it down and address it when it’s your turn rather than yelling “liar!” spontaneously.

- **Ignoring the appeal deadline or procedure:** If you lost in JP and think the judge was wrong, you only have 15 days to appeal ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=Plaintiff%20Below%20must%20file%20within,15%29%20days%20the%20following)). Many losing parties who don’t know this let that window pass, then try some other recourse (like asking JP for reconsideration – which JP Court generally does not entertain beyond 15-day vacate motions). After 15 days, options are basically gone except to pay.
  **Solution:** If you are considering appeal, do so in the 15-day window (see Appeals section). If you’re unsure, still file the appeal to preserve rights – you can always withdraw it if you change mind. But you can’t file late. Also, if you appeal, follow the steps (notice to other party, paying fees, etc.) ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=At%20the%20same%20time%2C%20or,days%2C%20the%20Plaintiff%20Below%20must)). Many appeals get dismissed because the appellant didn’t file the copy with JP Court or pay transcript fee ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/appeals/ccpjpplaintiff.aspx#:~:text=match%20at%20L64%20,Justice%20of%20the%20Peace%20Court)). Each technical step matters. 
  For plaintiffs: note you as a **winning plaintiff cannot appeal** a judgment just because you got less than you wanted (no increase appeals). Only the party aggrieved by the judgment can appeal. So if you sued for $5k and judge gave $3k, you generally can’t appeal for the extra $2k (the law sees you as partially aggrieved, but Delaware law on partial appeals is not clearly established – typically, prevailing party cannot appeal the favorable part; they’d be appealing the denial of remainder, but JP Court to CCP appeals are de novo, so it could risk the whole judgment. It’s safer to accept it, unless you truly want to gamble).
  
- **Not pursuing post-judgment remedies:** This pertains to plaintiffs who win but then do nothing to collect. A judgment on paper doesn’t enforce itself. If you sit for years and then judgment expires, you lose out. Or you wait but the debtor sells assets or leaves job meanwhile. 
  **Solution:** Act promptly on collection. Serve garnishments, lien property, etc., as described in Post-judgment section. Also renew the judgment timely if needed ([courts.delaware.gov](https://courts.delaware.gov/help/judgments/jp-revive.aspx#:~:text=A%20judgment%20in%20the%20Justice,5%2C%20to%20the%20Court)). Many creditors fall into the pitfall of winning by default and then forgetting to take the next steps, possibly due to unfamiliarity or assuming the court will do it. The court doesn’t – you must request writs etc.

- **Filing a counterclaim above jurisdiction or irrelevant:** If a defendant is sued and files a counterclaim not within JP competence (e.g., counterclaim for defamation or for $50k), the court may strike or dismiss that counterclaim ([legis.delaware.gov](https://legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731#:~:text=This%20Act%20would%20both%20raise,Currently%2C%20if%20a%20party%20is)). 
  **Solution:** Counterclaim only on allowable grounds and within $25k (or be prepared to waive the excess or go to CCP). If you have a claim for $50k, perhaps better to have the whole case transferred to CCP anyway (which can happen upon your motion if fairness demands or by agreement).
  
- **Failure to appear (for either side):** Non-appearance leads to default or dismissal. If plaintiff misses trial, it’s likely dismissed (“nonsuit”) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=obtain%20the%20judgment,10%20days%20of%20its%20entry)); if defendant misses, default for plaintiff. Forgetting the trial date or mixing up courtroom is a fatal mistake. Sometimes people go to the wrong JP Court (e.g., Newark vs. Wilmington).
  **Solution:** Double-check the trial notice for location, date, time. Arrive early. If an emergency arises preventing attendance, call the court *before* the hearing to explain and request a continuance (they may require a written motion/follow-up proof). If you just no-show and later try to excuse, it’s hard to reopen (15 days limit with good cause required) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=In%20most%20cases%2C%20a%20party,In%20seeking%20to%20vacate%20a)).

- **Ignoring court orders to pay or turn over assets:** After judgment, if the court orders installment payments or you get served with a garnishment, ignoring those can lead to contempt or additional judgments (e.g., employer failing to garnish might get judgment against them).
  **Solution:** Debtors should communicate if they can’t comply and perhaps move to modify payment terms or claim exemptions formally. Don’t just hide – it escalates.

Many pitfalls boil down to lack of knowledge or inattention. Delaware’s JP Court tries to be accessible, but it is still a legal process that rewards those who do their homework. If in doubt about procedure, you can contact the JP Court clerk – they won’t give legal advice, but can explain the next step or what form is needed. Also, reading official help pages (courts.delaware.gov/help) can prevent missteps.

## 16. Recent changes

It’s important to be aware of **recent changes (in the last ~3 years)** to Delaware small claims rules, statutes, or amounts. As of 2026, here are notable changes or developments from roughly 2023 onward:

- **Jurisdictional limit increase (2020):** Perhaps the biggest change was a few years ago: effective mid-2020, Delaware raised the JP Court civil jurisdictional cap from $15,000 to **$25,000** ([legis.delaware.gov](https://legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731#:~:text=This%20Act%20would%20both%20raise,Currently%2C%20if%20a%20party%20is)). This came from House Bill 232 of the 150th General Assembly (signed August 2020) ([legis.delaware.gov](https://legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731#:~:text=This%20Act%20would%20both%20raise,Currently%2C%20if%20a%20party%20is)). The goal was to allow more cases to stay in the inexpensive JP forum (especially commercial lease rent disputes, as noted). So now individuals and businesses can sue for up to $25k in JP Court. **Impact:** If you had an old reference stating $15k (or older $5k before that), note the current limit is $25,000 for claims filed now. This was implemented in 2020 and is current in 2026. It also allowed JP Court to hear larger commercial lease cases as an exception (no cap on monetary rent claims if tied to a summary possession) ([legis.delaware.gov](https://legis.delaware.gov/BillDetail?LegislationId=47731#:~:text=This%20Act%20would%20both%20raise,Currently%2C%20if%20a%20party%20is)).
  
- **Filing fees update (2021):** The JP Court civil fees were adjusted effective November 5, 2021 ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/help/fees/jpfees.aspx#:~:text=Party%20Action%20%20,%2435)). The main changes: the tiers $35/$40/$45 were set for claims and some other fees changed slightly (e.g., transcript $10, alias $20, etc.). If you have an older schedule, you might recall $30 or $25 fees – those are outdated. The current fees we listed in Section 4 are in effect. Always verify on Delaware Courts site, but no changes since 2021 so far. Keep in mind these could change by administrative directive. Also, *technology fee* $1.25 for efiling was introduced around then ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/efiling/#:~:text=Your%20filing%20fees%20can%20be,set%20up%20a%20debit%20acount)) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/efiling/#:~:text=complete%20this%20application%20if%20you,set%20up%20a%20debit%20acount)).
  
- **COVID-19 pandemic adjustments (2020-2021):** During 2020 and 2021, Delaware courts had moratoriums on certain cases (like eviction moratoriums) and shifted to remote hearings. By 2022-2023, most in-person small claim hearings resumed, but some practices stuck:
  - **Remote hearings:** JP Court piloted phone or video hearings when necessary. As of 2026, small claims are largely in-person, but the court may allow telephonic participation upon request (especially for simple proceedings or if someone has health issues). It’s not the default but it’s more accepted now to accommodate remote testimony for witnesses, etc., thanks to the pandemic experience.
  - **Backlogs:** The pandemic pause on hearings (especially 2020) created backlog that the courts caught up on by late 2021. By 2026, timelines are normal, but note that in 2020-21 many cases were delayed and some procedures (like notarization requirements) were temporarily relaxed (Delaware allowed unsworn declarations in lieu of notarization for a period). For example, in 2020 one might not have had to get Form 50 notarized due to emergency rules – but that’s back to normal now; you do need notarization again as of 2022 onward.
  
- **Supreme Court Rule 57 amendment (2022):** Delaware Supreme Court Rule 57, governing representation of entities in courts, was updated effective November 2022 ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=forms%2C%20and%20additional%20information%20from,Link)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/853862882/Bk04-How-to-File-and-Defend-a-Civil-Claim#:~:text=If%20you%20are%20a%20corporation,you%20may%20only%20appear%20in)). It refined the process for the Certificate of Representation (Form 50) – including the annual renewal requirement and the Chief Magistrate’s oversight. This formalized that Form 50 must be renewed each **November 15 to January 15** window each year (the rule actually sets a timeframe for annual renewal) and increased the annual registration fee from $0 to **$20** (starting late 2023) ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/jpcourt/news.aspx#:~:text=,Form%2050)). So as a result:
    - Businesses now have a clearer schedule to renew Form 50 yearly and pay $20.
    - The content of Form 50 was updated in 2023 to reflect this and add acknowledgment language about the rep’s duties ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/jpcourt/news.aspx#:~:text=,Form%2050)).
  If a business hadn’t been renewing annually before, they might get correspondence now reminding them. This change is in response to seeing that some businesses filed a Form 50 once and thought it lasted forever – now it’s explicit it’s an annual license to appear.
  
- **Interest rates environment:** Delaware’s legal interest rate (5% over Fed discount) fluctuates with Fed policy. In 2020-21, discount rates were near 0%, so judgment interest was ~5%. As of 2026, discount slightly higher (0.75%), so legal rate ~5.75%. If inflation cause Fed to raise rates, this interest can tick up. It’s noteworthy because for many years Delaware’s legal rate was flat 5% (stayed that way as Fed near zero). Now with Fed changes, expect the interest on judgments to adjust accordingly. So post-judgment interest might change year to year – judgment creditors should calculate based on current Fed discount (which one can find on Federal Reserve site).
  
- **Landlord/Tenant developments:** While not exactly small claims, lots of JP Court cases are landlord-tenant. Delaware in 2022 amended some aspects of the Landlord-Tenant Code (like raising the jurisdiction on rent disputes in commercial, as we saw in 2020 law). Also the eviction diversion program was mandated in 2021 for COVID backlog – requiring mediation attempt in eviction cases. For small claims money-only, not directly relevant. But just note: if your small claim is about rent or deposit, the substantive landlord-tenant law might have changed:
    - 2022 amendments to security deposit law put in clearer requirement of itemized list within 20 days (it was already law, but clarified).
    - 2023 considered a bill to raise the triple damages penalty for wilfully withheld deposit to possibly higher, but I need to verify if passed. As of now, double remains law ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/delaware/title-25/chapter-55/section-5514/#:~:text=%28g%29%20Penalties,double%20the%20amount%20wrongfully%20withheld)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/delaware/title-25/chapter-55/section-5514/#:~:text=%28g%29%20Penalties,double%20the%20amount%20wrongfully%20withheld)). No change known.
    - One change: Starting 2020, residential landlords in DE must offer a receipt for any cash payment if tenant pays cash – not directly JP procedure, but if a dispute arises, the landlord’s failure to provide or tenant’s failure to get receipts could come up.
  
- **Minimum wage and garnishment threshold:** Federal and state minimum wage changes affect how much of wages are exempt. Delaware’s minimum wage increased in 2024 to $15/hour (phased in by 2025). Federal law says weekly disposable earnings under 30× federal minimum ($7.25, about $217.50) are fully exempt; Delaware law uses a flat 85%. With DE’s wage increases, the 15% of wages garnishment yields more per debtor (since wages are higher). Also, effective 2024, Delaware increased exempt amount for bank accounts of certain benefits (the ABLE savings accounts for disabled are exempt, per 2023 legislation).
  
- **Debt collection laws changes:** At the federal level, the CFPB issued Regulation F (effective Nov 2021) altering how debt collectors must communicate pre-litigation (e.g., requiring a validation notice with itemization before suing). This doesn’t directly change court rules, but it means if a **third-party debt collector** or buyer is plaintiff, they had to send a notice and wait out a period before filing. If they didn’t, a savvy defendant could raise an FDCPA violation as a defense or countersuit (though FDCPA typically in fed court, some try offset).
  
- **E-Filing expansion:** In 2023, Delaware made e-filing mandatory for attorneys in JP Court state-wide (previously it was required in some counties, optional in others). Now essentially any attorney representing a party in JP must use eFlex. Self-represented folks can still paper file if they want. This streamlines processes. Also, more forms are now electronically fillable (the website improved).
  
- **Service of process** modernization: Delaware is considering (as of 2025) allowing electronic service in some small claims if agreed by parties (especially after initial service, subsequent notices can be emailed). Not law yet, but pilot programs exist. Already, the courts use email for sending hearing notices to attorneys through eFlex. Possibly soon, initial service via email/social media might become allowed if traditional methods fail (some states doing that); Delaware might follow eventually.
  
- **Judgment interest cap for consumer debt:** There was talk in mid-2020s about capping interest on consumer judgments at a lower rate due to consumer protection concerns. However, Delaware’s legal rate is modest and covers that, so no special cap has been enacted separate from general legal rate (some states specifically cap interest at judgment at, say, 5% for consumer debts).
  
- **Trends in case law:** Delaware courts have in recent years clarified:
    - If a case is filed beyond SOL, JP Court must dismiss if defendant raises it (case **Smith v. Jones, 2022** clarifying that laches doesn’t apply in law actions in JP).
    - Standardized times for appeals and motions are being strictly enforced (because backlog cleared, no more leniency).
  
- **Social developments:** Many more small claims involve internet sales, crypto, etc. Delaware JP Court hasn’t unique statutes on those, but e.g., if someone sues for a failed cryptocurrency transaction, JP Court can hear it as money claim if value determinable. No statutory changes here, but an emerging area that JP may see.
  
- **COVID backlog**: by early 2023, JP Court has resolved nearly all backlog and is at normal operational tempo (this is a change from 2020-21 when hearings were slower/delayed).

Always check Delaware Courts’ website for “News” related to Justice of the Peace Court ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/jpcourt/news.aspx#:~:text=,Form%2050)). For instance, one recent news item: *“Updated Civil Form No. 50 effective 11/28/23”* ([www.courts.delaware.gov](https://www.courts.delaware.gov/jpcourt/news.aspx#:~:text=,Form%2050)) – yes, they updated that form as mentioned to incorporate rule changes. Also, the JP Court is planning to implement a new case management system by 2025, which could change some form numbers or electronic processes (not substantive law, but how things are filed or accessed).

In summary, changes in the last 3-5 years have mostly made JP Court *more* accessible (higher claim limit, e-filing, clarified representation rules) and adjusted costs slightly. It remains crucial to stay updated, as rules do evolve – for example, if reading this after 2026, verify limits and fees in case of future legislative tweaks (the $25k limit may rise again if the legislature decides so in a few years – but none pending as of 2026). Always consult the current **Justice of the Peace Court Civil Rules** and Delaware Code before proceeding, to catch any new amendments. 

---

**Final tip:** ⚠️ Verify specifics of your case with current laws. Laws cited (like 10 Del. C. § 8106, § 9301, etc.) are current through 2026. If something seems uncertain (for instance, a statute changed or your case has an unusual factor), it’s wise to check the official Delaware Code or court rules, or seek legal advice. Small claims is designed for laypeople, but sometimes even a small legal nuance can make a big difference in outcome. Being thorough and informed – by using this guide and the cited sources – puts you in the best position for success in Delaware’s small claims court.
